In a controlled experiment across six industry configurations (72 tool invocations using Qwen3-32B), unconstrained tool parameters produced a 43% hallucination rate for domain identifiers; ontology-grounded parameters reduced this to 0%. We validate the approach through a digital twin analytics platform demonstrating that a single codebase with domain-specific ontology configurations eliminates tool-call hallucination and achieves cross-domain configurability without application code changes.

AI.tldr: Paper proposes embedding manufacturing ontologies into the AI agent tool layer to eliminate hallucinated domain identifiers and semantic drift in LLM-based agents used in manufacturing.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents in manufacturing can speak the language of the domain but lack grounded, operational understanding of how equipment IDs, process parameters, failure codes, and regulations relate in a specific plant. This creates a semantic training gap: models learn vocabulary statistically, while plants define meaning via explicit ontologies and relational constraints. This gap leads to linguistically fluent but operationally wrong outputs, which become worse in multi-agent settings as semantic drift accumulates.
AI.method: The authors formalize the semantic training gap and its multi-agent manifestation, semantic drift. They design an architecture that encodes the manufacturing ontology as a typed relational configuration in the AI tool layer. Instead of retraining models, they enforce semantic constraints at runtime through a three-operation interface contract: resolve (map terms/IDs to ontology entities), contextualize (bind queries to the correct operational context), and annotate (attach semantic metadata). An AIOps orchestration layer enforces invariants over these operations. They evaluate the architecture using Qwen3-32B across six industrial configurations and 72 tool invocations, comparing unconstrained vs ontology-grounded tool parameters.
AI.result: In experiments, unconstrained tool parameters led to a 43% hallucination rate for domain identifiers in tool calls. When parameters were constrained by the ontology-based configuration, hallucinations of domain identifiers dropped to 0%. A digital twin analytics platform implementation showed that a single codebase, combined with different ontology configurations, could be reused across domains without changing the application logic, while avoiding tool-call hallucinations.
AI.conclusion: Grounding LLM-based manufacturing agents in an explicit, runtime-enforced ontology at the tool layer closes the semantic training gap responsible for operationally incorrect but linguistically fluent outputs. The proposed resolve-contextualize-annotate interface, governed by an AIOps orchestration layer, eliminates identifier hallucinations in tested scenarios and enables cross-domain configurability from a single codebase. This suggests that semantic correctness in industrial AI agents should be achieved via tool-layer ontology constraints rather than solely via model training or prompt engineering.

title: An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation
summary: Agent evaluation requires assessing complex multi-step behaviors involving tool use and intermediate reasoning, making it costly and expertise-intensive. A natural question arises: can frontier coding assistants reliably automate this evaluation process? Our study shows that simply prompting coding assistants is insufficient for this task. Without domain-specific evaluation knowledge, frontier coding assistants achieve only a 30% execution success rate and produce over-engineered evaluations averaging 12+ metrics per agent, indicating that strong coding ability does not automatically translate to reliable agent evaluation. We introduce EvalAgent, an AI assistant that automates the end-to-end agent evaluation pipeline. EvalAgent encodes evaluation domain expertise as evaluation skills (procedural instructions, reusable code and templates, and dynamically retrieved API documentation) that compose into a trace-based pipeline producing complete evaluation artifacts including metrics, executable code, and reports. To systematically assess generated evaluations, we introduce a meta-evaluation framework alongside AgentEvalBench, a benchmark comprising 20 agents, each paired with evaluation requirements and test scenarios. We further propose the Eval@1 metric to measure whether generated evaluation code both executes and yields meaningful results on the first run. Our experiments show that EvalAgent produces focused evaluations, improving Eval@1 from 17.5% to 65%, and achieving 79.5% human expert preference over baseline approaches. Further ablation studies show that evaluation skills are critical for handling complex evaluation: removing them causes Eval@1 to drop significantly from 65% to 30%.

AI.tldr: The paper presents EvalAgent, an AI assistant that automates end-to-end evaluation of AI agents and outperforms generic coding assistants on a new benchmark and meta-evaluation framework.
AI.motivation: Evaluating AI agents is difficult because it involves complex multi-step behavior, tool use, and intermediate reasoning. Manual evaluation is costly and requires domain expertise. A tempting idea is to use powerful coding assistants to automatically generate evaluation pipelines, but the authors find that simply prompting these assistants leads to low execution success, over-engineered metrics, and unreliable evaluations. This reveals a gap between coding capability and robust, domain-aware agent evaluation, motivating a specialized system and framework for agent evaluation automation and assessment.
AI.method: The authors first empirically test frontier coding assistants on agent evaluation tasks and analyze their failure modes (low execution success and overly complex metric design). They then design EvalAgent, which explicitly encodes evaluation domain expertise as modular "evaluation skills"—procedural instructions, reusable code and templates, and dynamically retrieved API documentation. These skills are composed into a trace-based pipeline that generates full evaluation artifacts: metrics definitions, executable evaluation code, and human-readable reports. To measure the quality of these generated evaluations, they introduce a meta-evaluation framework and construct AgentEvalBench, a benchmark of 20 diverse agents paired with evaluation requirements and test scenarios. They also propose the Eval@1 metric, capturing whether the evaluation code both runs successfully and yields meaningful outputs on its first execution. Finally, they perform controlled ablations to quantify the contribution of evaluation skills.
AI.result: Frontier coding assistants, when used without explicit evaluation expertise, achieve only about a 30% execution success rate and produce evaluations with more than 12 metrics on average, many of which are unnecessary or unfocused. In contrast, EvalAgent generates more targeted, streamlined evaluations. On AgentEvalBench and under the meta-evaluation framework, EvalAgent improves the Eval@1 metric from 17.5% for baselines to 65%. Human experts prefer EvalAgent’s evaluations over baselines 79.5% of the time. Ablation studies show that removing evaluation skills from EvalAgent drastically reduces performance, causing Eval@1 to fall from 65% down to approximately 30%, highlighting the importance of explicit evaluation domain knowledge.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that strong general-purpose coding ability alone is insufficient for reliable, automated evaluation of AI agents. Domain-specific evaluation expertise must be explicitly represented and orchestrated. EvalAgent operationalizes this idea via evaluation skills and a trace-based pipeline, substantially improving first-run executability and expert-rated quality of evaluation setups compared to generic coding assistants. The proposed AgentEvalBench and Eval@1 metric provide a systematic way to benchmark and meta-evaluate automated evaluation systems. Overall, the work demonstrates that encoding evaluation knowledge as reusable skills is critical for handling complex agent evaluation tasks and can significantly close the gap between automated and expert-designed evaluations.

title: Unlocking LLM Creativity in Science through Analogical Reasoning
summary: Autonomous science promises to augment scientific discovery, particularly in complex fields like biomedicine. However, this requires AI systems that can consistently generate novel and diverse solutions to open-ended problems. We evaluate LLMs on the task of open-ended solution generation and quantify their tendency to mode collapse into low-diversity generations. To mitigate this mode collapse, we introduce analogical reasoning (AR) as a new approach to solution generation. AR generates analogies to cross-domain problems based on shared relational structure, then uses those analogies to search for novel solutions. Compared to baselines, AR discovers significantly more diverse generations (improving solution diversity metrics by 90-173%), generates novel solutions over 50% of the time (compared to as little as 1.6% for baselines), and produces high-quality analogies. To validate the real-world feasibility of AR, we implement AR-generated solutions across four biomedical problems, yielding consistent quantitative gains. AR-generated approaches achieve a nearly 13-fold improvement on distributional metrics for perturbation effect prediction, outperform all baselines on AUPRC when predicting cell-cell communication, infer brain region interactions with a high Spearman correlation ($ρ$=0.729) to published methods, and establish state-of-the-art performance on 2 datasets for oligonucleotide property prediction. The novel and diverse solutions produced by AR can be used to augment the search space of existing solution generation methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an analogical reasoning (AR) framework to make large language models generate more novel and diverse solutions for open‑ended scientific problems, especially in biomedicine, and shows that AR substantially improves diversity, novelty, and real‑world performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Autonomous scientific discovery with AI requires systems that can propose many distinct, high‑quality, and genuinely new solutions to open‑ended problems. Existing large language models tend to mode collapse, repeatedly producing similar or overlapping answers instead of a broad solution space. This limits their usefulness for tasks like hypothesis generation and method design in complex domains such as biomedicine. The authors aim to both quantify this low‑diversity behavior and design a method that reliably overcomes it.
AI.method: The authors evaluate LLMs on an open‑ended solution generation task and measure diversity and novelty of outputs, documenting a tendency to mode collapse. They then introduce an Analogical Reasoning (AR) framework: (1) identify cross‑domain source problems that share relational structure with the target problem; (2) generate explicit analogies between those problems and the target; (3) use these analogies to guide the search for novel solutions, effectively transferring structural patterns from other domains. They compare AR to baseline solution‑generation strategies and also implement AR‑generated solutions in several concrete biomedical tasks to test practical impact.
AI.result: Compared with baselines, AR greatly increases solution diversity (90–173% improvement on diversity metrics) and produces novel solutions over half the time, versus as low as 1.6% for baselines. The generated analogies are judged to be high quality. In real biomedical applications, AR‑derived solutions yield consistent quantitative gains: ~13× improvement on distributional metrics for perturbation‑effect prediction; best AUPRC for predicting cell–cell communication; brain region interaction predictions with high Spearman correlation (ρ = 0.729) to established methods; and state‑of‑the‑art performance on two datasets for oligonucleotide property prediction.
AI.conclusion: Analogical Reasoning is an effective way to counteract mode collapse in LLM‑based solution generation, enabling more diverse, novel, and practically useful ideas for open‑ended scientific problems. In biomedicine, AR not only broadens the solution search space but also delivers state‑of‑the‑art or strongly competitive performance on multiple real tasks, suggesting that AR can be used as a general augmentation layer on top of existing solution‑generation methods for autonomous science.

title: Deep Reasoning in General Purpose Agents via Structured Meta-Cognition
summary: Humans intuitively solve complex problems by flexibly shifting among reasoning modes: they plan, execute, revise intermediate goals, resolve ambiguity through associative judgment, and apply formal procedures to well-specified subproblems. Current LLM agents lack this flexibility, as their scaffolds hard-code such reasoning decisions in advance. These scaffolds are effective when their prescribed structure matches the task, but brittle when solving the task requires adapting the structure of reasoning itself. We introduce Deep Reasoning -- an inference-time approach for constructing task-specific scaffolds through structured meta-reasoning. Deep Reasoning uses a formal language that represents meta-reasoning as executable decompositions over associative inference, formal computation, and recursive subproblem solving, enabling decomposition principles to be encoded as in-context examples that guide test-time scaffold construction. We instantiate this approach in a general-purpose agent (DOLORES) that distributes complex tasks across more controlled reasoning threads. We evaluate it against state-of-the-art scaffolding methods across four hard benchmarks: multi-hop reasoning, long-chain question answering, long-context aggregation, and deep research-style information seeking. DOLORES outperforms all evaluated scaffolds across three model sizes and two model families, improving over the strongest evaluated scaffold baseline by 24.8% on average. DOLORES distributes cognition across structured, lower-load reasoning threads, thereby reducing premature termination and hallucinations. This advantage can even bridge the scaling gap, with an 8B version surpassing all evaluated 32B baselines from the same family in more than half the settings. These results point toward future agentic systems that treat scaffolding as adaptive reasoning, constructing the structure each task requires just-in-time.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an inference-time framework, Deep Reasoning, that lets LLM agents dynamically build task-specific reasoning scaffolds, instantiated in an agent called DOLORES, which outperforms fixed scaffolding baselines on several complex reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agents rely on hard-coded, static scaffolds (like fixed chains-of-thought or tool-calling workflows) that assume in advance how reasoning should be structured. These work only when the predesigned scaffold matches the task, leading to brittleness on complex problems that require switching between different reasoning modes, revising plans, and handling ambiguity. The authors aim to give agents the human-like flexibility to adapt the reasoning structure itself at inference time.
AI.method: The authors introduce Deep Reasoning, which frames meta-reasoning as an executable decomposition language: a formal language where high-level control flow composes three capabilities—associative inference (LLM-style reasoning), formal computation (tools, code, or precise operations), and recursive subproblem solving. Decomposition principles are provided as in-context examples in this language, so at test time the model itself constructs a tailored scaffold for each task. They implement this in an agent named DOLORES, which uses the meta-reasoning language to spawn and coordinate multiple, lower-load reasoning threads that collaboratively solve complex tasks.
AI.result: Across four difficult benchmarks—multi-hop reasoning, long-chain QA, long-context aggregation, and deep research-style information seeking—DOLORES consistently outperforms state-of-the-art fixed scaffolding baselines. Averaged across three model sizes and two model families, it achieves a 24.8% improvement over the strongest scaffold baseline. Notably, an 8B-parameter DOLORES configuration surpasses all compared 32B baselines from the same family in more than half of the evaluated settings, suggesting that better scaffolding can partially substitute for model scaling.
AI.conclusion: Treating scaffolding as an adaptive meta-reasoning problem, rather than a static workflow design, yields more robust and powerful LLM agents. By distributing cognition across structured, low-load reasoning threads and flexibly composing associative and formal computation, Deep Reasoning (via DOLORES) reduces hallucinations and premature termination while significantly boosting performance on complex reasoning tasks. This points towards future agentic systems that construct just-in-time reasoning structures tailored to each task.

title: Template-as-Ontology: Configurable Synthetic Data Infrastructure for Cross-Domain Manufacturing AI Validation
summary: LLarge language model (LLM)-based AI agents deployed in manufacturing environments require populated, schema-correct data for validation, yet production MES data is proprietary, privacy-encumbered, and vendor-specific. This paper introduces the Template-as-Ontology principle: a single Python configuration module (700-770 lines, 45 validated exports) serves simultaneously as the specification for a time-stepped manufacturing simulator and as the runtime domain schema for AI analytics tools, producing alignment by construction rather than integration. We formally define the domain template as a typed relational configuration schema and prove that structural alignment between simulation and tool layers is guaranteed by single-source consumption. A five-layer pipeline--simulation, PostgreSQL, CDC/Iceberg lakehouse, star schema, and 12 parameterized AI tools--generates causally coherent, MES-shaped data spanning 66 entity types across four operational domains mapped to ISA-95/IEC 62264. We validate the architecture with six industry templates (aerospace, pharma, automotive, electronics, beverages, warehousing) running on identical framework code. Calibration experiments (60 runs, 10 seeds per template) confirm parametric controllability: observed KPIs fall within configured ranges across all templates. A controlled hallucination experiment (72 tool invocations, Qwen3-32B) demonstrates that ontology-constrained parameters eliminate tool-parameter fabrication (0% constrained vs. 43% unconstrained hallucination rate for the evaluated model, Fisher's exact test p < 10^-12); the 0% constrained rate is an architectural guarantee that holds for any model. The framework provides a reusable data layer for discrete manufacturing AI validation.

AI.tldr: They propose a Python-based “template-as-ontology” that simultaneously defines a manufacturing simulator and the schema used by AI tools, ensuring perfect structural alignment and generating realistic, MES-like data for LLM agent validation.
AI.motivation: LLM-based AI agents in manufacturing need realistic, schema-correct data for validation, but real MES data is proprietary, privacy-restricted, and heterogeneous across vendors, making it hard to share and standardize. The authors want a reusable, open framework that can stand in for real MES data while guaranteeing structural consistency between simulation and analytics tools.
AI.method: They define a domain template as a typed relational configuration schema implemented as a single Python configuration module. This template is consumed both by a time-stepped manufacturing simulator and by AI analytics tools, enforcing alignment “by construction.” They implement a five-layer data pipeline (simulation → PostgreSQL → CDC/Iceberg lakehouse → star schema → AI tools), map 66 entity types to ISA-95/IEC 62264, and instantiate six industry-specific templates using the same codebase. They run calibration experiments over multiple seeds and templates to test KPI controllability, and perform a controlled hallucination experiment where an LLM (Qwen3-32B) uses tools whose parameters are either constrained or unconstrained by the ontology, then compare hallucination rates statistically (Fisher’s exact test).
AI.result: The system produces MES-shaped, causally coherent data across 66 entity types and four operational domains, with six industry templates all running on the same framework. Calibration runs (60 runs, 10 seeds per template) show KPIs stay within configured parameter ranges, demonstrating controllability. In the hallucination study with 72 tool calls, ontology-constrained tools eliminate parameter fabrication entirely (0% hallucination vs. 43% when unconstrained), with a highly significant Fisher’s exact test (p < 10^-12).
AI.conclusion: A single-source, template-as-ontology configuration can guarantee structural alignment between manufacturing simulators and AI analytics tools, yielding a reusable data and schema layer for validating LLM-based agents in discrete manufacturing. Ontology-constrained tool design can architecturally eliminate a major class of LLM hallucinations in tool parameters, independent of the underlying model, making the approach broadly applicable to AI validation in industrial contexts.

title: Freeze Deep, Train Shallow: Interpretable Layer Allocation for Continued Pre-Training
summary: Selective layer-wise updates are essential for low-cost continued pre-training of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet determining which layers to freeze or train remains an empirical black-box problem due to the lack of interpretable guidance. To address this issue, we propose LayerTracer, an architecture-agnostic diagnostic framework that reveals the evolution patterns of layer-wise representations and stability by locating task execution positions and quantifying layer sensitivity. Analysis results reveal that deep layers act as critical regions for task execution and maintain high stability against disruptive updates. Guided by this finding, we conduct three controlled continued pre-training trials to compare diverse freeze-train strategies, demonstrating that training shallow layers while freezing deep layers consistently outperforms full-parameter fine-tuning and the opposite allocation on both C-Eval and CMMLU benchmarks. We further present a hybrid model case study, which validates that placing high-quality pre-trained modules in deep layers effectively preserves inherent knowledge of the model. This work delivers a low-cost and interpretable solution for resource-constrained teams, offering actionable guidance for layer-wise parameter allocation in continued pre-training and hybrid model construction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LayerTracer, a diagnostic framework that provides interpretable guidance for deciding which layers of an LLM to train or freeze during continued pre-training, leading to lower cost and better performance than full fine-tuning by training shallow layers and freezing deep ones.
AI.motivation: Continued pre-training of LLMs is computationally expensive if all parameters are updated, so practitioners often freeze some layers. However, deciding which layers to freeze or train is currently empirical and lacks interpretability, creating a black-box process that can be suboptimal and waste resources. The authors want a principled, architecture-agnostic way to understand layer roles and sensitivities in order to guide low-cost, effective layer-wise parameter allocation.
AI.method: They propose LayerTracer, a diagnostic framework that: (1) identifies where in the network task execution mainly occurs, and (2) quantifies the sensitivity and stability of each layer’s representations to disruptive updates during continued pre-training. Using this analysis, they study the evolution patterns of layer-wise representations and determine which layers are critical and which are more adaptable, and then design controlled continued pre-training experiments that apply different freeze-train strategies based on these insights.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that deep layers are the main regions for task execution and are highly stable under disruptive updates, whereas shallow layers are more amenable to training. Guided by these insights, experiments show that strategies which train shallow layers while freezing deep layers consistently outperform both full-parameter fine-tuning and the opposite strategy (training deep, freezing shallow) on C-Eval and CMMLU benchmarks. A hybrid model case study further shows that placing high-quality pre-trained modules in deep layers preserves the model’s inherent knowledge effectively.
AI.conclusion: LayerTracer provides an interpretable, architecture-agnostic way to understand and exploit the layer-wise behavior of LLMs during continued pre-training. By revealing that deep layers are both critical for task execution and stable, the work supports a practical strategy: train shallow layers and freeze deep ones, or place the best pre-trained components in deep layers. This yields lower-cost, more effective continued pre-training and offers actionable guidance for resource-constrained teams building or extending LLMs and constructing hybrid models.

title: LatentRouter: Can We Choose the Right Multimodal Model Before Seeing Its Answer?
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have heterogeneous strengths across OCR, chart understanding, spatial reasoning, visual question answering, cost, and latency. Effective MLLM routing therefore requires more than estimating query difficulty: a router must match the multimodal requirements of the current image-question input with the capabilities of each candidate model. We propose LatentRouter, a router that formulates MLLM routing as counterfactual multimodal utility prediction. Given an image-question query, LatentRouter extracts learned multimodal routing capsules, represents each candidate MLLM with a model capability token, and performs latent communication between these states to estimate how each model would perform if selected. A distributional outcome head predicts model-specific counterfactual quality, while a bounded capsule correction refines close decisions without allowing residual signals to dominate the prediction. The resulting utility-based policy supports performance-oriented and performance-cost routing, and handles changing candidate pools through shared per-model scoring with availability masking. Experiments on MMR-Bench and VL-RouterBench show that LatentRouter outperforms fixed-model, feature-level, and learned-router baselines. Additional analyses show that the gains are strongest on multimodal task groups where model choice depends on visual, layout-sensitive, or reasoning-oriented requirements, and that latent communication is the main contributor to the improvement. The code is available at: https://github.com/LabRAI/LatentRouter.

AI.tldr: LatentRouter is a routing method that selects the best multimodal large language model (MLLM) per query by predicting each model’s counterfactual performance via latent communication between query and model representations.
AI.motivation: Different MLLMs excel at different tasks (OCR, charts, spatial reasoning, etc.), and they also differ in latency and cost. Existing routing approaches often rely on estimating query difficulty or superficial features, which is insufficient for multimodal tasks where success depends on detailed visual, layout, and reasoning requirements. There is a need for a router that can explicitly model the match between each query’s multimodal requirements and each candidate model’s capabilities, and flexibly support both performance-only and performance–cost trade-offs while handling dynamic model pools.
AI.method: LatentRouter represents the input image-question pair as multimodal routing capsules and each candidate MLLM as a learnable model capability token. It enables latent communication between the query capsules and each model token to simulate how each model would behave on the query. A distributional outcome head then predicts a counterfactual quality score for each model, capturing uncertainty. To avoid over-reliance on residual corrections in close decisions, a bounded capsule correction module refines predictions while constraining its influence. The router outputs a utility-based policy that can prioritize accuracy, cost, or a combination, and supports dynamic candidate sets via shared per-model scoring with availability masking.
AI.result: On MMR-Bench and VL-RouterBench benchmarks, LatentRouter achieves better routing performance than using a single fixed model, handcrafted feature-based routing, and other learned routers. The improvements are particularly strong on multimodal task subsets where picking the right model depends heavily on visual, layout-sensitive, or reasoning-heavy aspects of the query. Ablation analyses indicate that the latent communication mechanism between query capsules and model capability tokens is the primary source of the performance gains.
AI.conclusion: LatentRouter effectively formulates multimodal model routing as counterfactual utility prediction by modeling fine-grained interactions between query requirements and model capabilities. This leads to superior model selection, especially in complex multimodal scenarios, and supports flexible performance–cost trade-offs and dynamic candidate pools. The study highlights latent communication as a key architectural ingredient for robust multimodal routing and provides an open-source implementation for further research and application.

title: Agent-BRACE: Decoupling Beliefs from Actions in Long-Horizon Tasks via Verbalized State Uncertainty
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed on long-horizon tasks in partially observable environments, where they must act while inferring and tracking a complex environment state over many steps. This leads to two challenges: partial observability requires maintaining uncertainty over unobserved world attributes, and long interaction history causes context to grow without bound, diluting task-relevant information. A principled solution to both challenges is a belief state: a posterior distribution over environment states given past observations and actions, which compactly encodes history for decision making regardless of episode length. In LLM agents, however, the open-ended nature of text makes it unclear how to represent such a distribution. Therefore, we introduce Agent-BRACE: Agent Belief state Representation via Abstraction and Confidence Estimation, a method that decouples an LLM agent into a belief state model and a policy model, jointly optimized via reinforcement learning. The belief state model produces a structured approximation of the belief distribution: a set of atomic natural language claims about the environment, each annotated with an ordinal verbalized certainty label ranging from certain to unknown. The policy model conditions on this compact, structured approximate belief rather than the full history, learning to select actions under explicit uncertainty. Across long-horizon, partially observable embodied language environments, Agent-BRACE achieves an average absolute improvement of +14.5% (Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct) and +5.3% (Qwen3-4B-Instruct), outperforming strong RL baselines while maintaining a near-constant context window independent of episode length. Further analysis shows that the learned belief becomes increasingly calibrated over the course of an episode as evidence accumulates.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Agent-BRACE, a framework that separates an LLM agent into a belief state model and a policy model, using structured, uncertainty-labeled natural language claims as a compact belief state for long-horizon, partially observable tasks, and shows significant performance gains while keeping context size nearly constant.
AI.motivation: LLMs are now used as agents in environments where they must act over many steps with only partial observability. This creates two core problems: (1) they must track uncertainty over hidden aspects of the world (true belief maintenance), and (2) the interaction history grows without bound, making it hard to focus on task-relevant information within limited context windows. Existing approaches often just stuff more history into the prompt or use ad-hoc memory, which is not a principled belief representation. The authors are motivated to bring the notion of belief states from POMDPs into LLM agents in a way that is compatible with natural language and scalable to long horizons.
AI.method: The authors propose Agent-BRACE (Agent Belief state Representation via Abstraction and Confidence Estimation). They architect the agent as two coupled components trained with reinforcement learning: (1) a belief state model that maps the full history of actions and observations into a structured belief representation; and (2) a policy model that conditions only on this belief representation to select actions. The belief representation is an approximate posterior over environment states expressed as a set of atomic natural language claims about the environment, each annotated with an ordinal certainty label (e.g., certain, likely, uncertain, unknown). This converts the open-ended belief distribution into a compact, discrete summary. The belief and policy models are jointly optimized so that the belief becomes useful for decision-making and calibrated under accumulating evidence. The approach is tested on long-horizon, partially observable embodied language environments using Qwen LLM backbones.
AI.result: Agent-BRACE significantly improves performance over strong RL baselines in the evaluated settings. With Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, it yields an average absolute performance gain of +14.5%, and with Qwen3-4B-Instruct, a gain of +5.3% across long-horizon, partially observable embodied language tasks. These improvements are obtained while the agent operates with a near-constant context window size that does not grow with episode length, thanks to the compact belief representation. Additional empirical analyses indicate that the model’s belief states become more calibrated over time within an episode, meaning that certainty labels better match the actual likelihood of correctness as more observations are collected.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that explicitly modeling a structured belief state for LLM agents—via natural language claims plus verbalized certainty labels—and decoupling this from the policy is an effective and scalable way to handle partial observability and long horizons. Agent-BRACE demonstrates that such approximate belief distributions can be learned and exploited using RL, improving performance over baselines while controlling context length. The authors also highlight that belief calibration improves as evidence accumulates, suggesting that belief-state-centric architectures are a promising direction for building more robust, uncertainty-aware LLM agents in complex environments.

title: Constraint-Data-Value-Maximization: Utilizing Data Attribution for Effective Data Pruning in Low-Data Environments
summary: Attributing model behavior to training data is an evolving research field. A common benchmark is data removal, which involves eliminating data instances with either low or high values, then assessing a model's performance trained on the modified dataset. Many existing studies leverage Shapley-based data values for this task. In this paper, we demonstrate that these data values are not optimally suited for pruning low-value data when only a limited amount of data remains. To address this limitation, we introduce the Constraint-Data-Value-Maximization (CDVM) approach, which effectively utilizes data attributions for pruning in low-data scenarios. By casting pruning as a constrained optimization that both maximizes total influence and penalizes excessive per-test contributions, CDVM delivers robust performance when only a small fraction of the data is retained. On the OpenDataVal benchmark, CDVM shows strong performance and competitive runtime.

AI.tldr: They propose a new data-pruning method, CDVM, that uses constrained optimization on data attributions to better select which training examples to keep in low-data regimes, outperforming Shapley-based approaches on OpenDataVal.
AI.motivation: Existing work uses data values (often Shapley-based) to decide which training examples to remove and evaluates by retraining models on pruned datasets. However, these Shapley-style values are not well suited for scenarios where only a small subset of data can be retained, leading to suboptimal pruning and degraded performance.
AI.method: They formulate data pruning as a constrained optimization problem that maximizes the total influence of the retained data while adding a penalty for overly large per-test-instance contributions. Based on this formulation, they define the Constraint-Data-Value-Maximization (CDVM) procedure, which uses data attributions but modifies the selection criterion to avoid over-relying on a few highly influential points, targeting robustness in low-data settings.
AI.result: On the OpenDataVal benchmark, their CDVM method achieves strong empirical performance compared to prior Shapley-based pruning approaches, especially when only a small fraction of data is kept, while maintaining competitive runtime efficiency.
AI.conclusion: By re-framing data pruning as a constrained optimization over data attributions, CDVM better matches the objective of robust learning in low-data regimes than traditional Shapley-based data values. This yields improved benchmark results and shows that data attribution methods should explicitly account for constraints like limited data budgets and balanced influence across test examples.

title: StoicLLM: Preference Optimization for Philosophical Alignment in Small Language Models
summary: While large language models excel at factual adaptation, their ability to internalize nuanced philosophical frameworks under severe data constraints remains underexplored. We investigate this by specializing small LLMs on micro-datasets of foundational Stoic texts using preference optimization (ORPO, AlphaPO). Evaluated via a multi-model critic bank, our results show that just 300 high-fidelity examples can induce strong alignment with inward-facing Stoic virtues, closely approaching few-shot prompting while freeing the context window. Critically, however, all models, including few-shot baselines, exhibit a persistent failure on Stoicism's outward-facing cosmopolitan duties, pointing to a representational limitation of small models that micro-dataset adaptation alone cannot overcome.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well small language models can internalize Stoic philosophy from very small, high-quality datasets using preference optimization methods.
AI.motivation: To understand whether small LLMs can deeply internalize complex, nuanced philosophical frameworks (specifically Stoicism) when trained on extremely limited but high-fidelity data, beyond standard factual tuning.
AI.method: Specialize small LLMs on micro-datasets (~300 examples) derived from foundational Stoic texts using preference optimization algorithms (ORPO, AlphaPO). Evaluate alignment using a multi-model critic bank and compare against few-shot prompting baselines that use Stoic examples only in context.
AI.result: With only 300 curated examples, small LLMs show strong alignment with inward-facing Stoic virtues (e.g., self-discipline, emotional regulation) and reach performance close to few-shot prompting, while no longer needing context window space for demonstrations. However, all adapted models and few-shot baselines consistently fail to capture outward-facing Stoic cosmopolitan duties (duties to others and the broader community).
AI.conclusion: Micro-dataset preference optimization can effectively instill certain internal, virtue-related aspects of Stoic philosophy in small LLMs, but it does not solve deeper representational limitations: small models systematically fail on Stoicism’s outward, cosmopolitan obligations, suggesting that some ethical dimensions may require larger capacity, richer training signals, or different architectures.

title: Rethinking Evaluation for LLM Hallucination Detection: A Desiderata, A New RAG-based Benchmark, New Insights
summary: Hallucination, broadly referring to unfaithful, fabricated, or inconsistent content generated by LLMs, has wide-ranging implications. Therefore, a large body of effort has been devoted to detecting LLM hallucinations, as well as designing benchmark datasets for evaluating these detectors. In this work, we first establish a desiderata of properties for hallucination detection benchmarks (HDBs) to exhibit for effective evaluation. A critical look at existing HDBs through the lens of our desiderata reveals that none of them exhibits all the properties. We identify two largest gaps: (1) RAG-based grounded benchmarks with long context are severely lacking (partly because length impedes human annotation); and (2) Existing benchmarks do not make available realistic label noise for stress-testing detectors although real-world use-cases often grapple with label noise due to human or automated/weak annotation. To close these gaps, we build and open-source a new RAG-based HDB called T RIVIA+ that underwent a rigorous human annotation process. Notably, our benchmark exhibits all desirable properties including (1) T RIVIA+ contains samples with the longest context in the literature; and (2) we design and share four sets of noisy labels with different, both sample-dependent and sampleindependent, noise schemes. Finally, we perform experiments on RAG-based HDBs, including our T RIVIA+, using popular SOTA detectors that reveal new insights: (i) ample room remains for current detectors to reach the performance ceiling on RAG-based HDBs, (ii) the basic LLM-as-a-Judge baseline performs competitively, and (iii) label noise hinders detection performance. We expect that our findings, along with our proposed benchmark 1 , will motivate and foster needed research on hallucination detection for RAG-based tasks.

AI.tldr: They propose and release TRIVIA+, a new RAG-based hallucination detection benchmark with long contexts and controllable noisy labels, and show that current detectors still struggle, especially under label noise.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations from large language models, especially in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) settings, are harmful but hard to evaluate. Existing hallucination detection benchmarks miss key properties: almost no grounded RAG benchmarks with very long context windows, and no benchmarks that systematically expose detectors to realistic label noise that occurs in practice. The authors want a benchmark that better reflects real-world RAG use cases and supports rigorous stress-testing of detectors.
AI.method:
1) Define a set of desiderata (required properties) for strong hallucination detection benchmarks.
2) Audit existing benchmarks against these desiderata and identify missing aspects.
3) Construct TRIVIA+, a new RAG-based benchmark: use long context documents, retrieval-augmented questions, and careful human annotation to label hallucinations. 4) Generate four controlled noisy-label variants using different noise schemes (both sample-dependent and sample-independent) to mimic realistic annotation errors. 5) Evaluate several state-of-the-art hallucination detectors, including a simple LLM-as-a-judge baseline, on both clean and noisy versions of RAG benchmarks (including TRIVIA+).
AI.result: TRIVIA+ is shown to satisfy all the proposed desiderata for hallucination detection benchmarks, and it provides the longest-context RAG-based samples in the literature. The authors also successfully create and release four noisy-label sets capturing various noise patterns. Experimental results show: (i) current hallucination detectors are far from saturating performance on RAG-based benchmarks, (ii) an LLM-as-a-judge baseline is surprisingly competitive with more elaborate detectors, and (iii) even moderate label noise significantly degrades detector performance.
AI.conclusion: The work fills important gaps in hallucination detection evaluation by providing TRIVIA+, a long-context RAG-based benchmark with configurable noisy labels that meets a comprehensive desiderata list. The empirical study indicates that detecting hallucinations in realistic RAG scenarios remains an unsolved problem and that robustness to label noise is a key challenge. The authors expect TRIVIA+ and their analysis to catalyze further research on better detectors and more noise-robust evaluation methodologies.

title: Robust Biomedical Publication Type and Study Design Classification with Knowledge-Guided Perturbations
summary: Accurately and consistently indexing biomedical literature by publication type and study design is essential for supporting evidence synthesis and knowledge discovery. Prior work on automated publication type and study design indexing has primarily focused on expanding label coverage, enriching feature representations, and improving in-domain accuracy, with evaluation typically conducted on data drawn from the same distribution as training. Although pretrained biomedical language models achieve strong performance under these settings, models optimized for in-domain accuracy may rely on superficial lexical or dataset-specific cues, resulting in reduced robustness under distributional shift. In this study, we introduce an evaluation framework based on controlled semantic perturbations to assess the robustness of a publication type classifier and investigate robustness-oriented training strategies that combine entity masking and domain-adversarial training to mitigate reliance on spurious topical correlations. Our results show that the commonly observed trade-off between robustness and in-domain accuracy can be mitigated when robustness objectives are designed to selectively suppress non-task-defining features while preserving salient methodological signals. We find that these improvements arise from two complementary mechanisms: (1) increased reliance on explicit methodological cues when such cues are present in the input, and (2) reduced reliance on spurious domain-specific topical features. These findings highlight the importance of feature-level robustness analysis for publication type and study design classification and suggest that refining masking and adversarial objectives to more selectively suppress topical information may further improve robustness. Data, code, and models are available at: https://github.com/ScienceNLP-Lab/MultiTagger-v2/tree/main/ICHI

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates and improves the robustness of automated classifiers for biomedical publication type and study design using controlled semantic perturbations and robustness-oriented training.
AI.motivation: Automated indexing of biomedical literature by publication type and study design is vital for evidence synthesis and knowledge discovery, but current systems, though accurate in-domain, often depend on superficial or dataset-specific cues. This leads to performance degradation when the data distribution shifts, undermining reliability in real-world applications where topics, phrasing, and domains vary.
AI.method: The authors design an evaluation framework based on controlled semantic perturbations of input texts to systematically test how a publication type classifier behaves under distributional shifts. They then propose robustness-oriented training strategies combining entity masking (to hide topic-specific entities and terms) with domain-adversarial training (to discourage the model from encoding domain-specific topical information). These techniques aim to suppress spurious topical correlations while maintaining sensitivity to methodological signals that truly define publication types and study designs.
AI.result: The experiments show that it is possible to mitigate the typical trade-off between robustness and in-domain accuracy. Classifiers trained with the proposed methods maintain strong in-domain performance while becoming substantially more robust to semantic perturbations. Analysis reveals that the improved models shift their focus toward explicit methodological cues, when available, and away from spurious topical features tied to particular domains or datasets.
AI.conclusion: Feature-level robustness analysis is crucial for reliable publication type and study design classification. By designing masking and adversarial objectives that selectively suppress topical information without erasing methodological content, models can become more robust to distributional shifts without sacrificing in-domain accuracy. Further refining these objectives is a promising path to even more robust biomedical literature indexing systems.

title: CPEMH: An Agentic Framework for Prompt-Driven Behavior Evaluation and Assurance in Foundation-Model Systems for Mental Health Screening
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures. Accepted at the AGENT 2026 Workshop (ICSE 2026)
summary: This paper presents CPEMH, an agentic framework designed to evaluate prompt-driven behavior in foundation-model systems operating on transcript-based datasets for mental-health screening. CPEMH serves as an engineering methodology for behavioral assurance in large-scale language systems, introducing an orchestrated architecture that autonomously performs the design, evaluation, and selection of prompt strategies, enabling systematic control of behavioral variability across contexts. Its modular agentic design, combining orchestrator, inference, and evaluation agents, ensures traceability, reproducibility, and robustness throughout the prompting lifecycle. A case study on automated depression screening from interview transcripts demonstrates the framework's capacity to stabilize and audit foundation-model behavior in conversational and clinically sensitive domains. Lessons learned emphasize the role of modular orchestration in behavioral assurance, the prioritization of stability over architectural complexity, and the integration of F1, bias, and robustness as core acceptance criteria.

AI.tldr: Introduces CPEMH, an agent-based framework for evaluating and stabilizing prompt-driven behavior of foundation models on transcript-based mental-health screening tasks.
AI.motivation: Foundation models used for mental-health screening via text transcripts show high behavioral variability and lack systematic, auditable methods to design and evaluate prompts, which is risky in clinically sensitive settings. The paper aims to provide an engineering framework for behavioral assurance and prompt strategy management in such systems.
AI.method: Proposes CPEMH, an agentic, modular framework with orchestrator, inference, and evaluation agents. The orchestrator coordinates automated design, evaluation, and selection of prompt strategies; inference agents run the foundation model over transcript data; evaluation agents assess outputs using metrics like F1, bias, and robustness. The architecture focuses on traceability, reproducibility, and robustness across the full prompting lifecycle and is instantiated on a depression-screening task using interview transcripts.
AI.result: In a case study on automated depression screening from interview transcripts, CPEMH successfully stabilizes and audits the behavior of foundation models, enabling more consistent and robust classification performance while surfacing bias and robustness characteristics under different prompting strategies.
AI.conclusion: CPEMH demonstrates that modular, orchestrated agentic architectures can provide behavioral assurance for prompt-driven foundation-model systems in conversational, clinically sensitive domains. The work concludes that stability should be prioritized over architectural complexity and that evaluation criteria must jointly consider F1, bias, and robustness to determine acceptable model behavior.

title: A Study on Hidden Layer Distillation for Large Language Model Pre-Training
summary: Knowledge Distillation (KD) is a critical tool for training Large Language Models (LLMs), yet the majority of research focuses on approaches that rely solely on output logits, neglecting semantic information in the teacher's intermediate representations. While Hidden Layer Distillation (HLD) showed potential for encoder architectures, its application to decoder-only pre-training at scale remains largely unexplored. Through compute-controlled experiments, we benchmark HLD against logit-based KD and self-supervised baselines with Gemma3 3.4B as teacher and 123M and 735M students trained on up to 168B tokens from the C4 dataset. Our experiments show that HLD does not consistently outperform standard KD on downstream evaluation tasks. Nevertheless, we show that HLD can yield a systematic perplexity gain over KD across all shared-hyperparameter configurations, suggesting that a latent signal can be extracted, but a breakthrough may be needed for it to play a more significant role in LLM pre-training.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates hidden layer distillation (HLD) for decoder-only LLM pre-training and finds that, although it consistently improves perplexity over logit-based knowledge distillation, it does not translate into better downstream task performance and thus needs further advances to be truly impactful.
AI.motivation: Most KD methods for LLMs only distill information from the teacher’s output logits and ignore the rich semantic signals in intermediate hidden representations. While HLD has been promising in encoder models, it is unclear whether and how it helps in large-scale decoder-only LLM pre-training, especially under realistic compute budgets.
AI.method: Use compute-controlled experiments to compare hidden layer distillation to standard logit-based KD and self-supervised training baselines. The teacher is a Gemma3 3.4B model and the student models are 123M and 735M parameters, trained on up to 168B C4 tokens. The study systematically varies shared hyperparameters and measures perplexity and downstream evaluation performance.
AI.result: HLD fails to consistently outperform standard logit-based KD on downstream evaluation tasks for decoder-only LLMs. However, across all shared-hyperparameter settings, HLD systematically improves training perplexity relative to logit-based KD, indicating that useful latent information from intermediate representations is being captured.
AI.conclusion: HLD does capture additional latent signal beyond what logit-based KD provides, as evidenced by systematic perplexity gains, but this benefit does not currently translate into consistent downstream improvements for decoder-only LLM pre-training. Achieving a meaningful impact from HLD in this setting likely requires new techniques or insights that better connect intermediate-representation distillation to task-level generalization.

title: CVEvolve: Autonomous Algorithm Discovery for Unstructured Scientific Data Processing
summary: Scientific data processing often requires task-specific algorithms or AI models, creating a barrier for domain scientists who need to analyze their data but may not have extensive computing or image-processing expertise. This barrier is especially pronounced when data are noisy, have a high dynamic range, are sparsely labeled, or are only loosely specified. We introduce CVEvolve, an autonomous agentic harness with a zero-code interface for scientific data-processing algorithm discovery. CVEvolve combines a multi-round search strategy with tools for code execution, evaluation implementation, history management, holdout testing, and optional inspection of scientific data and visual outputs. The search alternates between discovery and improvement actions, and uses lineage-aware stochastic candidate sampling to balance exploration and exploitation. We demonstrate CVEvolve on x-ray fluorescence microscopy image registration, Bragg peak detection, and high-energy diffraction microscopy image segmentation. Across these tasks, CVEvolve discovers algorithms that improve over baseline methods, while holdout test tracking helps identify candidates that generalize better than later over-optimized alternatives. These results show that zero-code, autonomous LLM-powered algorithm development can help domain scientists turn unstructured scientific image data into practical algorithms and downstream scientific discoveries.

AI.tldr: Introduces CVEvolve, a zero-code, LLM-powered autonomous system that searches for and refines image-processing algorithms for challenging scientific data, outperforming baselines on several microscopy tasks.
AI.motivation: Domain scientists often need custom image-processing or data-analysis algorithms but lack the programming, ML, or image-processing expertise to design and tune them. This barrier is worse for difficult data (noisy, high dynamic range, sparse labels, loosely defined tasks). There is a need for tools that let scientists obtain high-quality, task-specific algorithms from unstructured data without writing code.
AI.method: They build CVEvolve, an autonomous agentic framework with a zero-code interface that orchestrates multi-round search over candidate algorithms. The system can execute code, evaluate candidate solutions, track history, perform holdout testing, and optionally inspect scientific images and visual outputs. The search loop alternates between discovery (generating diverse new algorithmic ideas) and improvement (refining promising candidates), using lineage-aware stochastic sampling to balance exploration and exploitation while keeping track of how candidates evolve over time.
AI.result: Applied to three scientific imaging problems—x-ray fluorescence microscopy registration, Bragg peak detection, and high-energy diffraction microscopy segmentation—CVEvolve automatically discovers algorithms that outperform baseline approaches. Holdout test tracking reveals when earlier candidates generalize better than later ones that may be overfit to the development data.
AI.conclusion: Zero-code, autonomous LLM-driven algorithm discovery can convert unstructured scientific image data into effective, deployable algorithms, lowering the barrier for domain scientists and supporting more robust downstream scientific discoveries. CVEvolve provides evidence that agentic LLM systems can meaningfully contribute to scientific data-processing pipeline design without requiring users to write code.

title: Checkup2Action: A Multimodal Clinical Check-up Report Dataset for Patient-Oriented Action Card Generation
summary: Clinical check-up reports are multimodal documents that combine page layouts, tables, numerical biomarkers, abnormality flags, imaging findings, and domain-specific terminology. Such heterogeneous evidence is difficult for laypersons to interpret and translate into concrete follow-up actions. Although large language models show promise in medical summarisation and triage support, their ability to generate safe, prioritised, and patient-oriented actions from multimodal check-up reports remains under-benchmarked. We present \textbf{Checkup2Action}, a multimodal clinical check-up report dataset and benchmark for structured \textit{Action Card} generation. Each card describes one clinically relevant issue and specifies its priority, recommended department, follow-up time window, patient-facing explanation, and questions for clinicians, while avoiding diagnostic or treatment-prescriptive claims. The dataset contains 2,000 de-identified real-world check-up reports covering demographic information, physical examinations, laboratory tests, cardiovascular assessments, imaging-related evidence, and physician summaries. We formulate checkup-to-action generation as a constrained structured generation task and introduce an evaluation protocol covering issue coverage and precision, priority consistency, department and time recommendation accuracy, action complexity, usefulness, readability, and safety compliance. Experiments with general-purpose and medical large language models reveal clear trade-offs between issue coverage, action correctness, conciseness, and safety alignment. Checkup2Action provides a new multimodal benchmark for evaluating patient-oriented reasoning over clinical check-up reports.

AI.tldr: They build a multimodal dataset and benchmark (Checkup2Action) for turning clinical check-up reports into structured, safe, and patient-friendly action cards, and evaluate LLMs on it.
AI.motivation: Clinical check-up reports are rich, multimodal, and hard for laypeople to understand and turn into concrete follow-up steps. Existing LLM work on medical summarisation/triage doesn’t specifically focus on generating safe, prioritised, structured patient-oriented actions from such multimodal input, and this capability is under-benchmarked.
AI.method: They construct Checkup2Action, a dataset of 2,000 de-identified real-world multimodal check-up reports. For each report, they annotate structured ‘Action Cards’, each card focusing on one clinically relevant issue and specifying priority, recommended department, time window for follow-up, lay explanation, and questions for clinicians, under constraints that avoid diagnosis/treatment prescriptions. They formalise the problem as constrained structured generation and design an evaluation protocol measuring issue coverage/precision, consistency of priorities, accuracy of department/time suggestions, and qualitative properties like complexity, usefulness, readability, and safety. They then test general and medical LLMs on this benchmark.
AI.result: They obtain quantitative and qualitative metrics for several general-purpose and medical LLMs on the new benchmark, revealing trade-offs: models that cover more issues may sacrifice accuracy or safety, while safer or more concise models may miss issues or produce less correct actions.
AI.conclusion: Checkup2Action offers a new multimodal benchmark and structured task definition that enables systematic evaluation of LLMs’ ability to perform patient-oriented reasoning and generate safe, prioritised action plans from complex clinical check-up reports, highlighting current model trade-offs and guiding future model development.

title: Causal Bias Detection in Generative Artifical Intelligence
summary: Automated systems built on artificial intelligence (AI) are increasingly deployed across high-stakes domains, raising critical concerns about fairness and the perpetuation of demographic disparities that exist in the world. In this context, causal inference provides a principled framework for reasoning about fairness, as it links observed disparities to underlying mechanisms and aligns naturally with human intuition and legal notions of discrimination. Prior work on causal fairness primarily focuses on the standard machine learning setting, where a decision-maker constructs a single predictive mechanism $f_{\widehat Y}$ for an outcome variable $Y$, while inheriting the causal mechanisms of all other covariates from the real world. The generative AI setting, however, is markedly more complex: generative models can sample from arbitrary conditionals over any set of variables, implicitly constructing their own beliefs about all causal mechanisms rather than learning a single predictive function. This fundamental difference requires new developments in causal fairness methodology. We formalize the problem of causal fairness in generative AI and unify it with the standard ML setting under a common theoretical framework. We then derive new causal decomposition results that enable granular quantification of fairness impacts along both (a) different causal pathways and (b) the replacement of real-world mechanisms by the generative model's mechanisms. We establish identification conditions and introduce efficient estimators for causal quantities of interest, and demonstrate the value of our methodology by analyzing race and gender bias in large language models across different datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a causal-inference-based framework to analyze and quantify fairness in generative AI systems, extending existing causal fairness methods from standard predictive models to generative models and demonstrating its use on bias in large language models.
AI.motivation: AI systems, especially in high-stakes domains, can perpetuate or worsen demographic disparities. Most causal fairness work assumes a standard ML setup with a single predictive model and real-world covariates, which does not fit generative AI where models internally represent and sample from many causal mechanisms. There is a need for a principled, causal way to define, measure, and decompose fairness in generative models that can synthesize entire data distributions, not just predictions.
AI.method: The authors formalize causal fairness for generative AI by embedding both standard predictive models and generative models into a unified structural causal model framework. They generalize causal fairness definitions and derive decomposition results that separate fairness impacts along different causal pathways and separate the effects of replacing real-world mechanisms with the model’s learned mechanisms. They provide identification conditions for these causal quantities and propose efficient estimators that can be applied in practice.
AI.result: They obtain new decomposition theorems that break down fairness impacts in generative models into interpretable components: path-specific effects and mechanism-replacement effects. Under given assumptions, they show these effects are identifiable and can be consistently estimated. Empirically, they apply their estimators to large language models, quantifying race and gender bias across datasets and illustrating how different generative mechanisms contribute to observed disparities.
AI.conclusion: Causal fairness needs to be rethought for generative AI because such models effectively instantiate full sets of causal mechanisms, not just a single predictor. By unifying the standard ML and generative settings in one causal framework and providing decomposition, identification, and estimation tools, the paper offers a systematic way to analyze and quantify fairness in generative systems, as demonstrated on bias in large language models.

title: Taming Extreme Tokens: Covariance-Aware GRPO with Gaussian-Kernel Advantage Reweighting
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has emerged as a promising approach for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models. However, it struggles to effectively balance the tradeoff between exploration and exploitation during training, often resulting in suboptimal performance. Motivated by the theoretical insight that changes in entropy are governed by the covariance between token probabilities and their corresponding advantages, we propose a hyperparameter-free, covariance-weighted optimization method that dynamically down-weights extreme token-level updates via a Gaussian kernel. This approach automatically reduces the instability caused by exploration-exploitation trade-off while preserving informative learning signals. Extensive empirical evaluations show that our approach improves downstream performance across reasoning benchmarks compared with GRPO, and effectively stablizes entropy as training progresses.

AI.tldr: They propose a new, hyperparameter-free optimization variant of GRPO that stabilizes exploration–exploitation by covariance-weighted token updates, improving reasoning benchmarks and entropy stability.
AI.motivation: GRPO improves reasoning in large language models but has difficulty maintaining a good exploration–exploitation balance, leading to instability and suboptimal performance. The authors are motivated by theory linking entropy changes to the covariance between token probabilities and their advantages, suggesting a principled way to control update magnitudes without manual tuning.
AI.method: They derive a covariance-based view of entropy dynamics in GRPO and introduce a modified optimization rule that reweights token-level gradient updates by a Gaussian kernel applied to their covariance (or advantage) values. This acts as a smooth, hyperparameter-free mechanism that down-weights extreme token updates while leaving moderate, informative updates relatively unchanged. The method is integrated into GRPO as a drop-in replacement for its usual update rule.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple reasoning benchmarks show consistent improvements in downstream task performance over standard GRPO. Training curves indicate that the new method stabilizes policy entropy over time, reducing the oscillations and collapse associated with poor exploration–exploitation tradeoffs, while retaining or enhancing learning efficiency.
AI.conclusion: A covariance-weighted, Gaussian-kernel update rule addresses GRPO’s exploration–exploitation instability without extra hyperparameters. It yields better reasoning performance and more stable entropy during training, suggesting a generally useful way to regularize token-level policy updates in large language model training.

title: Causal Algorithmic Recourse: Foundations and Methods
summary: The trustworthiness of AI decision-making systems is increasingly important. A key feature of such systems is the ability to provide recommendations for how an individual may reverse a negative decision, a problem known as algorithmic recourse. Existing approaches treat recourse outcomes as counterfactuals of a fixed unit, ignoring that real-world recourse involves repeated decisions on the same individual under possibly different latent conditions. We develop a causal framework that models recourse as a process over pre- and post-intervention outcomes, allowing for partial stability and resampling of latent variables. We introduce post-recourse stability conditions that enable reasoning about recourse from observational data alone, and develop a copula-based algorithm for inferring the effects of recourse under these conditions. For settings where paired observations of the same individual before and after intervention are available (called recourse data), we develop methods for inferring copula parameters and performing goodness-of-fit testing. When the copula model is rejected, we provide a distribution-free algorithm for learning recourse effects directly from recourse data. We demonstrate the value of the proposed methods on real and semi-synthetic datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a causal framework and statistical methods to evaluate and learn algorithmic recourse (how individuals can overturn negative AI decisions) accounting for repeated decisions and latent variability.
AI.motivation: Most existing algorithmic recourse methods assume a single counterfactual decision for a fixed individual, ignoring that in reality, the same person may face repeated decisions under changing, unobserved conditions. This gap makes recourse assessment unrealistic and potentially misleading. The paper aims to build a more realistic and causally sound framework for understanding and estimating the impact of recourse in such dynamic settings.
AI.method: The authors model recourse as a causal process linking pre- and post-intervention outcomes, allowing some latent factors to be stable and others to be resampled between decisions. They define post-recourse stability conditions under which one can reason about recourse using only observational data. They then propose a copula-based algorithm to infer recourse effects under these conditions. For scenarios with matched pre/post intervention records for the same individual (recourse data), they give procedures to estimate copula parameters and to conduct goodness-of-fit tests. If the copula is rejected, they design a nonparametric, distribution-free algorithm to directly learn recourse effects from the recourse data.
AI.result: The methods are implemented and tested on both real and semi-synthetic datasets. The experiments show that their causal and copula-based framework can effectively estimate recourse effects, and that the distribution-free approach is useful when the copula assumptions do not hold. Overall, the empirical results support that their framework better captures the dynamics and uncertainty of repeated recourse than traditional static counterfactual approaches.
AI.conclusion: Viewing algorithmic recourse as a causal process over repeated decisions with partially stable and partially varying latent conditions yields more realistic and reliable analysis than static counterfactual models. Under post-recourse stability, recourse effects can be learned from observational data via copula models; when those models fail, distribution-free methods based on recourse data still allow robust estimation. This framework advances trustworthy AI by providing more faithful tools to understand and evaluate how individuals can change negative automated decisions.

title: Three Regimes of Context-Parametric Conflict: A Predictive Framework and Empirical Validation
comment: 10 pages, 13 tables, no figures. 9,970 API calls across five frontier models
summary: The literature on how large language models handle conflict between their training knowledge and a contradicting document presents a persistent empirical contradiction: some studies find models stubbornly retain their trained answers, ignoring provided documents nearly half the time, while others find models readily defer to the document, following context approximately 96% of the time. We argue these contradictions dissolve once one recognises that prior experiments have studied three qualitatively distinct processing situations without distinguishing them. We propose a three-regime framework: Regime 1 (single-source updating, dominant predictor: evidence coherence), Regime 2 (competitive integration, dominant predictor: parametric certainty), and Regime 3 (task-appropriate selection, dominant predictor: task knowledge requirement). We formalise a distinction between parametric strength (exposure frequency) and parametric uniqueness (encoding consistency), showing empirically that these are orthogonal dimensions (r = -0.002, p = .97) with strength as the operative predictor in stable factual domains. We validate the framework across Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.5, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Llama 4 Maverick, and DeepSeek V3 using 9,970 API calls in three experimental phases. GEE logistic regression confirms the predicted Regime 2 certainty gradient for all five models (beta = -0.38 to -0.50, all p <= .013, BH-FDR corrected). A Regime 3 ablation shows task framing alone flips context-following from near-100% (contextual knowledge condition) to 6-71% (parametric knowledge condition), with all five models significant (p < .001). The certainty gradient is robust to multinomial outcome modeling, sensitivity analyses for hedging responses, and FDR correction.

AI.tldr: The paper explains why previous studies disagree about whether large language models follow new documents or stick to their training: they were unknowingly testing three different processing regimes. It proposes a three-regime framework, empirically distinguishes two kinds of parametric knowledge, and shows that which answer models give depends systematically on evidence coherence, parametric certainty, and task framing.
AI.motivation: Prior empirical work on large language models facing conflicts between training knowledge and new documents has yielded contradictory results: some report high stubbornness to training data, others strong deference to context. This inconsistency makes it hard to understand or reliably predict model behaviour in real applications where documents may conflict with pretraining. The paper is motivated by the need to reconcile these findings, build a unified conceptual framework for such conflicts, and identify the factors that actually govern when models follow context versus parametric knowledge.
AI.method: The authors propose a conceptual three-regime framework for LLM processing under knowledge conflict: (1) single-source updating, (2) competitive integration, and (3) task-appropriate selection. They formally distinguish parametric strength (how frequently a fact was seen during training) from parametric uniqueness (how consistently it is encoded). They empirically assess the orthogonality and predictive value of these dimensions, then design experiments that instantiate the three regimes across five frontier models (Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.5, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Llama 4 Maverick, DeepSeek V3). Using 9,970 API calls, they collect model responses under controlled document–parametric conflicts, then analyze them with GEE logistic regression, multinomial models, and sensitivity checks for hedging. They manipulate task framing to flip between contextual-knowledge and parametric-knowledge conditions and run ablations to isolate regime-specific effects.
AI.result: They find that parametric strength and uniqueness are statistically orthogonal (r = -0.002, p = .97), so they capture distinct properties of model knowledge, with parametric strength emerging as the key predictor in stable factual settings. Across all five models, GEE logistic regressions show a consistent certainty gradient in Regime 2 (competitive integration): higher parametric certainty predicts lower context-following (beta ≈ -0.38 to -0.50, all p ≤ .013 after BH-FDR correction). In Regime 3, changing only the task framing—whether the task calls for contextual versus parametric knowledge—drastically shifts behaviour: context-following rates move from near 100% when the task invites contextual knowledge to as low as 6–71% when it calls for parametric knowledge, with all effects highly significant (p < .001). These results hold under multinomial outcome modeling, robustness checks for hedged answers, and multiple-comparisons correction.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that apparent contradictions in prior work on LLM responses to conflicting training versus contextual information stem from lumping together three distinct processing regimes. Once one separates single-source updating, competitive integration, and task-appropriate selection, model behaviour becomes predictable: evidence coherence, parametric certainty, and task framing dominate in Regimes 1–3 respectively. Parametric strength, not uniqueness, is the operative factor for stable factual knowledge. The proposed framework provides a unified account of when and why LLMs follow documents versus their training, offering clearer guidance for experiment design, model evaluation, and application-level prompting where knowledge conflicts are likely.

title: LLM-X: A Scalable Negotiation-Oriented Exchange for Communication Among Personal LLM Agents
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures, accepted at AGENT 2026 Workshop, co-located with ICSE 2026
summary: We propose a personal-LLM exchange (LLM-X), a scalable negotiation-oriented environment that enables direct, structured communication across populations of personal agents (LLMs), each representing an individual user.
