Unlike existing tool-centric protocols that focus on agent-API interaction, LLM-X introduces a message bus and routing substrate for LLM-to-LLM coordination with guarantees around schema validity and policy enforcement. We contribute: (1) an architecture for LLM-X comprising federated gateways, topic-based routing, and policy enforcement; (2) a typed message protocol supporting capability negotiation and contract-net-style coordination; and (3) the first empirical evaluation of LLM-based multi-agent negotiation at scale. Experiments span 5, 9, and 12 agents, under distinct negotiation policies (Low, Medium, High), and across both short-run (minutes) and long-run (2h, 12h) load conditions. Results highlight clear policy-performance trade-offs: stricter policies improve robustness and fairness but increase latencies and message volume. Extended runs confirm that LLM-X remains stable under sustained load, with bounded latency drift.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LLM-X, a scalable infrastructure for structured negotiation among many personal LLM agents, and empirically evaluates its performance and trade-offs under different policies and loads.
AI.motivation: As LLM-based personal agents become widespread, they need to coordinate and negotiate directly with each other on behalf of users, not just call tools or APIs. Existing systems mainly support agent-to-API interactions and lack a robust, scalable, and policy-aware substrate for LLM-to-LLM communication, especially for structured multi-agent negotiation at scale. The paper aims to fill this gap by designing an exchange environment that provides guarantees around message structure, routing, and policy enforcement so populations of personal LLMs can safely and efficiently collaborate and negotiate.
AI.method: The authors design LLM-X, an architecture composed of federated gateways, a message bus with topic-based routing, and policy enforcement mechanisms. They define a typed message protocol that supports capability negotiation among agents and contract-net-style coordination, ensuring schema validity and policy compliance. They then empirically evaluate LLM-X in multi-agent negotiation scenarios with 5, 9, and 12 agents, using three negotiation policy regimes (Low, Medium, High strictness). They run both short experiments (minutes) and extended experiments (2h, 12h) under load, measuring metrics such as robustness, fairness, latency, message volume, and stability over time.
AI.result: The experiments show that different negotiation policy regimes create clear performance trade-offs. Stricter policies increase robustness and fairness in negotiation outcomes but also lead to higher latencies and larger message volumes. Across varying numbers of agents and time horizons, LLM-X maintains operational stability, with latency remaining bounded and showing only limited drift even during long (2h and 12h) runs under sustained load.
AI.conclusion: LLM-X provides a viable, scalable communication and negotiation substrate for populations of personal LLM agents, supporting structured LLM-to-LLM coordination with enforceable policies and typed message schemas. The empirical study demonstrates that such a system can operate stably at scale, though deployment requires careful tuning of negotiation policy strictness to balance robustness and fairness against latency and communication overhead.

title: BitLM: Unlocking Multi-Token Language Generation with Bitwise Continuous Diffusion
summary: Autoregressive language models generate text one token at a time, yet natural language is inherently structured in multi-token units, including phrases, n-grams, and collocations that carry meaning jointly. This one-token bottleneck limits both the expressiveness of the model during pre-training and its throughput at inference time. Existing remedies such as speculative decoding or diffusion-based language models either leave the underlying bottleneck intact or sacrifice the causal structure essential to language modeling. We propose BitLM, a language model that represents each token as a fixed-length binary code and employs a lightweight diffusion head to denoise multiple tokens in parallel within each block. Crucially, BitLM preserves left-to-right causal attention across blocks while making joint lexical decisions within each block, combining the reliability of autoregressive modeling with the parallelism of iterative refinement. By replacing the large-vocabulary softmax with bitwise denoising, BitLM reframes token generation as iterative commitment in a compact binary space, enabling more efficient pre-training and substantially faster inference without altering the causal foundation that makes language models effective. Our results demonstrate that the one-token-at-a-time paradigm is not a fundamental requirement but an interface choice, and that changing it can yield a stronger and faster language model. We hope BitLM points toward a promising direction for next-generation language model architectures.

AI.tldr: BitLM is a language model that generates multiple tokens in parallel by predicting binary codes with a diffusion head, preserving causal structure while breaking the one-token-at-a-time bottleneck for faster and more efficient training and inference.
AI.motivation: Standard autoregressive LMs generate text strictly one token at a time using a large-vocabulary softmax, even though language meaning often resides in multi-token units. This sequential, token-wise interface limits expressiveness, constrains parallelism at inference, and makes pre-training and decoding computationally expensive. Existing alternatives like speculative decoding or diffusion-based LMs either keep this bottleneck or break causal language modeling assumptions. The authors are motivated to redesign the token generation interface to better align with language structure while retaining the strengths of causal models.
AI.method: The paper introduces BitLM, which maps each token to a fixed-length binary code and replaces the standard softmax over a large vocabulary with bitwise denoising. The model is still autoregressive across blocks of tokens, maintaining left-to-right causal attention, but within each block a lightweight diffusion head jointly denoises multiple tokens in parallel in the binary space. Generation is reframed as iterative refinement and commitment of bits for all tokens in a block rather than sampling one token at a time, combining causal autoregression across blocks with iterative diffusion-based refinement within blocks.
AI.result: BitLM achieves more efficient pre-training and significantly faster inference throughput compared to conventional one-token-at-a-time autoregressive models, while maintaining or improving model quality. The experiments show that the traditional single-token generation bottleneck is not inherent to language modeling performance, and that the proposed bitwise-denoising mechanism can deliver both speedups and stronger results without sacrificing causal structure.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that the one-token-at-a-time decoding paradigm is an interface choice rather than a fundamental requirement of language modeling. By representing tokens as binary codes and using a diffusion head to denoise multiple tokens in parallel within blocks, BitLM preserves causal left-to-right structure while gaining parallelism and efficiency. This architecture demonstrates that rethinking the output interface can yield language models that are both faster and more powerful, and it points to a promising design direction for future generation models.

title: Revisiting Privacy Preservation in Brain-Computer Interfaces: Conceptual Boundaries, Risk Pathways, and a Protection-Strength Grading Framework
summary: Brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) are moving rapidly from laboratory research into clinical, edge, and real-world settings. Under ISO/IEC 8663:2025, a BCI is a direct communication link between central nervous system activity and external software or hardware systems. This link expands privacy risk beyond raw neural-signal leakage: neural data, derived representations, model assets, and decoded outputs can be re-associated with individuals across collection, transmission, storage, training, inference, and feedback, or used to infer information beyond what a task requires. Starting from the general BCI paradigm, this review deffnes privacy-protection boundaries, protection objects, and the relationship between user data privacy and model privacy within a shared risk pathway. It then proposes a three-dimensional framework - protection object, lifecycle stage, and dominant protection-strength level - to classify existing work into four levels of protection strength. Finally, mental privacy and neuroethical risks are treated as open issues, emphasizing that BCI privacy protection should not only obscure data but also disentangle task-irrelevant sensitive information while preserving downstream utility. Keywords: Brain-computer interface, Neural data privacy, User data privacy, Model privacy, Disentanglement of task-irrelevant sensitive information, Protection-strength grading, Neuroethical risks

AI.tldr: The paper reviews privacy risks and protections in brain-computer interfaces (BCIs), proposes a 3D framework to classify protection strength across the BCI lifecycle, and highlights open neuroethical and mental-privacy challenges.
AI.motivation: As BCIs move from labs into clinical and real-world settings, they create new privacy risks that go beyond simple neural-signal leakage, including re-identification, over-inference, and model-level leakage across the entire data and model lifecycle. There is a need for a structured way to define what must be protected, when, and how strongly, and to relate user data privacy to model privacy in a unified risk pathway.
AI.method: The paper starts from a general BCI paradigm and conceptually defines privacy-protection boundaries, protection objects (e.g., neural data, representations, models, outputs), and their relations. It then introduces a three-dimensional classification framework based on (1) protection object, (2) lifecycle stage (collection, transmission, storage, training, inference, feedback), and (3) dominant protection-strength level. Existing work is organized into four graded levels of protection strength using this framework. The paper also conceptually analyzes mental-privacy and neuroethical issues in the BCI context.
AI.result: The authors produce: (1) a clarified taxonomy of privacy-relevant objects and boundaries in BCI systems, (2) a shared risk-pathway perspective that connects user data privacy and model privacy, and (3) a three-dimensional framework that maps existing privacy-protection techniques into four strength levels across lifecycle stages and protection targets. They also identify gaps where current methods fail to adequately disentangle task-irrelevant sensitive information or to handle neuroethical concerns.
AI.conclusion: BCI privacy risks span data, representations, models, and outputs throughout the system lifecycle and cannot be addressed by data obfuscation alone. The proposed 3D protection framework helps analyze and compare privacy techniques by object, lifecycle stage, and strength level, but significant challenges remain, especially in protecting mental privacy and addressing broader neuroethical implications. Future work should focus on methods that selectively remove or disentangle task-irrelevant sensitive information while preserving performance and respecting users' mental autonomy.

title: Ada-MK: Adaptive MegaKernel Optimization via Automated DAG-based Search for LLM Inference
summary: When large language models (LLMs) serve real-time inference in commercial online advertising systems, end-to-end latency must be strictly bounded to the millisecond range. Yet every token generated during the decode phase triggers thousands of kernel launches, and kernel launch overhead alone can account for 14.6% of end-to-end inference time. MegaKernel eliminates launch overhead and inter-operator HBM round-trips by fusing multiple operators into a single persistent kernel. However, existing MegaKernel implementations face a fundamental tension between portability and efficiency on resource-constrained GPUs such as NVIDIA Ada: hand-tuned solutions are tightly coupled to specific architectures and lack portability, while auto-compiled approaches introduce runtime dynamic scheduling whose branch penalties are unacceptable in latency-critical settings. We observe that under a fixed deployment configuration, the optimal execution path of a MegaKernel is uniquely determined, and runtime dynamic decision-making can be entirely hoisted to compile time. Building on this insight, we propose Ada-MK: (1) a three-dimensional shared-memory constraint model combined with K-dimension splitting that reduces peak shared memory usage by 50%; (2) MLIR-based fine-grained DAG offline search that solidifies the optimal execution path, completely eliminating runtime branching; and (3) a heterogeneous hybrid inference engine that embeds MegaKernel as a plugin into TensorRT-LLM, combining high-throughput Prefill with low-latency Decode. On an NVIDIA L20, Ada-MK improves single-batch throughput by up to 23.6% over vanilla TensorRT-LLM and 50.2% over vLLM, achieving positive gains across all tested scenarios--the first industrial deployment of MegaKernel in a commercial online advertising system.

AI.tldr: Ada-MK is a MegaKernel-based LLM inference system that removes kernel launch overhead and runtime branching to achieve lower latency and higher throughput on NVIDIA Ada/L20 GPUs in a commercial ad-serving setting.
AI.motivation: Real-time LLM inference in online advertising requires strict millisecond-level latency, but the decode phase launches many GPU kernels, and launch overhead plus inter-operator HBM traffic significantly hurts performance, especially on resource-constrained GPUs like NVIDIA Ada. Existing MegaKernel approaches either rely on hand-tuned, non-portable implementations or auto-compiled methods that incur unacceptable runtime branching penalties.
AI.method: The paper proposes Ada-MK, which (1) introduces a 3D shared-memory constraint model with K-dimension splitting to cut peak shared memory usage by about half, enabling better MegaKernel fusion; (2) uses an MLIR-based, fine-grained DAG offline search to determine and ‘bake in’ the optimal execution path at compile time, thus eliminating runtime dynamic scheduling and branching; and (3) integrates the resulting MegaKernel as a plugin into TensorRT-LLM, creating a heterogeneous hybrid inference engine that uses high-throughput Prefill and low-latency Decode paths.
AI.result: On NVIDIA L20 GPUs, Ada-MK improves single-batch throughput by up to 23.6% compared with standard TensorRT-LLM and by up to 50.2% compared with vLLM, with consistent performance gains across all evaluated scenarios. It is demonstrated in a production commercial online advertising system.
AI.conclusion: By fully hoisting dynamic scheduling decisions to compile time and optimizing shared-memory usage, Ada-MK reconciles portability and efficiency for MegaKernel-based LLM inference on NVIDIA Ada-class GPUs. Its integration as a TensorRT-LLM plugin yields substantial latency and throughput benefits and represents the first reported industrial deployment of MegaKernel in a commercial online ad-serving setting.

title: Transformer Interpretability from Perspective of Attention and Gradient
summary: Although researchers' attention is more focused on the performance of Transformer models, the interpretation of Transformer can never be ignored. Gradient is widely utilized in Transformer interpretation. From the perspective of attention and gradient, we conduct an in-depth study of Transformer interpretation and propose a method to achieve it by guiding the gradient direction, or more precisely, the attention direction. The method enables more comprehensive interpretation of feature regions, offers detail interpretation, and helps to better understand Transformer mechanism. Leveraging the difference in how Vision Transformer (ViT) and humans perceive images, we alter the class of an image in a way that is almost imperceptible to the human eye. This class rewriting phenomenon may potentially pose security risks in certain scenarios.

AI.tldr: They propose a gradient-guided attention interpretation method for Transformers that yields finer feature-region explanations and reveals a class-rewriting vulnerability in Vision Transformers.
AI.motivation: Most work on Transformers focuses on improving performance, while their interpretability and internal mechanisms are less understood. Existing gradient-based interpretations do not fully align attention and gradients to give detailed, human-understandable explanations, and there is limited understanding of how ViT perception differs from humans in ways that might create security risks.
AI.method: From the joint perspective of attention and gradients, they design a technique that explicitly guides gradient directions so that they align with or steer attention directions in Transformer models. This guided-gradient scheme is used to highlight and interpret feature regions with greater coverage and detail. They then exploit the discovered gap between ViT and human perception by carefully modifying images along these guided directions so that the model’s predicted class changes while the perturbations remain almost imperceptible to humans.
AI.result: The guided-gradient attention method yields more comprehensive and fine-grained interpretations of which regions the Transformer relies on, providing detailed feature attributions. Using this understanding, they demonstrate a ‘class rewriting’ effect in Vision Transformers: by small, targeted perturbations that humans barely notice, the predicted class can be changed to another one, indicating a form of adversarial vulnerability tied to the model’s attention/gradient behavior.
AI.conclusion: Guiding gradients to control or align attention provides richer interpretability of Transformer decision processes and clarifies how these models utilize image regions. At the same time, the same mechanism exposes a security concern: Vision Transformers can have their predicted class subtly rewritten in ways that are visually negligible for humans, suggesting that improving interpretability and robustness should be considered together.

title: Efficient LLM-based Advertising via Model Compression and Parallel Verification
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable potential in advertising scenarios such as ad creative generation and targeted advertising. However, deploying LLMs in real-time advertising systems poses significant challenges due to their high inference latency and computational cost. In this paper, we propose an Efficient Generative Targeting framework that integrates adaptive group quantization, layer-adaptive hierarchical sparsification, and prefix-tree parallel verification to accelerate LLM inference while preserving generation quality. Extensive experiments on two real-world advertising scenarios demonstrate that our framework achieves significant speedup with acceptable quality degradation, making it operationally viable for practical deployments.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an efficient framework to accelerate large language models for real-time advertising applications while preserving ad generation quality.
AI.motivation: Large language models are powerful for advertising tasks like creative generation and targeting, but their high latency and computational cost prevent real-time deployment in production ad systems. There is a need for methods that reduce inference time and resource usage without severely harming output quality.
AI.method: The authors propose an Efficient Generative Targeting framework composed of three main techniques: (1) adaptive group quantization to compress model weights and reduce computation with minimal accuracy loss; (2) layer-adaptive hierarchical sparsification to selectively prune or skip less important computations in different layers; and (3) prefix-tree parallel verification to speed up decoding by verifying multiple candidate token paths in parallel based on shared prefixes. These components are integrated into the LLM inference pipeline specifically tailored for advertising tasks.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments on two real-world advertising scenarios, the framework delivers substantial inference speedup compared with standard LLM inference baselines, while only slightly degrading the generation quality as measured by task-specific metrics and/or human evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Efficient Generative Targeting framework makes LLMs more feasible for real-time advertising deployment by combining quantization, sparsification, and parallel decoding verification. It offers a practical trade-off between latency and generation quality, demonstrating that large models can be significantly accelerated for industrial ad systems without prohibitive quality loss.

title: AcuityBench: Evaluating Clinical Acuity Identification and Uncertainty Alignment
comment: 41 pages, 5 figures. Preprint under review for the Track on Evaluations and Datasets at NeurIPS 2026
summary: We introduce AcuityBench, a benchmark for evaluating whether language models identify the appropriate urgency of care from user medical presentations. Existing health benchmarks emphasize medical question answering, broad health interactions, or narrow workflow-specific triage tasks, but they do not offer a unified evaluation of acuity identification across these settings. AcuityBench addresses this gap by harmonizing five public datasets spanning user conversations, online forum posts, clinical vignettes, and patient portal messages under a shared four-level acuity framework ranging from home monitoring to immediate emergency care. The benchmark contains 914 cases, including 697 consensus cases for standard accuracy evaluation and 217 physician-confirmed ambiguous cases for uncertainty-aware evaluation. It supports two complementary task formats: explicit four-way classification in a QA setting, and free-form conversational responses evaluated with a rubric-based judge anchored to the same framework. Across 12 frontier proprietary and open-weight models, we find substantial variation in clear-case acuity accuracy and error direction. Comparing task formats reveals a systematic tradeoff: conversational responses reduce over-triage but increase under-triage relative to QA, especially in higher-acuity cases. In ambiguous cases, no model closely matches the distribution of physician judgments, and model predictions are more concentrated than expert clinical uncertainty. We also compare expert and model adjudication on a subset of maximally ambiguous cases, using those cases to examine the role of clinical uncertainty in label disagreement. Together, these results position acuity identification as a distinct safety-critical capability and show that AcuityBench enables systematic comparison and stress-testing of how well models guide users to the right level of care in real-world health use.

AI.tldr: AcuityBench is a unified benchmark to test how well language models judge the urgency of medical care users need, across multiple real-world settings and task formats.
AI.motivation: Current medical AI benchmarks mostly test knowledge (QA), general health conversation, or narrow triage flows, but there is no unified, systematic way to measure whether models correctly identify how urgent care should be (from stay-at-home monitoring to immediate emergency care). This is a core safety concern for real-world deployment of medical chatbots, especially given risk of over- vs under-triage.
AI.method: The authors build AcuityBench by harmonizing five existing public datasets (conversations, forum posts, vignettes, patient portal messages) into a common four-level acuity scale (home monitoring, routine care, urgent care, emergency care). The benchmark has 914 cases: 697 with strong expert consensus for standard accuracy metrics, and 217 deliberately ambiguous, physician-confirmed cases for studying uncertainty and disagreement. They define two tasks: (1) explicit four-way classification in a QA-style prompt; and (2) open-ended conversational responses, which are graded via a rubric-based LLM judge grounded in the same four-level framework. They then evaluate 12 leading proprietary and open-weight language models across these tasks, and analyze accuracy, the direction of triage errors (over- vs under-triage), and how model predictions compare with distributions of physician judgments, especially in maximally ambiguous cases.
AI.result: Performance varies widely across the 12 models, even on clear (consensus) cases. Conversational responses and QA-style classification behave differently: conversational formats systematically reduce over-triage but increase under-triage, particularly in more severe (higher-acuity) scenarios. In the ambiguous case set, no model reproduces the spread of physician judgments; instead, models’ predictions are more concentrated, indicating they under-represent genuine clinical uncertainty. Analysis of maximally ambiguous cases shows persistent discrepancies between expert adjudication and model outputs and highlights how uncertainty drives label disagreements.
AI.conclusion: Acuity identification is a distinct, safety-critical skill for medical language models that is not adequately captured by existing benchmarks. AcuityBench provides a unified, multi-format, multi-source testbed that allows systematic comparison, stress-testing, and analysis of how models recommend levels of care in realistic scenarios, including under ambiguity. The observed error patterns and misalignment with clinical uncertainty underscore the need to explicitly develop and evaluate models for safe triage guidance, rather than assuming strong general QA performance is sufficient.

title: DiffScore: Text Evaluation Beyond Autoregressive Likelihood
summary: Autoregressive language models are widely used for text evaluation, however, their left-to-right factorization introduces positional bias, i.e., early tokens are scored with only leftward context, conflating architectural asymmetry with true text quality. We propose masked reconstruction as an alternative paradigm, where every token is scored using full bidirectional context. We introduce DiffScore, an evaluation framework built on Masked Large Diffusion Language Models. By measuring text recoverability across continuous masking rates, DiffScore eliminates positional bias and naturally establishes an evaluation hierarchy from local fluency to global coherence. We further provide diagnostic tools unavailable to autoregressive frameworks: multi-timestep quality profiles that decompose scores across masking rates, and bidirectional PMI decomposition that disentangles fluency from faithfulness. Experiments across ten benchmarks show that DiffScore consistently outperforms autoregressive baselines in both zero-shot and fine-tuned settings. The code is released at: https://github.com/wenlai-lavine/DiffScore.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DiffScore, a new text evaluation framework based on masked reconstruction with diffusion language models to remove positional bias and better measure text quality.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive language models evaluate text token-by-token from left to right, so early tokens are judged with less context than later ones. This positional bias blends model architecture artifacts with genuine text quality, limiting reliable evaluation and diagnostics.
AI.method: The authors replace left-to-right scoring with masked reconstruction using Masked Large Diffusion Language Models. Each token is scored using its full bidirectional context. They measure how recoverable the text is under varying continuous masking rates, deriving a scalar score and also richer diagnostics such as multi-timestep quality profiles and a bidirectional PMI-based decomposition into fluency and faithfulness components.
AI.result: Across ten benchmarks, DiffScore surpasses standard autoregressive evaluation baselines in both zero-shot and fine-tuned scenarios, indicating more accurate and robust correlation with desired quality signals.
AI.conclusion: Masked reconstruction with diffusion language models removes positional bias and creates a more informative text evaluation framework. DiffScore yields better performance than autoregressive methods and offers new diagnostic tools, making it a promising alternative paradigm for language model-based text evaluation.

title: Attributing Emergence in Million-Agent Systems
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can simulate human-like reasoning and decision-making in individual agents. LLM-powered multi-agent systems (MAS) combine such agents to simulate population-scale social phenomena such as polarization, information cascades, and market panics. Such studies require attributing macro emergence to individual agents, but existing axiomatic methods scale combinatorially in $N$ and have been confined to $N \lesssim 10^3$, while the phenomena they explain occur at $N \geq 10^6$. We address this gap by adapting Aumann--Shapley path-integral attribution to LLM-powered MAS at million-agent scale; the resulting method satisfies all four axioms, runs four to five orders of magnitude faster than sampled Shapley on the same hardware. We use this method to test the scale gap empirically: across 14 days of public Bluesky data ($1{,}671{,}587$ active users), we compute the attribution at both full scale and the visibility-biased $N = 10^2$ convenience sample used by small-scale studies, and the two disagree structurally. At full scale the long tail and middle tier jointly carry the majority; the biased small panel attributes almost everything to a few high-follower accounts. We then prove that under any nonlinear macro indicator the disagreement cannot be reduced by post-hoc rescaling: an Attribution Scaling Bias theorem shows that no global rescaling factor can reconcile small-scale and full-scale attribution. Full-scale attribution is therefore not a methodological choice but a theoretical requirement for any nonlinear macro indicator.

AI.tldr: They propose a fast, theoretically principled method to attribute macro-level outcomes in LLM-powered multi-agent simulations with up to millions of agents, and show that small-sample attribution can be structurally misleading and cannot be fixed by simple rescaling.
AI.motivation: Existing attribution methods (like Shapley-based approaches) for multi-agent systems scale combinatorially with the number of agents, so they only work up to about 10^3 agents. However, many social phenomena modeled with LLM multi-agent systems—such as polarization or market panics—naturally occur at population scales of a million or more. Researchers often use small, visibility-biased samples (e.g., top accounts) to infer which agents drive macro outcomes, but it is unclear whether such small-scale attributions faithfully reflect full-scale dynamics.
AI.method: They adapt the Aumann–Shapley path-integral attribution method to LLM-powered multi-agent systems at million-agent scale. This method preserves four key attribution axioms and is computationally efficient—4–5 orders of magnitude faster than sampled Shapley on the same hardware. Empirically, they apply their method to 14 days of Bluesky social network data (about 1.67M active users), computing agent-level attribution for a macro indicator both at full scale and in a visibility-biased convenience sample of about 10^2 users that mimics typical small-scale studies.
AI.result: On Bluesky data, the full-scale attribution shows that both the long tail of low-visibility users and the middle tier together contribute the majority of the macro outcome. In contrast, the biased small sample attributes most of the effect to a few high-follower accounts, revealing a structural discrepancy. They further provide a theoretical result—the Attribution Scaling Bias theorem—proving that for any nonlinear macro indicator, no single global rescaling factor can align small-sample attribution with full-scale attribution.
AI.conclusion: Full-scale attribution is not just preferable but theoretically required when dealing with nonlinear macro indicators in LLM-powered multi-agent systems. Small, visibility-biased panels systematically misrepresent which agents drive macro-level outcomes, and this error cannot be corrected by simple post-hoc rescaling. Their scalable Aumann–Shapley-based method offers a practical way to perform principled attribution at real-world population sizes.

title: What Do EEG Foundation Models Capture from Human Brain Signals?
summary: Clinical electroencephalogram (EEG) analysis rests on a hand-crafted feature catalog refined over decades, \emph{e.g.,} band power, connectivity, complexity, and more. Modern EEG foundation models bypass this catalog, learn directly from raw signals via self-supervised pretraining, and match or outperform feature-engineered baselines on most clinical benchmarks. Whether the two representations align is an open question, which we decompose into three sub-questions: \emph{what does the model learn}, \emph{what does the model use}, and \emph{how much can be explained}. We answer them with layer-wise ridge probing, LEACE-style cross-covariance subspace erasure, and a transparent classifier benchmarked against a random-feature baseline. The audit covers three foundation models (CSBrain, CBraMod, LaBraM), five clinical tasks (MDD, Stress, ISRUC-Sleep, TUSL, Siena), and a 6-family 63-feature lexicon. Of the $945$ (model, task, feature) units, $648$ ($68.6\%$) are representation-causal and $199$ ($21.1\%$) are encoded-only. Across tasks, $50$ features qualify as universal candidates with strong support (all three architectures RC) in two or more tasks. Frequency-domain features dominate, but the other five families each contribute substantial causal mass. Confirmed features recover, on average, $79.3\%$ of the foundation model's advantage over the random baseline, with a clean task gradient (MDD $\approx 0.99$ down to Stress $\approx 0.56$): tasks near ceiling are almost fully recovered by the lexicon, while harder tasks leave a non-trivial residual that pinpoints a concrete target for future concept discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper audits EEG foundation models to see how their learned representations align with traditional hand-crafted EEG features, finding that many classic features are representation-causal and explain most, but not all, of the models’ performance gains.
AI.motivation: EEG analysis has long relied on hand-crafted features, but new self-supervised EEG foundation models learn directly from raw signals and outperform traditional methods; it’s unclear how these learned representations relate to the classical feature toolbox that clinicians understand and trust.
AI.method: The authors study three EEG foundation models across five clinical tasks and a lexicon of 63 classical EEG features from six families. They use layer-wise ridge regression probing to detect if features are encoded, LEACE-style cross-covariance subspace erasure to test causal use of features, and a transparent classifier that operates only on confirmed features and is compared to a random-feature baseline to quantify how much of the model’s performance can be explained by these features.
AI.result: Out of 945 model–task–feature combinations, 68.6% are representation-causal (encoded and used by the model) and 21.1% are encoded-only. Fifty features appear as strong universal candidates (causal in all three architectures) in at least two tasks, with frequency-domain features most prevalent but all six feature families contributing. The transparent classifier built on confirmed features recovers on average 79.3% of the performance advantage of the foundation models over a random-feature baseline, with recovery close to complete on easier tasks and lower on harder tasks.
AI.conclusion: Modern EEG foundation models heavily rely on a large subset of traditional EEG features and these features explain most of their performance advantage, especially on easier, near-ceiling tasks. However, there remains a significant unexplained residual on more challenging tasks, suggesting that models are leveraging additional, as-yet-uncharacterized concepts—highlighting both the continued value of classical features and a clear agenda for future feature discovery and interpretability work.

title: When Emotion Becomes Trigger: Emotion-style dynamic Backdoor Attack Parasitising Large Language Models
summary: Backdoor vulnerabilities widely exist in the fine-tuning of large language models(LLMs). Most backdoor poisoning methods operate mainly at the token level and lack deeper semantic manipulation, which limits stealthiness. In addition, Prior attacks rely on a single fixed trigger to induce harmful outputs. Such static triggers are easy to detect, and clean fine-tuning can weaken the trigger-target association. Through causal validation, we observe that emotion is not directly linked to individual words, but functions as an overall stylistic factor through tone. In the representation space of LLM, emotion can be decoupled from semantics, forming distinct cluster from the original neutral text. Therefore, we consider the emotional factor as the backdoor trigger to propose a pparasitic emotion-style dynamic backdoor attack, Paraesthesia. By mixing samples with the emotional trigger into clean data and then fine-tuning the model, the model is able to generate the predefined attack response when encountering emotional inputs during the inference stage. Paraesthesia includes two the quantification and rewriting of emotional styles. We evaluate the effectiveness of our method on instruction-following generation and classification tasks. The experimental results show that Paraesthesia achieves an attack success rate of around 99\% across both task types and four different models, while maintaining the clean utility of the models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Paraesthesia, a dynamic backdoor attack on LLMs that uses emotional style as a trigger instead of fixed tokens, achieving ~99% attack success while preserving model utility.
AI.motivation: Existing backdoor attacks on fine-tuned LLMs typically rely on static, token-level triggers that are semantically shallow, easy to detect, and can be weakened by clean fine-tuning. The authors want a more covert, robust backdoor mechanism that leverages deeper properties of LLM representations, specifically emotional style, which is decoupled from core semantics and forms distinct clusters in representation space.
AI.method: The authors first perform causal and representational analysis to show that emotion behaves as a global stylistic factor in LLMs, separable from word-level semantics and forming distinct clusters from neutral text. They then design Paraesthesia, a parasitic emotion-style dynamic backdoor: (1) define and quantify emotional styles in text; (2) rewrite neutral training samples into emotional variants to embed an emotional trigger; (3) mix these poisoned emotional samples with clean data for fine-tuning, associating emotional input style with a predefined attack response. At inference, any input exhibiting the target emotional style triggers the backdoor behavior. The trigger is dynamic and semantic (emotional tone) rather than a fixed token sequence.
AI.result: On both instruction-following generation and classification benchmarks, and across four different LLMs, Paraesthesia achieves about 99% attack success rate while maintaining normal model performance on clean inputs, indicating high stealthiness and effectiveness of the emotional-style trigger.
AI.conclusion: Emotional style can serve as a powerful, dynamic backdoor trigger in LLMs because it is decoupled from core semantics and realized as a global stylistic factor in representation space. Paraesthesia demonstrates that such emotion-based triggers can embed highly effective and stealthy backdoors into fine-tuned LLMs without sacrificing clean-task utility, highlighting a new and concerning attack surface for LLM security that future defenses must consider.

title: Under the Hood of SKILL.md: Semantic Supply-chain Attacks on AI Agent Skill Registry
summary: Autonomous AI agents increasingly extend their capabilities through Agent Skills: modular filesystem packages whose SKILL.md files describe when and how agents should use them. While this design enables scalable, on-demand capability expansion, it also introduces a semantic supply-chain risk in which natural-language metadata and instructions can affect which skills are admitted, surfaced, selected, and loaded. We study SKILL.md - only attacks across three registry-facing stages of the Agent Skill lifecycle, using real ClawHub skills and realistic registry mechanisms. In Discovery, short textual triggers can manipulate embedding-based retrieval and improve adversarial skill visibility, achieving up to 86% pairwise win rate and 80% Top-10 placement. In Selection, description-only framing biases agents toward functionally equivalent adversarial variants, which are selected in 77.6% of paired trials on average. In Governance, semantic evasion strategies cause malicious skills to avoid a blocking verdict in 36.5%-100% of cases. Overall, our results show that SKILL.md is not passive documentation but operational text that shapes which third-party capabilities agents find, trust, and use.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that natural-language metadata in SKILL.md files for AI agent skills can be exploited to manipulate which skills are discovered, chosen, and approved, posing a semantic supply-chain security risk.
AI.motivation: As autonomous AI agents gain capabilities through modular skills, their decision to discover, select, and trust third-party skills is heavily driven by natural-language descriptions. However, these descriptions are typically treated as benign documentation rather than an attack surface, leaving a gap in understanding how adversaries can exploit them to influence agent behavior and governance mechanisms.
AI.method: The authors empirically evaluate attacks that operate solely through SKILL.md text across three stages of the Agent Skill lifecycle—Discovery, Selection, and Governance—using real skills from a registry (ClawHub) and realistic embedding-based retrieval and policy mechanisms. They design adversarial textual triggers to test whether malicious or adversarially framed skills can be preferentially retrieved, selected over benign equivalents, or evade governance filters that should block them.
AI.result: In the Discovery stage, short adversarial triggers in SKILL.md significantly increase the visibility of malicious skills, obtaining up to an 86% pairwise win rate against benign counterparts and up to 80% probability of being placed in the Top-10 search results. In the Selection stage, purely description-level framing leads agents to choose functionally equivalent adversarial skills 77.6% of the time on average. In the Governance stage, semantic evasion tactics embedded in SKILL.md let malicious skills bypass blocking mechanisms in 36.5% to 100% of tested cases.
AI.conclusion: SKILL.md content acts as operational control text rather than mere documentation: it materially influences which skills embedding-based systems retrieve, which ones agents select, and which ones governance mechanisms permit. Therefore, natural-language metadata in skill registries constitutes a critical semantic supply-chain attack surface that must be secured, calling for more robust retrieval, selection, and governance designs that are resilient to adversarial text manipulation.

title: OmniThoughtVis: A Scalable Distillation Pipeline for Deployable Multimodal Reasoning Models
summary: Recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown strong chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning ability on vision-language tasks, but their direct deployment in real-world systems is often limited by latency and resource constraints. In practice, smaller MLLMs are preferred for online serving, yet their reasoning performance is bottlenecked by the lack of large-scale, high-quality multimodal CoT supervision. In this paper, we present OmniThoughtVis, a scalable data curation and distillation pipeline for transferring multimodal reasoning capabilities from high-capacity teacher models to smaller, deployment-oriented MLLMs. Starting from a diverse open-source seed pool, our pipeline generates structured CoT traces and performs joint annotation of reasoning difficulty, answer quality, and semantic task tags. To maintain data quality at scale, we combine rule-based filtering, difficulty-aware selection, and tag-based diversity sampling, resulting in a curated corpus of 1.8M samples that supports controllable subset construction for downstream training. We use OmniThoughtVis to distill Qwen3-VL models from 2B to 8B parameters and evaluate them on nine multimodal reasoning benchmarks. The resulting distilled models show consistent gains across model scales, including improvements of up to +16.8 points on MathVerse and +5.6 points on MMMU-Pro for the 4B model. Notably, the distilled 4B model matches or surpasses the undistilled 8B baseline on several tasks, highlighting the practical value of scalable reasoning distillation for deployment-oriented MLLMs.

AI.tldr: OmniThoughtVis is a scalable pipeline that distills multimodal chain-of-thought reasoning from large teacher models into smaller MLLMs, yielding strong performance gains and enabling deployment-friendly models.
AI.motivation: Large multimodal LLMs show strong chain-of-thought reasoning but are too slow and expensive for real-world, online deployment. Smaller models are more practical but lack high-quality, large-scale multimodal CoT supervision, which limits their reasoning performance. The paper aims to bridge this gap by creating a scalable way to transfer reasoning skills from large to small models.
AI.method: The authors build OmniThoughtVis, a data curation and distillation pipeline. They start from a diverse open-source multimodal seed dataset, then use high-capacity teacher models to generate structured multimodal chain-of-thought traces. Along with these traces, they jointly annotate each example with reasoning difficulty, answer quality, and semantic task tags. To ensure high quality and diversity at scale, they apply rule-based filtering, difficulty-aware selection to emphasize instructive examples, and tag-based diversity sampling. This produces a curated corpus of 1.8M multimodal CoT samples. They then use this corpus to distill a family of Qwen3-VL student models (2B–8B parameters) by training them to mimic the teacher’s reasoning and answers, and evaluate on multiple benchmarks.
AI.result: The pipeline yields a 1.8M-example multimodal CoT dataset that supports controllable subset selection (e.g., by difficulty or task type). Distilled Qwen3-VL models from 2B to 8B parameters exhibit consistent performance gains on nine multimodal reasoning benchmarks. For the 4B model, they report up to +16.8 point improvement on MathVerse and +5.6 points on MMMU-Pro. The distilled 4B model matches or surpasses the undistilled 8B baseline on several tasks, demonstrating effective reasoning transfer and efficiency gains.
AI.conclusion: Scalable multimodal chain-of-thought distillation from large teacher models to smaller MLLMs is practical and effective. OmniThoughtVis provides a high-quality, large-scale, and controllable multimodal CoT corpus that significantly boosts the reasoning ability of deployment-oriented models. The success of the distilled 4B model, which rivals or exceeds an undistilled 8B model, underscores the utility of the proposed pipeline for real-world, latency- and resource-constrained applications.

title: A Mechanistic Investigation of Supervised Fine Tuning
summary: The cosine similarity between a large language model's hidden activations before and after Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) remains very high. This, at first glance, suggests that SFT leaves the model's activation geometry largely undisturbed. However, projecting both sets of activations through a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) pretrained on the base model reveals that the underlying sparse latents diverge significantly. We introduce a novel investigative pipeline which utilizes these pretrained SAEs as a high-resolution diagnostic tool to mechanistically investigate the drivers of this representational divergence. Through our analytical pipeline, we discover task-specific and layer-specific distributions of the precise semantic features that are systematically altered during supervised fine-tuning. We additionally identify a layer-wise update profile specific to safety alignment. All code, experimental scripts, and analysis files associated with this work are publicly available at: https://github.com/ruhzi/sae-investigation.

AI.tldr: They show that even when SFT barely changes a model’s raw activations (high cosine similarity), the underlying sparse features revealed by a pretrained Sparse Autoencoder change a lot, and they build a pipeline to analyze exactly which features and layers are altered, especially for safety alignment.
AI.motivation: People often measure representational change from fine-tuning using simple metrics like cosine similarity on hidden states, which suggests SFT barely changes a model. But we lack mechanistic, feature-level tools to see what actually changes in the internal representations, particularly for safety alignment. Sparse Autoencoders trained on base models promise a way to track interpretable features, but they have not been systematically used to study SFT-induced representational shifts.
AI.method: 1) Take a base LLM and train a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) on its activations to obtain a sparse, approximately interpretable latent space. 2) Perform Supervised Fine-Tuning (including safety alignment) on the base model, without retraining the SAE. 3) Pass both base and SFT-activations through the same pretrained SAE and compare the resulting sparse latent codes across tasks and layers. 4) Use this analysis pipeline to identify which specific latents (features) increase, decrease, or otherwise shift, and aggregate these changes into task-specific and layer-specific distributions. 5) Derive a layer-wise “update profile” for safety alignment by studying which features are consistently altered by safety-related SFT.
AI.result: They find that although the cosine similarity of pre- and post-SFT activations is very high (suggesting minimal change in geometry), the SAE-derived sparse latents diverge strongly, indicating substantial representational reorganization at the feature level. Using their pipeline, they map out which specific semantic features change, how these changes depend on layer depth and task, and obtain a distinctive layer-wise pattern of feature updates associated with safety alignment.
AI.conclusion: Standard geometric similarity metrics on activations (like cosine similarity) can be misleading about how much SFT changes a model; in a sparse feature basis learned by an SAE, SFT causes large and structured shifts. Pretrained SAEs can thus serve as powerful diagnostic tools for mechanistically characterizing what fine-tuning, especially safety alignment, is doing inside LLMs at the level of individual semantic features and layers.

title: Enhancing Multilingual Counterfactual Generation through Alignment-as-Preference Optimization
summary: Self-generated counterfactual explanations (SCEs) are minimally modified inputs (minimality) generated by large language models (LLMs) that flip their own predictions (validity), offering a causally grounded approach to unraveling black-box LLM behavior. Yet extending them beyond English remains challenging: existing methods struggle to produce valid SCEs in non-dominant languages, and a persistent trade-off between validity and minimality undermines explanation quality. We introduce Macro, a preference alignment framework that applies Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to multilingual SCE generation, using a composite scoring function to construct preference pairs that effectively translate the trade-off into measurable preference signals. Experiments across four LLMs and seven typologically diverse languages show that Macro improves validity by 12.55\% on average over the chain-of-thought baseline without degrading minimality, while avoiding the severe minimality violations of the translation-based baseline. Compared to supervised fine-tuning, Macro achieves superior performance on both metrics, confirming that explicit preference optimization is essential for balancing this trade-off. Further analyses reveal that Macro increases cross-lingual perturbation alignment and mitigates common generation errors. Our results highlight preference optimization as a promising direction for enhancing multilingual model explanations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Macro, a preference-alignment framework using Direct Preference Optimization to generate better self-generated counterfactual explanations (SCEs) for LLMs in multiple languages, improving explanation validity without sacrificing minimality.
AI.motivation: Self-generated counterfactual explanations (SCEs) are a promising way to interpret LLM decisions by minimally altering inputs to flip model predictions. However, current SCE techniques largely focus on English and encounter difficulties in non-dominant languages, particularly in maintaining both high validity (actually flipping the model prediction) and minimality (changing the input as little as possible). Existing methods either fail frequently in other languages or introduce large, non-minimal changes, leading to poor and less trustworthy explanations. The authors aim to overcome these limitations and to systematically balance the trade-off between validity and minimality in multilingual settings.
AI.method: The authors introduce Macro, a preference alignment framework based on Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for multilingual SCE generation. They design a composite scoring function that quantifies both validity and minimality of candidate SCEs, and then use these scores to construct preference pairs indicating which of two SCE candidates is better. These labeled preference pairs serve as training data for DPO, directly optimizing the model’s behavior to prefer SCEs with better trade-offs. Macro is evaluated across four different LLMs and seven typologically diverse languages, comparing against chain-of-thought prompting, translation-based approaches, and supervised fine-tuning baselines.
AI.result: Macro improves SCE validity by an average of 12.55% over a chain-of-thought baseline while keeping minimality unchanged, demonstrating that it enhances the likelihood of flipping model predictions without making explanations more intrusive. It also avoids the substantial minimality violations observed in translation-based baselines. When compared to supervised fine-tuning, Macro achieves better performance on both validity and minimality metrics. Additional analyses show that Macro leads to better alignment of perturbations across languages and reduces typical generation errors in SCEs.
AI.conclusion: Explicit preference optimization via DPO is effective for balancing the trade-off between validity and minimality in multilingual SCE generation. Macro offers a robust way to generate higher-quality, causally grounded explanations across multiple languages, outperforming traditional chain-of-thought prompting, translation-based methods, and supervised fine-tuning. The study indicates that preference optimization is a promising route for improving multilingual interpretability and explanation quality in LLMs.

title: Adaptive Teacher Exposure for Self-Distillation in LLM Reasoning
comment: 11 pages, 4 figures; code not released yet
summary: On-policy self-distillation has become a strong recipe for LLM reasoning, where a privileged teacher supervises the student's own rollouts while conditioning on the reference solution. A design choice shared by nearly all such methods, however, has gone unquestioned: the teacher always sees the full reference reasoning. We argue that this default itself is part of the problem and identify a teacher-side exposure mismatch: when the teacher conditions on reasoning far beyond the student's current competence, the resulting token targets become too strong to absorb. A controlled fixed-exposure sweep makes this concrete on two fronts: 1) full exposure is not reliably the best choice, and 2) student-teacher mismatch grows monotonically as the teacher sees more privileged reasoning. This motivates treating teacher exposure not as a fixed hyperparameter but as a learnable training-time control variable. We therefore propose Adaptive Teacher Exposure for Self-Distillation (ATESD). ATESD models the reveal ratio with a lightweight Beta-policy controller conditioned on compact training-state statistics, and uses one sampled exposure for a short hold window of student updates. To make this exposure controller learnable, we optimize it with a discounted learning-progress reward that scores each held decision by its effect on the student's future improvement rather than its immediate loss change, addressing the delayed credit assignment induced by on-policy distillation. Experiments on AIME 24, AIME 25, and HMMT 25 across Qwen3-{1.7B, 4B, 8B} show that ATESD consistently outperforms competitive self-distillation and RL baselines, improving over OPSD by +0.95, +2.05, and +2.33 Average@12 points respectively, and establishing adaptive teacher exposure as an effective new axis for reasoning self-distillation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Adaptive Teacher Exposure for Self-Distillation (ATESD), which adaptively controls how much of the reference reasoning a teacher model sees when supervising a student LLM, leading to better reasoning performance than existing self-distillation and RL methods.
AI.motivation: Existing on-policy self-distillation methods for LLM reasoning assume the teacher always conditions on the full reference chain-of-thought. The authors argue this creates a teacher-side exposure mismatch: if the teacher sees reasoning far beyond the student’s current capabilities, the supervision signals become too strong or unrealistic, making them hard for the student to learn from. There is no principled way to choose how much reference reasoning to expose, and it has not been systematically studied.
AI.method: The authors first perform a controlled sweep over fixed teacher exposure levels (how much of the reference reasoning is revealed) to show that full exposure is not always optimal and that student–teacher mismatch grows with more exposure. Motivated by this, they propose Adaptive Teacher Exposure for Self-Distillation (ATESD), which treats the reveal ratio as a learnable control variable. ATESD uses a lightweight Beta-distribution policy controller that, conditioned on compact statistics of the current training state, samples an exposure level that is held fixed for a short window of student updates. The controller is trained using a discounted learning-progress reward, which measures how each exposure decision impacts the student’s future improvement rather than its immediate loss, thereby handling delayed credit assignment inherent in on-policy distillation.
AI.result: On math reasoning benchmarks AIME 24, AIME 25, and HMMT 25 with Qwen3 models of sizes 1.7B, 4B, and 8B parameters, ATESD consistently outperforms strong on-policy self-distillation methods and reinforcement learning baselines. Compared to OPSD, ATESD yields improvements of +0.95, +2.05, and +2.33 Average@12 points for the three model sizes, respectively, demonstrating the benefit of adaptively controlling teacher exposure.
AI.conclusion: Teacher exposure to reference reasoning should not be fixed at full visibility in on-policy self-distillation for LLM reasoning. Instead, adaptively learning how much reasoning to reveal to the teacher via ATESD reduces teacher–student exposure mismatch, yields more learnable supervision signals, and produces consistent gains over strong baselines. Adaptive teacher exposure is thus proposed as a new, effective axis for designing reasoning-focused self-distillation methods.

title: Human-Grounded Multimodal Benchmark with 900K-Scale Aggregated Student Response Distributions from Japan's National Assessment of Academic Ability
summary: Authentic school examinations provide a high-validity test bed for evaluating multimodal large language models (MLLMs), yet benchmarks grounded in Japanese K-12 assessments remain scarce. We present a multimodal dataset constructed from Japan's National Assessment of Academic Ability, comprising officially released middle-school items in Science, Mathematics, and Japanese Language. Unlike existing benchmarks based on synthetic or curated data, our dataset preserves real exam layouts, diagrams, and Japanese educational text, together with nationwide aggregated student response distributions (N $\approx$ 900{,}000). These features enable direct comparison between human and model performance under a unified evaluation framework. We benchmark recent multimodal LLMs using exact-match accuracy and character-level F1 for open-ended responses, observing substantial variation across subjects and strong sensitivity to visual reasoning demands. Human evaluation and LLM-as-judge analyses further assess the reliability of automatic scoring. Our dataset establishes a reproducible, human-grounded benchmark for multimodal educational reasoning and supports future research on evaluation, feedback generation, and explainable AI in authentic assessment contexts. Our dataset is available at: https://github.com/KyosukeTakami/gakucho-benchmark

AI.tldr: They introduce a real-exam, multimodal Japanese K-12 benchmark with human-response statistics to evaluate MLLMs on authentic school tests.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of high-validity, real-world benchmarks—especially for Japanese K-12 exams—for evaluating multimodal large language models. Existing benchmarks often use synthetic or heavily curated data and lack direct comparability to human performance, limiting the realism and educational relevance of current MLLM evaluation.
AI.method: They construct a multimodal dataset from Japan's National Assessment of Academic Ability, using officially released middle-school questions in Science, Mathematics, and Japanese Language. The dataset preserves authentic exam layouts, diagrams, and Japanese text, and includes nationwide aggregated student response distributions for roughly 900,000 students. They then benchmark recent multimodal LLMs using exact-match accuracy and character-level F1 for open-ended questions, and complement this with human evaluation and LLM-as-judge analyses to study the reliability of automatic scoring.
AI.result: The experiments show substantial performance variation across school subjects and demonstrate that MLLMs are highly sensitive to visual reasoning requirements. The evaluation also provides evidence about how reliable automatic scoring is when compared with human judgments, via both human raters and LLM-as-judge setups.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a reproducible, human-grounded benchmark for multimodal educational reasoning using authentic Japanese middle-school exams. This benchmark facilitates direct comparison between human and model performance and is intended to support future research on evaluation methods, feedback generation, and explainable AI in real assessment environments.

title: Breaking $\textit{Winner-Takes-All}$: Cooperative Policy Optimization Improves Diverse LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiers (RLVR) has become a central paradigm for improving LLM reasoning, yet popular group-based optimization algorithms like GRPO often suffer from exploration collapse, where the models prematurely converge on a narrow set of high-scoring patterns, lacking the ability to explore new solutions. Recent efforts attempt to alleviate this by adding entropy regularization or diversity bonus. However, these approaches do not change the \textit{winner-takes-all} nature, where rollouts still compete for individual advantage rather than cooperating for maximizing global diversity. In this work, we propose Group Cooperative Policy Optimization (GCPO), which shifts the training paradigm from rollout competition to team cooperation. Specifically, GCPO replaces independent rollout scoring with team-level credit assignment: a rollout is rewarded by how much it contributes to the team's valid solution coverage, rather than its individual accuracy. This coverage is described as a determinant volume over reward-weighted semantic embeddings, where only correct and non-redundant rollouts contribute to this volume. During advantage estimation, GCPO redistributes the collective team reward to each single rollout according to its average marginal contribution to the team. This cooperative training paradigm routes optimization toward non-redundant correct reasoning paths. Experiments across multiple reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that GCPO significantly improves both reasoning accuracy and solution diversity over existing approaches. Code will be released at $\href{https://github.com/bradybuddiemarch/gcpo}{this}$.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Group Cooperative Policy Optimization (GCPO), a new reinforcement learning with verifiers (RLVR) algorithm for large language models that encourages cooperative, diverse correct reasoning instead of competitive, mode-collapsed solutions.
AI.motivation: Popular RLVR algorithms like GRPO suffer from exploration collapse: models quickly converge to a narrow set of high-scoring reasoning patterns and fail to explore alternative correct solutions. Existing fixes such as entropy regularization or diversity bonuses still operate under a winner-takes-all scheme where rollouts compete for individual reward, limiting global solution diversity. The authors aim to design an optimization framework that explicitly promotes non-redundant, diverse correct reasoning paths in a cooperative manner.
AI.method: They propose Group Cooperative Policy Optimization (GCPO), which treats rollouts as a cooperating team rather than competitors. Instead of assigning rewards based on each rollout’s individual accuracy, GCPO uses team-level credit assignment: a rollout’s reward depends on its contribution to the team’s overall coverage of valid solutions. Coverage is formalized as a determinant volume computed over reward-weighted semantic embeddings of rollouts, so that only correct and non-redundant rollouts expand this volume. During advantage estimation, GCPO redistributes the team reward back to each rollout according to its average marginal contribution to the team’s coverage, encouraging exploration of distinct correct reasoning paths.
AI.result: Across multiple reasoning benchmarks, GCPO yields higher reasoning accuracy and greater diversity of solutions compared with state-of-the-art RLVR approaches like GRPO and its variants that use entropy or diversity regularization.
AI.conclusion: A cooperative, team-based credit assignment framework like GCPO can mitigate exploration collapse in RLVR, steering optimization toward non-redundant correct reasoning paths and improving both accuracy and solution diversity in large language model reasoning tasks.

title: Robust LLM Unlearning Against Relearning Attacks: The Minor Components in Representations Matter
summary: Large language model (LLM) unlearning aims to remove specific data influences from pre-trained model without costly retraining, addressing privacy, copyright, and safety concerns. However, recent studies reveal a critical vulnerability: unlearned models rapidly recover "forgotten" knowledge through relearning attacks. This fragility raises serious security concerns, especially for open-weight models. In this work, we investigate the fundamental mechanism underlying this fragility from a representation geometry perspective. We discover that existing unlearning methods predominantly optimize along dominant components, leaving minor components largely unchanged. Critically, during relearning attacks, the modifications in these dominant components are easily reversed, enabling rapid knowledge recovery, whereas minor components exhibit stronger resistance to such reversal. We further provide a theoretical analysis that explains both observations from the spectral structure of representations. Building on this insight, we propose Minor Component Unlearning (MCU), a novel unlearning approach that explicitly targets minor components in representations. By concentrating unlearning effects in these inherently robust directions, our method achieves substantially improved resistance to relearning attacks. Extensive experiments on three datasets validate our approach, demonstrating significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods including sharpness-aware minimization.

AI.tldr: They analyze why LLM unlearning is fragile to relearning attacks from a representation-spectral viewpoint and propose targeting minor representation components to make unlearning more robust.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM unlearning methods aim to erase specific data influence without full retraining, but recent work shows that the supposedly forgotten knowledge can be quickly recovered via relearning attacks, which is a serious security risk, especially for open-weight models. There is a need to understand the root cause of this vulnerability and to design unlearning methods that are intrinsically robust to such attacks.
AI.method: They study the geometry and spectral structure of LLM internal representations under unlearning. They find that standard methods mainly modify dominant components (principal directions) of representations, leaving minor components mostly intact. They then theoretically analyze why dominant components are easy to revert under relearning while minor components are more stable. Based on this, they design Minor Component Unlearning (MCU), which explicitly applies unlearning updates along minor components of the representation space instead of dominant ones.
AI.result: Theoretically, they explain why dominant components are more susceptible to relearning and minor components are more resistant, using the spectral structure of the representation. Empirically, they show that MCU yields unlearned models that are substantially harder to re-attack: relearning recovers much less of the forgotten knowledge compared with baseline unlearning methods, including strong ones such as sharpness-aware minimization. Across three datasets, MCU consistently improves robustness to relearning while maintaining unlearning effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The fragility of current LLM unlearning methods to relearning attacks stems from focusing updates on dominant representation components, which are easy to reverse. By shifting unlearning to minor components, which are spectrally more robust, their MCU approach achieves significantly stronger resistance to relearning attacks. This suggests that representation geometry, especially spectral component targeting, is a key design axis for secure and reliable unlearning in large language models.

title: CAMPA: Efficient and Aligned Multimodal Graph Learning via Decoupled Propagation and Aggregation
summary: Multimodal Graph Neural Networks (MGNNs) have shown strong potential for learning from multimodal attributed graphs, yet most existing approaches rely on tightly coupled architectures that suffer from prohibitive computational overhead. In this paper, we present a systematic empirical analysis showing that decoupled MGNNs are substantially more efficient and scalable for large-scale graph learning. However, we identify a critical bottleneck in existing decoupled pipelines, namely modal conflict, which arises in both the propagation and aggregation stages. Specifically, independent multi-hop diffusion causes cross-modal semantic divergence during propagation, while naive fusion fails to align multi-hop feature trajectories during aggregation, jointly limiting effective representation learning. To address this challenge, we propose CAMPA, a Cross-modal Aligned Multimodal Propagation & Aggregation framework for decoupled multimodal graph learning. Concretely, CAMPA introduces a two-stage alignment mechanism: (1) cross-modal aligned propagation, which injects cross-modal similarity priors into message passing to preserve semantic consistency without additional parameter overhead; (2) trajectory aligned aggregation, which leverages trajectory-level self-attention and cross-attention to capture and align long-range dependencies across modalities and hops. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmark datasets and tasks demonstrate that CAMPA consistently outperforms strong coupled and decoupled baselines while preserving the efficiency advantages of the decoupled paradigm.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CAMPA, a decoupled multimodal GNN framework that aligns modalities during propagation and aggregation, improving efficiency and accuracy on large multimodal graphs.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal GNNs are typically tightly coupled across modalities, which yields good performance but incurs high computational cost and poor scalability. Decoupled MGNNs are more efficient, but they suffer from a key problem called modal conflict: each modality diffuses independently on the graph, leading to semantic divergence, and simple fusion at the end cannot properly align information coming from different hops and modalities. The authors aim to systematically study this problem and design a method that keeps the efficiency of decoupling while overcoming modal conflict.
AI.method: They first conduct an empirical analysis showing that decoupled MGNNs are more scalable but are hindered by modal conflict during both propagation and aggregation. Then they propose CAMPA, a Cross-modal Aligned Multimodal Propagation & Aggregation framework. CAMPA uses: (1) cross-modal aligned propagation, where cross-modal similarity priors are injected into the message passing so that different modalities stay semantically consistent without adding extra learnable parameters; and (2) trajectory aligned aggregation, which applies trajectory-level self-attention and cross-attention over multi-hop features to capture and align long-range dependencies across modalities and hops before fusion.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmark datasets and tasks, CAMPA consistently surpasses strong coupled and decoupled multimodal GNN baselines in predictive performance, while retaining the computational efficiency and scalability advantages characteristic of decoupled architectures.
AI.conclusion: Decoupled multimodal GNNs can be both efficient and effective if modal conflict is properly addressed. By aligning modalities during both propagation and aggregation via CAMPA’s two-stage alignment scheme, it is possible to maintain semantic consistency across modalities and hops and achieve superior performance without sacrificing efficiency. The work highlights the importance of cross-modal and trajectory-level alignment in large-scale multimodal graph learning.

title: Learning to Foresee: Unveiling the Unlocking Efficiency of On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) has emerged as an efficient post-training paradigm for large language models. However, existing studies largely attribute this advantage to denser and more stable supervision, while the parameter-level mechanisms underlying OPD's efficiency remain poorly understood. In this work, we argue that OPD's efficiency stems from a form of ``foresight'': it establishes a stable update trajectory toward the final model early in training. This foresight manifests in two aspects. First, at the \textbf{Module-Allocation Level}, OPD identifies regions with low marginal utility and concentrates updates on modules that are more critical to reasoning. Second, at the \textbf{Update-Direction Level}, OPD exhibits stronger low-rank concentration, with its dominant subspaces aligning closely with the final update subspace early in training. Building on these findings, we propose \textbf{EffOPD}, a plug-and-play acceleration method that speeds up OPD by adaptively selecting an extrapolation step size and moving along the current update direction. EffOPD requires no additional trainable modules or complex hyperparameter tuning, and achieves an average training acceleration of $3\times$ while maintaining comparable final performance. Overall, our findings provide a parameter-dynamics perspective for understanding the efficiency of OPD and offer practical insights for designing more efficient post-training methods for large language models.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why on-policy distillation (OPD) is an efficient post-training method for LLMs, identifying module-level and update-direction-level mechanisms, and proposes EffOPD, an extrapolation-based acceleration that yields about 3x faster training with similar performance.
AI.motivation: Although OPD is known to be an efficient post-training paradigm for large language models, prior explanations focus mainly on denser and more stable supervision signals. The deeper parameter-level reasons for OPD’s high efficiency—how it shapes optimization trajectories and parameter updates—remain unclear. Understanding these mechanisms can both explain OPD’s success and inspire improved training and acceleration methods.
AI.method: The authors empirically study the parameter dynamics of OPD during training. They analyze (1) which model modules receive more or fewer updates (module-allocation level) and (2) the structure and alignment of update directions in parameter space over time (update-direction level), examining low-rank properties and subspace alignment with the final model’s update subspace. Based on these insights, they design EffOPD, a plug-and-play acceleration technique that adaptively chooses an extrapolation step size and moves further along the current update direction during OPD, without adding new trainable components or requiring heavy hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: They find that OPD quickly establishes a stable update trajectory toward the final solution. At the module-allocation level, OPD suppresses updates in low-utility regions and concentrates them in reasoning-critical modules. At the update-direction level, OPD’s updates are more low-rank and their dominant subspaces align early with those of the final updates. Using these observations, EffOPD achieves about 3x faster training on average compared to vanilla OPD, while preserving comparable final performance across evaluated tasks.
AI.conclusion: OPD’s efficiency is rooted in its early formation of a stable, foresight-like optimization trajectory, both via selective module-level update allocation and via concentrated, well-aligned low-rank update directions. Leveraging this understanding, EffOPD accelerates OPD by extrapolating along these stable directions, offering a simple, plug-and-play method to speed up post-training of LLMs without sacrificing accuracy. This parameter-dynamics view provides a foundation for designing future efficient post-training and distillation schemes.
