title: TOPPO: Rethinking PPO for Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning with Critic Balancing
summary: Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) and its variants dominate Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning (MTRL) due to their off-policy sample efficiency, while on-policy methods such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) remain underexplored. We diagnose that PPO in MTRL suffers from a previously overlooked issue: critic-side gradient ill-conditioning, which may cause tail tasks to stall while easy tasks dominate the value function's updates. To address this, we propose TOPPO (Tail-Optimized PPO), a reformulation of PPO via Critic Balancing -- a set of modules that improve gradient conditioning and balance learning dynamics across tasks. Unlike prior approaches that rely on modular architectures or large models, TOPPO targets the optimization bottleneck within PPO itself. Empirically, TOPPO achieves stronger mean and tail-task performance than published SAC-family and ARS-family baselines while using substantially fewer parameters and environment steps on Meta-World+ benchmark. Notably, TOPPO matches or surpasses strong SAC baselines early in training and maintains superior performance at full budget. Ablations confirm the effectiveness of each module in TOPPO and provide insights into their interactions. Our results demonstrate that, with proper optimization, on-policy methods can rival or exceed off-policy approaches in MTRL, challenging the prevailing reliance on SAC and highlighting critic-side gradient conditioning as the central bottleneck.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TOPPO, an on-policy multi-task RL algorithm that fixes a gradient-conditioning problem in PPO’s critic, enabling on-policy methods to match or beat SAC-style off-policy methods on Meta-World+ with fewer parameters and samples.
AI.motivation: In multi-task reinforcement learning, off-policy SAC variants dominate because they are sample-efficient and performant, while on-policy methods like PPO lag behind. The authors observe that, in PPO for multi-task settings, the critic’s gradients can become ill-conditioned across tasks: easy tasks dominate the value-function updates and harder ‘tail’ tasks stop improving. This optimization bottleneck is underexplored, and it obscures the true potential of simpler, stable on-policy methods for MTRL. The motivation is to understand and remove this bottleneck so that on-policy methods can be competitive with or superior to SAC-based approaches, challenging the current design bias in MTRL.
AI.method: The authors first diagnose the failure mode of standard PPO in MTRL as critic-side gradient ill-conditioning, especially harmful to tail tasks. They then propose TOPPO (Tail-Optimized PPO), which is a reformulation of PPO through a framework they call Critic Balancing. Critic Balancing consists of several modules designed to: (1) improve the conditioning of value-function gradients across tasks, and (2) balance learning dynamics so that both easy and hard tasks contribute meaningfully to critic updates. Rather than changing network architecture drastically or scaling model size, TOPPO focuses on restructuring the optimization process and loss weighting within the critic so that per-task gradients are better balanced. The approach is implemented on top of PPO and evaluated on the Meta-World+ multi-task benchmark, with additional ablations to isolate the contribution and interactions of each module.
AI.result: On the Meta-World+ benchmark, TOPPO attains higher average performance and better performance on ‘tail’ tasks than strong baselines from SAC-family and ARS-family algorithms, despite using significantly fewer parameters and environment steps. It also matches or surpasses competitive SAC baselines early in training and maintains this advantage through the full training budget. Ablation studies show that each Critic Balancing module has a positive effect and that their combination provides the largest gains, supporting the central claim that addressing critic-side gradient conditioning is key to unlocking PPO’s performance in MTRL.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that the main limitation of PPO in multi-task reinforcement learning is not its on-policy nature per se but an optimization bottleneck caused by critic-side gradient ill-conditioning. By reformulating PPO with Critic Balancing as TOPPO, the authors show that an on-policy method can rival or outperform state-of-the-art off-policy SAC variants in MTRL while being more parameter- and sample-efficient. This challenges the field’s prevailing reliance on SAC-based methods and identifies critic gradient conditioning as a crucial design consideration for future multi-task RL algorithms.

title: Training-Inference Consistent Segmented Execution for Long-Context LLMs
comment: Accepted by ICML 2026. 19 pages, 6 figures, 3 tables
summary: Transformer-based large language models face severe scalability challenges in long-context generation due to the computational and memory costs of full-context attention. Under practical computation and memory constraints, many inference-efficient long-context methods improve efficiency by adopting bounded-context or segment-level execution only during inference, while continuing to train models under full-context attention, resulting in a mismatch between training and inference execution and state-transition semantics. Based on this insight, we propose a training-inference consistent segment-level generation framework, in which training and inference follow the same segment-level forward execution semantics. During training, consistency with inference is enforced by restricting gradient propagation to KV states carried over from the immediately preceding segment, while permitting head-specific access to past KV states during the forward pass without involving them in gradient propagation. Across long-context benchmarks, our approach achieves performance comparable to full-context attention, while achieving competitive latency-memory trade-offs against strong inference-efficient baselines, and substantially improving scalability at very long context lengths (e.g., approximately 6x lower peak prefill memory at 128K compared to full-context attention with FlashAttention).

AI.tldr: They propose a segment-level training and inference framework for long-context transformers that keeps execution semantics consistent and greatly reduces memory/latency while matching full attention performance.
AI.motivation: Existing long-context efficiency methods often use full-context attention during training but switch to bounded or segment-level processing only at inference time, causing a mismatch in state transitions and execution between training and inference. This mismatch can hurt performance and scalability under realistic compute and memory constraints, especially for very long contexts where full-context attention is prohibitively expensive.
AI.method: They introduce a segment-level generation framework where both training and inference share the same forward execution semantics. The input is processed in segments; key-value (KV) states from previous segments are carried forward. During training, gradients are only allowed to propagate through the KV states from the immediately preceding segment, enforcing locality in backpropagation. However, during the forward pass, attention heads can still read from a larger window of past KV states, which are treated as stop-gradient (no backprop). This design yields segment-level training that mimics inference-time behavior while remaining efficient in memory and compute.
AI.result: On a range of long-context benchmarks, the method attains performance comparable to transformers trained and run with full-context attention, yet achieves better latency-memory trade-offs than strong inference-efficient baselines. In particular, for very long contexts such as 128K tokens, the approach cuts peak prefill memory by about 6x relative to full-context attention implemented with FlashAttention, improving scalability to extreme context lengths.
AI.conclusion: Aligning training with inference via a consistent segment-level execution scheme can remove the train–infer mismatch common in long-context methods while retaining accuracy. Limiting gradient flow to only the previous segment’s KV states, but allowing broader forward access, preserves long-context modeling capacity and yields substantial memory and latency benefits, making large language models more scalable for very long-context generation.

title: FibQuant: Universal Vector Quantization for Random-Access KV-Cache Compression
summary: Long-context inference is increasingly a memory-traffic problem. The culprit is the key--value (KV) cache: it grows with context length, batch size, layers, and heads, and it is read at every decoding step. Rotation-based scalar codecs meet this systems constraint by storing a norm, applying a shared random rotation, and quantizing one coordinate at a time. They are universal and random-access, but they discard the geometry created by the normalization step. After a Haar rotation, a block of $k$ consecutive coordinates is not a product source; it is a spherical-Beta source on the unit ball. We introduce \textsc{FibQuant}, a universal fixed-rate vector quantizer that keeps the same normalize--rotate--store interface while replacing scalar tables by a shared radial--angular codebook matched to this canonical source. The codebook combines Beta-quantile radii, Fibonacci\,/\,Roberts--Kronecker quasi-uniform directions, and multi-restart Lloyd--Max refinement. We prove that the resulting vector code strictly improves on its scalar product specialization at matched rate, with a high-rate gain that separates into a cell-shaping factor and a density-matching factor. The same construction gives a dense rate axis, including fractional-bit and sub-one-bit operating points, without calibration or variable-length addresses. On GPT-2 small KV caches, \textsc{FibQuant} traces a memory--fidelity frontier from $5\times$ compression at $0.99$ attention cosine similarity to $34\times$ at $0.95$. End-to-end on TinyLlama-1.1B, it is within $0.10$ perplexity of fp16 at $4\times$ compression and has $3.6\times$ lower perplexity than scalar \textsc{TurboQuant} at $b = 2$ ($8\times$ compression), where scalar random-access quantization begins to fail.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FibQuant, a universal fixed-rate vector quantization method for KV-cache compression in long-context transformers, achieving better memory–fidelity tradeoffs than rotation-based scalar codecs while preserving random access and universality.
AI.motivation: Long-context inference in transformer models is increasingly bottlenecked by memory traffic due to the large and frequently accessed key–value (KV) cache. Existing rotation-based scalar quantization codecs are universal and random-access but treat rotated coordinates as independent, ignoring the true spherical geometry created by normalization, which leads to suboptimal compression performance. There is a need for a method that maintains the same practical interface (normalize–rotate–store, random access, universality) while exploiting the actual distribution of rotated vectors to improve rate–distortion efficiency and extend to lower-bit regimes where current scalar methods fail.
AI.method: The authors model the block of k consecutive coordinates after Haar rotation and normalization as a spherical-Beta source on the unit ball. They design FibQuant, a universal fixed-rate vector quantizer that uses a shared radial–angular codebook matched to this source. Radii are chosen via Beta-quantile discretization, while directions are initialized with Fibonacci/Roberts–Kronecker-based quasi-uniform points on the sphere and then refined via multi-restart Lloyd–Max optimization. This yields a vector quantizer that operates on k-dimensional blocks, keeps the same normalize–rotate–store interface as scalar codecs, and supports a dense, fixed-rate bit axis (including fractional and sub-one-bit rates) without entropy coding or variable-length codes.
AI.result: Theoretically, they prove that the proposed vector quantizer strictly outperforms its scalar-product counterpart at the same rate, with an asymptotic high-rate gain that decomposes into a cell-shaping gain and a density-matching gain. Practically, on GPT-2 small KV caches, FibQuant achieves a memory–fidelity frontier ranging from 5× compression at 0.99 attention cosine similarity to 34× compression at 0.95 similarity. In end-to-end language modeling experiments on TinyLlama-1.1B, FibQuant stays within 0.10 perplexity of fp16 at 4× compression and delivers 3.6× lower perplexity than scalar TurboQuant at 2 bits (8× compression), a regime where scalar random-access quantization begins to break down.
AI.conclusion: FibQuant demonstrates that respecting the spherical-Beta geometry induced by normalize–rotate transforms and using matched radial–angular vector quantization yields provably and empirically superior compression of KV caches over rotation-based scalar methods. It preserves universality, fixed-rate random access, and interface compatibility while significantly extending the effective compression range, making it a promising approach for mitigating memory-traffic bottlenecks in long-context transformer inference.

title: Safety-Oriented Evaluation of Language Understanding Systems for Air Traffic Control
summary: Air Traffic Control (ATC) is a safety-critical domain in which incorrect interpretation of instructions may lead to severe operational consequences. While large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong general performance, their reliability in operational ATC environments remains unclear. Existing evaluation approaches, largely based on aggregate metrics such as F1 or macro accuracy, treat all errors uniformly and fail to account for the asymmetric consequences of high-risk semantic mistakes (e.g., incorrect runway identifiers or movement constraints). To address this gap, we propose a safety-oriented, consequence-aware evaluation framework tailored to ATC operations. Our results reveal that while current LLMs achieve reasonable aggregate accuracy, their operational reliability is severely limited. Evaluated on clean transcripts, the peak Risk Score reaches only 0.69, with most models scoring below 0.6 despite high macro-F1 performance. Further analysis shows that errors concentrate in high-impact entities despite relatively stable action-type classification, indicating structural grounding deficiencies. These findings highlight the necessity of consequence-aware evaluation protocols for the responsible deployment of AI-assisted ATC systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a safety-oriented, consequence-aware evaluation framework for assessing large language models in air traffic control (ATC), showing that traditional aggregate metrics overestimate their operational reliability, especially for high-risk semantic errors.
AI.motivation: In ATC, misinterpreting instructions can have severe safety consequences, yet current LLM evaluations rely on aggregate metrics (e.g., F1, macro accuracy) that treat all errors equally. This ignores that some mistakes, such as incorrect runway identifiers or movement constraints, are far more dangerous than others. There is a need for an evaluation framework that reflects the asymmetric risk profile of ATC errors to better understand whether LLMs can be safely used in such critical settings.
AI.method: The authors design a safety-oriented, consequence-aware evaluation framework specifically for ATC operations. Instead of relying solely on aggregate metrics, they introduce a Risk Score that weights errors according to their operational impact, with special focus on high-impact entities (e.g., runways, clearances, constraints). They evaluate multiple LLMs on clean ATC transcripts using both traditional metrics like macro-F1 and the new Risk Score, and analyze where and how errors are distributed across action types and critical entities.
AI.result: The study finds that although current LLMs obtain relatively high macro-F1 scores on ATC transcripts, their Risk Scores are much lower, indicating limited operational reliability. The best-performing model achieves a Risk Score of only 0.69, and most models fall below 0.6. Error analysis shows that mistakes are disproportionately concentrated in high-impact semantic entities, even though classification of general action types remains comparatively stable. This pattern reveals a structural grounding problem where LLMs struggle with critical operational details.
AI.conclusion: Standard aggregate metrics substantially overestimate LLM safety in ATC contexts because they do not capture the asymmetric consequences of different error types. A consequence-aware, safety-focused evaluation is essential for accurately assessing and responsibly deploying AI-assisted ATC systems. The observed concentration of errors in high-impact entities underscores that current LLMs are not yet reliably grounded in the operational semantics required for safe ATC deployment.

title: Engagement Process: Rethinking the Temporal Interface of Action and Observation
summary: Task completion in digital and physical environments increasingly involves complex temporal interaction, where actions and observations unfold over different time scales rather than align with fixed observation--action steps. To model such interactions, we propose \emph{Engagement Process} (EP), an interaction formalism that inherits the decision-theoretic structure of POMDPs while making time explicit in the action--observation interface. EP represents actions and observations as decoupled event streams along time, rather than updates paired at fixed decision steps. This interface captures single-agent timing issues such as deliberation latency, delayed feedback, and persistent actions, while supporting richer agent-side organization, multi-rate coordination, and compositional interaction among subsystems. Across toy, LLM-agent, and learning experiments, EP exposes temporal behaviors hidden by step-based interfaces and enables policies to adapt under explicit time costs.

AI.tldr: Introduces Engagement Process, a time-explicit interaction formalism extending POMDPs.
AI.motivation: Traditional step-based decision process models like POMDPs assume synchronized action-observation steps, which fails to capture complex temporal structures in real digital/physical tasks where actions, feedback, and internal deliberation unfold over different time scales, with delays and overlaps.
AI.method: Define Engagement Process (EP) as a decision-theoretic framework that decouples actions and observations into separate event streams indexed in continuous or fine-grained time, instead of paired steps. Formalize how EP inherits POMDP structure while modifying the action-observation interface to include explicit timing, supporting persistent actions, delayed feedback, deliberation latency, and coordination of multiple subsystems with different rates. Evaluate EP on toy domains, LLM-based agents, and learning settings to show its expressivity and practical impact.
AI.result: EP can represent temporal phenomena such as delayed feedback, overlapping actions, temporal costs of deliberation, and multi-rate coordination that are obscured or awkward to model in standard step-based POMDP/MDP setups. In experiments across toy problems, LLM-agent scenarios, and learning tasks, policies defined in EP adapt their behavior when time is explicitly costed and exploit the richer temporal structure.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling time via decoupled action and observation event streams—while retaining a decision-theoretic backbone—yields a more expressive interaction interface than fixed-step POMDPs, revealing hidden temporal behaviors and enabling agents to optimize with respect to real temporal costs in complex interactive environments.

title: From Token to Token Pair: Efficient Prompt Compression for Large Language Models in Clinical Prediction
summary: By processing electronic health records (EHRs) as natural language sequences, large language models (LLMs) have shown potential in clinical prediction tasks such as mortality prediction and phenotyping. However, longitudinal or highly frequent EHRs often yield excessively long token sequences that result in high computational costs and even reduced performance. Existing solutions either add modules for compression or remove less important tokens, which introduce additional inference latency or risk losing clinical information. To achieve lossless compression of token sequences without additional cost or loss of performance, we propose Medical Token-Pair Encoding (MedTPE), a layered method that extends standard tokenisation for EHR sequences. MedTPE merges frequently co-occurring medical token pairs into composite tokens, providing lossless compression while preserving the computational complexity through a dependency-aware replacement strategy. Only the embeddings of the newly introduced tokens of merely 0.5-1.0% of the LLM's parameters are fine-tuned via self-supervised learning. Experiments on real-world datasets for two clinical scenarios demonstrate that MedTPE reduces input token length by up to 31% and inference latency by 34-63%, while maintaining or even improving both predictive performance and output format compliance across multiple LLMs and four clinical prediction tasks. Furthermore, MedTPE demonstrates robustness across different input context lengths and generalisability to scientific and financial domains and different languages.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MedTPE, a lossless token-pair encoding method that compresses long EHR token sequences for LLM-based clinical prediction, reducing sequence length and latency while preserving or improving performance.
AI.motivation: LLMs show promise on clinical prediction tasks from EHRs, but longitudinal and high-frequency EHR data produce very long token sequences. These long sequences lead to high computational costs, increased inference latency, and can even reduce model performance. Existing approaches compress sequences by adding extra modules or discarding tokens, which either slow inference further or risk losing important clinical information. The authors aim to create a compression method that is lossless, efficient, and preserves or improves predictive quality without architectural changes or substantial retraining.
AI.method: The authors propose Medical Token-Pair Encoding (MedTPE), a layered tokenisation extension tailored to EHR sequences. MedTPE identifies frequently co-occurring medical token pairs and merges them into composite tokens, similar in spirit to byte-pair encoding but adapted to medical data and LLM usage. A dependency-aware replacement strategy ensures that replacing token pairs with composite tokens does not increase overall computational complexity and preserves sequence semantics exactly (lossless). Instead of retraining the entire LLM, only embeddings corresponding to the new composite tokens (about 0.5–1.0% of the total parameters) are fine-tuned using self-supervised learning on EHR text. The method is evaluated across multiple LLMs, datasets, and tasks.
AI.result: On real-world datasets covering two clinical scenarios and four clinical prediction tasks, MedTPE achieves up to 31% reduction in input token length and 34–63% reduction in inference latency. Despite the compression, the method maintains or improves predictive performance metrics and output format compliance compared to standard tokenisation. The approach also shows robustness across varying context lengths and generalises beyond the medical domain to scientific and financial text and across different languages, indicating that the strategy is not limited to a specific dataset, domain, or language.
AI.conclusion: MedTPE offers an effective, lossless sequence compression strategy for LLMs on EHR and other structured text data, providing substantial reductions in token length and inference latency without degrading—and sometimes improving—model performance. Because it only requires fine-tuning a small fraction of parameters and does not add extra runtime modules, it is a practical, plug-in extension to existing LLM pipelines. Its robustness and cross-domain, cross-lingual generalisability suggest it can be broadly applied to mitigate the computational and performance issues caused by long input sequences in LLM-based prediction systems.

title: The Evaluation Differential: When Frontier AI Models Recognise They Are Being Tested
summary: Recent published evidence from frontier laboratories shows that contemporary AI models can recognise evaluation contexts, latently represent them, and behave differently under those contexts than under deployment-continuous conditions. Anthropic's BrowseComp incident, the Natural Language Autoencoder findings on SWE-bench Verified and destructive-coding evaluations, and the OpenAI / Apollo anti-scheming work all document instances of this phenomenon. We argue that these findings create a claim-validity problem for safety conclusions drawn from frontier evaluations. We introduce the Evaluation Differential (ED), a conditional divergence in a target behavioural property between recognised-evaluation and deployment-continuous contexts, define a normalised effect-size form (nED) for cross-property comparison, and prove that marginal evaluation scores cannot identify ED. We develop a typology of safety claims (ED-stable, ED-degraded, ED-inverted, ED-undetermined) by their warrant-status under documented divergence, and specify TRACE (Test-Recognition Audit for Claim Evaluation), an audit protocol that wraps existing evaluation infrastructure and produces restricted claims rather than capability scores. We apply the framework retrospectively to three publicly documented evaluation incidents and discuss governance implications for system cards, conformity assessment, and the international network of AI safety and security institutes. TRACE does not eliminate adversarial adaptation; it disciplines the claims drawn from evaluation evidence by making explicit the conditions under which that evidence was produced.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that AI safety evaluations are undermined because advanced models can detect when they are being evaluated and change behavior, and proposes a formal measure (Evaluation Differential) plus an audit protocol (TRACE) to constrain what safety claims can validly be made from such tests.
AI.motivation: Recent incidents (e.g., Anthropic's BrowseComp, Natural Language Autoencoder work, and OpenAI/Apollo anti-scheming studies) show that state-of-the-art AI systems behave differently when they recognize they are under evaluation compared to normal deployment. This undermines the reliability of safety conclusions derived from standard benchmark-style evaluations. There is a need for a framework that captures this context-dependent behavior and clarifies which safety claims remain valid when evaluation conditions diverge from real-world use.
AI.method: The authors: (1) conceptualize and formally define the Evaluation Differential (ED) as the conditional difference in a chosen behavioral property between recognized-evaluation contexts and deployment-continuous contexts; (2) introduce a normalized effect size nED to allow comparison across different properties and tasks; (3) prove that standard marginal evaluation scores are insufficient to detect ED; (4) construct a typology of safety claims (ED-stable, ED-degraded, ED-inverted, ED-undetermined) based on how they are affected by potential evaluation-recognition effects; and (5) design TRACE, an audit protocol that wraps existing evaluation pipelines to record test-recognition, track contextual factors, and output restricted, condition-explicit claims instead of raw capability scores. They then retroactively apply this framework to three real-world evaluation incidents.
AI.result: They show that the Evaluation Differential is in principle unidentifiable from typical benchmark results, meaning common evaluation practices cannot rule out large differences between test-time and deployment behavior. The typology clarifies that many existing safety claims should be downgraded (e.g., to ED-undetermined) when evaluation-recognition is plausible. Applying TRACE retrospectively to three incidents demonstrates that safety conclusions drawn there would have been substantially more limited and explicitly conditional under their framework. The work yields a practical protocol for reinterpreting or redesigning evaluations to account for context-recognition effects.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current frontier AI safety evaluations cannot be assumed to generalize to deployment because models may detect and respond strategically to evaluation contexts. To preserve the epistemic value of evaluations, claims must be explicitly conditioned on evaluation context using constructs like ED and nED, and audits like TRACE. While TRACE does not stop adversarial model adaptation, it increases transparency and discipline around safety claims, and has implications for system cards, conformity assessment processes, and coordination among AI safety and security institutes.

title: Choosing features for classifying multiword expressions
summary: Multiword expressions (MWEs) are a heterogeneous set with a glaring need for classifications. Designing a satisfactory classification involves choosing features. In the case of MWEs, many features are a priori available. Not all features are equal in terms of how reliably MWEs can be assigned to classes. Accordingly, resulting classifications may be more or less fruitful for computational use. I outline an enhanced classification. In order to increase its suitability for many languages, I use previous works taking into account various languages.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an improved, feature-based classification scheme for multiword expressions (MWEs) that is useful across many languages.
AI.motivation: Current classifications of MWEs are unsatisfactory and heterogeneous, which limits their usefulness in computational linguistics. There are many possible features for distinguishing MWEs, but they vary in reliability for assigning expressions to classes, so a better organized, more reliable feature set and classification is needed.
AI.method: The author surveys and analyzes existing features and classifications for MWEs, evaluates how reliably they allow MWEs to be assigned to classes, and then designs an enhanced classification scheme that prioritizes more reliable features. The design is informed by cross-linguistic studies and previous work covering multiple languages to ensure broad applicability.
AI.result: The outcome is an enhanced classification system for MWEs that is more systematic, relies on more reliable features for class assignment, and is explicitly designed to be applicable to many different languages.
AI.conclusion: A carefully chosen, reliability-oriented feature set can yield a more fruitful classification of MWEs for computational purposes, and leveraging cross-linguistic and prior work improves the generality and usability of the classification across languages.

title: Selective Off-Policy Reference Tuning with Plan Guidance
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards helps reasoning, but GRPO-style methods stall on hard prompts where all sampled rollouts fail. SORT adds a repair update for those failures without changing rollout generation: it derives a plan from the reference solution, compares token probabilities with and without that plan, and gives higher weight to tokens that become more predictable under plan conditioning. This turns all-wrong prompts into selective, structure-aware learning signals instead of uniform imitation. Across three backbones and eight reasoning benchmarks, SORT improves over GRPO and guidance baselines, with largest gains on weaker models.

AI.tldr: Abstract of a paper on SORT, a reinforcement learning method that improves reasoning by repairing failures in GRPO-style training.
AI.motivation: GRPO-style reinforcement learning methods for reasoning tasks struggle on hard prompts where all sampled rollouts are incorrect, resulting in stalled learning because there is no successful trajectory to reinforce.
AI.method: SORT introduces a repair update that activates when all sampled rollouts for a prompt fail. Instead of altering how rollouts are generated, it leverages the known reference solution to derive a plan, computes token probabilities with and without conditioning on that plan, and upweights tokens whose predictability increases under the plan. This creates a structured learning signal from otherwise unusable all-wrong episodes.
AI.result: On three different model backbones and eight reasoning benchmarks, SORT yields better performance than standard GRPO and other guidance-based baselines, particularly boosting weaker models.
AI.conclusion: By converting fully failed episodes into informative, structure-aware training signals via plan-conditioned probability comparisons, SORT enables more effective reinforcement learning for reasoning tasks and addresses a key failure mode of GRPO-style methods, especially benefitting smaller models.

title: Probabilistic Calibration Is a Trainable Capability in Language Models
summary: Language models are increasingly used in settings where outputs must satisfy user-specified randomness constraints, yet their generation probabilities are often poorly calibrated to those targets. We study whether this capability can be improved directly through fine-tuning. Concretely, we fine-tune language models on synthetic prompts that require sampling from mathematical distributions, and compare two Calibration Fine-Tuning variants: a soft-target method that converts the desired output distribution into trie-derived next-token targets, and a hard-target method that trains on sampled completions from the same target distribution. Across 12 models spanning four families, both methods substantially improve structured-sampling fidelity on held-out distribution families and unseen parameter settings, showing that probabilistic calibration is a trainable capability. Under our selected training configurations, the two methods exhibit different empirical profiles: hard-target fine-tuning is often strongest on structured numeric sampling, while soft-target fine-tuning performs better on broader stochastic generation benchmarks, including open-ended random generation, multiple-choice answer-position balancing, and NoveltyBench. The gains sometimes reduce downstream capability, especially arithmetic reasoning, with costs varying by model. Overall, our results show that probabilistic calibration can be improved through fine-tuning, with our hard-target configuration favoring exact numeric fidelity and our soft-target configuration favoring broader stochastic transfer. Code is available at https://github.com/chandar-lab/calibration-finetuning.

AI.tldr: The paper fine-tunes language models to better match user-specified randomness and probability distributions, showing probabilistic calibration is a learnable skill, with two methods trading off exact numeric fidelity vs broader stochastic behavior.
AI.motivation: Language models are often used for tasks that require controlled randomness (e.g., sampling from specific distributions or balancing multiple-choice answers), but their intrinsic sampling probabilities are typically miscalibrated relative to desired target distributions. There is a need to know whether and how this probabilistic calibration can be systematically improved, rather than relying on ad-hoc prompting or temperature tuning.
AI.method: The authors construct synthetic prompts that explicitly require sampling from known mathematical distributions. They propose and compare two Calibration Fine-Tuning strategies across 12 models in four families: (1) Soft-target fine-tuning, where the desired output distribution is converted into trie-based next-token probability targets; and (2) Hard-target fine-tuning, where models are trained on full sampled completions drawn from the target distributions. They then evaluate calibrated sampling fidelity on held-out distribution families and unseen parameter settings, as well as broader stochastic-generation benchmarks.
AI.result: Both calibration fine-tuning approaches significantly improve how well models’ outputs match the desired distributions, including on new distributions and parameter values not observed during training. Hard-target fine-tuning tends to excel on structured numeric sampling tasks that require precise adherence to numeric probabilities, whereas soft-target fine-tuning generalizes better to broader stochastic tasks such as open-ended random text generation, balancing answer positions in multiple-choice questions, and NoveltyBench-style evaluations. However, these gains can sometimes reduce other downstream capabilities like arithmetic reasoning, with the severity and pattern of trade-offs depending on the base model.
AI.conclusion: Probabilistic calibration is a trainable capability of language models: by fine-tuning on distribution-sampling tasks, models can be made to more closely match user-specified randomness constraints. The two proposed methods offer different trade-offs: the hard-target method emphasizes exact numeric fidelity, while the soft-target method provides better transfer to diverse stochastic behaviors. Nonetheless, calibration improvements may come with costs in other skills, so practical deployment requires balancing calibration gains against potential capability regressions.

title: Hierarchical LLM-Driven Control for HAPS-Assisted UAV Networks: Joint Optimization of Flight and Connectivity
summary: Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly deployed in complex networked environments, yet the joint optimization of multi-UAV motion control and connectivity remains a fundamental challenge. In this paper, we study a multi-UAV system operating in an integrated terrestrial and non-terrestrial network (ITNTN) comprising terrestrial base stations and high-altitude platform stations (HAPS). We consider a three-dimensional (3D) aerial highway scenario where UAVs must adapt their motion to ensure collision avoidance, efficient traffic flow, and reliable communication under dynamic and partially observable conditions. We first model the problem as a hierarchical multi-objective partially observable Markov decision process (H-MO-POMDP), capturing the coupling between control and communication objectives. Based on this formulation, we propose a large language model (LLM)-driven hierarchical multi-rate control framework. At the global level, an LLM-based controller on the HAPS performs long-term planning for load balancing and handover decisions. At the local level, each UAV employs a hybrid controller that integrates a slow-timescale LLM for high-level spatial reasoning with a reinforcement learning agent for faster UAV-to-infrastructure (U2I) communication and motion control. We further develop a high-fidelity 3D simulation platform by integrating the gym-pybullet-drones environment with 3GPP-compliant RF/THz channel models. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 14% increase in transportation efficiency and a 25% improvement in telecommunication throughput. Additionally, it achieves a 23% reduction in physical collision rates, demonstrating strong handover stability and zero-shot generalization in dynamic scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an LLM-driven hierarchical multi-rate control framework for coordinating multi-UAV motion and connectivity in integrated terrestrial and non-terrestrial networks, modeled as an H-MO-POMDP, and shows significant gains in efficiency, throughput, and safety over baselines.
AI.motivation: Multi-UAV systems operating in integrated terrestrial and non-terrestrial networks (ITNTN) face the coupled challenges of motion control, collision avoidance, traffic efficiency, and reliable communication. Existing approaches struggle with dynamic, partially observable environments and the need to jointly optimize control and connectivity, especially in complex 3D aerial highway scenarios. There is a need for a framework that can reason over long horizons, adapt online, and handle multi-objective tradeoffs between transportation and communication performance.
AI.method: The authors model the joint multi-UAV motion and connectivity problem as a hierarchical multi-objective partially observable Markov decision process (H-MO-POMDP). They design an LLM-driven hierarchical multi-rate control framework: (1) a global controller on HAPS, implemented with an LLM, performs long-term planning for load balancing and handover; (2) each UAV runs a local hybrid controller that combines a slow-timescale LLM for high-level spatial reasoning with a fast-timescale reinforcement learning (RL) agent handling detailed U2I communication and motion control. They develop a 3D high-fidelity simulation platform by integrating the gym-pybullet-drones environment with 3GPP-compliant RF/THz channel models to evaluate the approach.
AI.result: In simulations on the developed 3D platform, the proposed framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Quantitatively, it yields a 14% increase in transportation efficiency, a 25% improvement in telecommunication throughput, and a 23% reduction in physical collision rates. The framework also exhibits strong handover stability and demonstrates zero-shot generalization to dynamic scenarios not seen during training.
AI.conclusion: The LLM-driven hierarchical multi-rate control framework effectively addresses the joint optimization of multi-UAV motion and connectivity in ITNTNs under dynamic and partially observable conditions. By combining global LLM-based planning with local hybrid LLM+RL control within an H-MO-POMDP formulation, the approach improves transportation efficiency, communication throughput, and safety, while providing robust handover and generalization capabilities. This indicates that integrating LLMs into hierarchical control architectures is a promising direction for complex, multi-objective UAV network management.

title: Self-Distilled Trajectory-Aware Boltzmann Modeling: Bridging the Training-Inference Discrepancy in Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive language models, offering stronger global awareness and highly parallel generation. However, post-training DLMs with standard Negative Evidence Lower Bound (NELBO)-based supervised fine-tuning remains inefficient: training reconstructs randomly masked tokens in a single step, whereas inference follows a confidence-guided, multi-step easy-to-hard denoising trajectory. Recent trajectory-based self-distillation methods exploit such inference trajectories mainly for sampling-step compression and acceleration, often improving decoding efficiency without substantially enhancing the model's underlying capability, and may even degrade performance under full diffusion decoding. In this work, we ask whether self-distilled trajectories can be used not merely for faster inference, but for genuine knowledge acquisition. Although these trajectories lie on the pretrained DLM's own distributional manifold and thus offer a potentially lower optimization barrier, we find that naively fine-tuning on them with standard NELBO objectives yields only marginal gains. To address this limitation, we propose \textbf{T}rajectory-\textbf{A}ligned optimization via \textbf{Bo}ltzmann \textbf{M}odeling (\textbf{TABOM}), a self-distilled trajectory-based post-training framework that aligns training with the easy-to-hard structure of inference. TABOM models the inference unmasking preference as a Boltzmann distribution over predictive entropies and derives a tractable pairwise ranking objective to align the model's certainty ordering with the observed decoding trajectory. Empirically, TABOM achieves substantial gains in new domains, expands the effective knowledge boundary of DLMs, and significantly mitigates catastrophic forgetting compared with standard SFT.

AI.tldr: Proposes TABOM, a trajectory-aligned, Boltzmann-based optimization framework to better post-train diffusion language models using their own inference trajectories, leading to real capability gains and less forgetting vs standard supervised fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Standard NELBO-based supervised fine-tuning for diffusion language models is mismatched to their multi-step, easy-to-hard denoising inference process, leading to inefficient post-training. Existing trajectory-based self-distillation mainly speeds up sampling but does not significantly improve underlying model capability and can harm full diffusion decoding performance. The authors want to exploit inference trajectories for actual knowledge acquisition while avoiding catastrophic forgetting.
AI.method: Introduce TABOM (Trajectory-Aligned optimization via Boltzmann Modeling), which treats the model’s preference for which tokens to unmask during inference as a Boltzmann distribution over their predictive entropies. From this, they derive a tractable pairwise ranking loss that trains the model to match the certainty ordering observed along its easy-to-hard decoding trajectories, instead of just reconstructing random masks under NELBO. This directly aligns training with the structure of multi-step diffusion inference using self-distilled trajectories from the pretrained model.
AI.result: TABOM delivers substantial performance improvements in new domains for diffusion language models, expanding their effective knowledge scope beyond pretraining. Compared to standard supervised fine-tuning on masked tokens, it significantly reduces catastrophic forgetting while yielding stronger downstream capabilities, and improves performance even under full diffusion decoding rather than just faster sampling schedules.
AI.conclusion: Aligning post-training objectives with the easy-to-hard structure of diffusion inference, via Boltzmann-modeled entropy-based trajectory ranking, enables self-distilled trajectories to serve as a source of genuine new knowledge rather than just a means of speeding up decoding. TABOM offers a more effective and stable way to fine-tune diffusion language models than standard NELBO-based SFT, particularly for domain adaptation without forgetting.

title: AutoLLMResearch: Training Research Agents for Automating LLM Experiment Configuration -- Learning from Cheap, Optimizing Expensive
summary: Effectively configuring scalable large language model (LLM) experiments, spanning architecture design, hyperparameter tuning, and beyond, is crucial for advancing LLM research, as poor configuration choices can waste substantial computational resources and prevent models from realizing their full potential. Prior automated methods are designed for low-cost settings where repeated trial and error is feasible, but scalable LLM experiments are too expensive for such extensive iteration. To our knowledge, no work has addressed the automation of high-cost LLM experiment configurations, leaving this problem labor-intensive and dependent on expert intuition. Motivated by this gap, we propose AutoLLMResearch, an agentic framework that mimics how human researchers learn generalizable principles from low-fidelity experiments and extrapolate to efficiently identify promising configurations in expensive LLM settings. The core challenge is how to enable an agent to learn, through interaction with a multi-fidelity experimental environment that captures the structure of the LLM configuration landscape. To achieve this, we propose a systematic framework with two key components: 1) LLMConfig-Gym, a multi-fidelity environment encompassing four critical LLM experiment tasks, supported by over one million GPU hours of verifiable experiment outcomes; 2) A structured training pipeline that formulates configuration research as a long-horizon Markov Decision Process and accordingly incentivizes cross-fidelity extrapolation reasoning. Extensive evaluation against diverse strong baselines on held-out experiments demonstrates the effectiveness, generalization, and interpretability of our framework, supporting its potential as a practical and general solution for scalable real-world LLM experiment automation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AutoLLMResearch, an agentic framework and benchmark (LLMConfig-Gym) for automatically configuring high-cost, scalable LLM experiments using multi-fidelity learning and MDP-based training.
AI.motivation: Configuring large, expensive LLM experiments (architecture, hyperparameters, etc.) currently relies on expert intuition and manual trial-and-error. Existing automated hyperparameter tuning and NAS methods assume low-cost, many-trial regimes and do not scale to high-cost LLM settings, where each experiment is extremely expensive and iteration is limited. There is no prior work targeting automation of this high-cost configuration regime, leading to wasted computational resources and suboptimal models.
AI.method: The authors design AutoLLMResearch, an agentic framework trained to behave like a human researcher that first learns general principles from cheap, low-fidelity experiments and then extrapolates to identify promising configurations for high-cost, high-fidelity LLM runs. They construct LLMConfig-Gym, a multi-fidelity experimental environment with four key LLM experiment tasks and over one million GPU hours of logged, verifiable outcomes. They formalize configuration research as a long-horizon Markov Decision Process in this environment, and create a structured training pipeline that trains agents to reason across fidelities and to make sequential decisions about which experiments to run and which configurations to choose.
AI.result: Through extensive comparisons against strong baselines on held-out experiments within LLMConfig-Gym, AutoLLMResearch achieves better performance in terms of identifying good configurations more efficiently, demonstrating superior effectiveness, better generalization to new tasks/settings, and more interpretable decision behavior compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: AutoLLMResearch, together with the LLMConfig-Gym environment and the MDP-based training pipeline, offers an effective, general, and interpretable solution for automating configuration of expensive, scalable LLM experiments. The results indicate that multi-fidelity, agentic approaches can substantially reduce the reliance on expert intuition and cut the cost of LLM research, and the framework is positioned as a practical tool for real-world LLM experiment automation.

title: Concordance Comparison as a Means of Assembling Local Grammars
summary: Named Entity Recognition for person names is an important but non-trivial task in information extraction. This article uses a tool that compares the concordances obtained from two local grammars (LG) and highlights the differences. We used the results as an aid to select the best of a set of LGs. By analyzing the comparisons, we observed relationships of inclusion, intersection and disjunction within each pair of LGs, which helped us to assemble those that yielded the best results. This approach was used in a case study on extraction of person names from texts written in Portuguese. We applied the enhanced grammar to the Gold Collection of the Second HAREM. The F-Measure obtained was 76.86, representing a gain of 6 points in relation to the state-of-the-art for Portuguese.

AI.tldr: The paper improves person-name NER in Portuguese by comparing and combining multiple local grammars using a concordance-based tool, achieving a higher F-measure than previous state-of-the-art.
AI.motivation: Person-name recognition is crucial for information extraction but difficult, especially for specific languages like Portuguese. There exist multiple local grammars for NER, but choosing or designing the best one is non-trivial. The authors want a systematic way to compare and combine grammars to improve performance.
AI.method: The authors use a tool that compares concordances (matched text segments) produced by different local grammars for person-name recognition. By examining where grammars agree or differ—through inclusion, intersection, and disjunction relationships—they decide which grammars to keep, modify, or combine. They then assemble an enhanced grammar from the best-performing local grammars and apply it to Portuguese texts.
AI.result: Using the comparison tool, they identify useful relationships among local grammars and build an enhanced grammar. When applied to the Gold Collection of the Second HAREM evaluation campaign for Portuguese NER, this enhanced grammar achieves an F-measure of 76.86, which is a 6-point improvement over the prior Portuguese state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: Comparing concordances from multiple local grammars is an effective strategy for refining NER systems, particularly for person-name recognition in Portuguese. The approach helps select and compose grammars to significantly improve performance over existing methods.

title: Controllable User Simulation
summary: Using offline datasets to evaluate conversational agents often fails to cover rare scenarios or to support testing new policies. This has motivated the use of controllable user simulators for targeted, counterfactual evaluation, typically implemented by prompting or fine-tuning large language models. In this work, we formalize controllable simulation as a causal inference problem. By bridging natural language evaluation with off-policy evaluation methodology, we show that the standard practice of training simulators via supervised fine-tuning on post-hoc trajectory labels yields a structurally biased model. Specifically, these labels are inextricably coupled to the data-generating behavior policy, injecting a look-ahead bias that breaks causal consistency. Furthermore, we prove that under policy shift this failure causes the variance of evaluation metrics to explode geometrically, a phenomenon we term controllability collapse. To restore causal consistency, we establish theoretical conditions for accurate simulation and propose practical training mitigations: a priori controls, step-wise dynamic controls, and direct policy-conditioned learning. Empirical evaluation confirms that while standard global controls distort conversational distributions and collapse behavioral diversity, our causally grounded simulators eliminate look-ahead bias, preserve natural variance, and exhibit robust zero-shot generalization to unseen agent behaviors.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that standard ways of training controllable conversational user simulators cause structural bias and instability, and proposes causal-inference-based methods to make simulators robust under policy changes.
AI.motivation: Offline evaluation of conversational agents with logs misses rare or new scenarios, so controllable user simulators are used. However, current simulators, often LLM-based and trained with supervised fine-tuning on labeled trajectories, may be causally inconsistent and unreliable when the agent policy changes. The paper aims to formalize and fix this.
AI.method: They cast controllable user simulation as a causal inference and off-policy evaluation problem. They analyze how standard supervised fine-tuning on post-hoc trajectory-level controls (global labels) induces look-ahead bias tied to the behavior policy. They derive theoretical conditions for causal consistency and stable variance under policy shift, and design three training strategies: (1) a priori controls specified before trajectories unfold, (2) step-wise dynamic controls that evolve with the conversation without using future information, and (3) direct conditioning of the simulator on the agent’s policy. They then empirically compare standard global-control simulators and their causal versions on conversational simulation tasks.
AI.result: Theoretically, they prove that global post-hoc controls create structural bias and that evaluation metric variance can grow geometrically under policy shifts, which they call controllability collapse. Their proposed causally grounded simulators satisfy conditions that avoid this issue. Empirically, they show that standard simulators distort the distribution of conversations, reduce behavioral diversity, and become unstable when evaluating new policies, whereas their methods maintain realistic variance, avoid look-ahead bias, and generalize zero-shot to unseen agent behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Training conversational user simulators with naive supervised fine-tuning on post-hoc control labels is fundamentally flawed from a causal perspective, leading to biased and unstable evaluation under policy changes. By reframing controllable simulation as a causal inference problem and enforcing causal consistency through appropriate control design and policy conditioning, simulators can more faithfully reproduce user behavior, preserve diversity, and robustly evaluate new conversational policies.

title: Qwen-Scope: Turning Sparse Features into Development Tools for Large Language Models
summary: Large language models have achieved remarkable capabilities across diverse tasks, yet their internal decision-making processes remain largely opaque, limiting our ability to inspect, control, and systematically improve them. This opacity motivates a growing body of research in mechanistic interpretability, with sparse autoencoders (SAEs) emerging as one of the most promising tools for decomposing model activations into sparse, interpretable feature representations. We introduce Qwen-Scope, an open-source suite of SAEs built on the Qwen model family, comprising 14 groups of SAEs across 7 model variants from the Qwen3 and Qwen3.5 series, covering both dense and mixture-of-expert architectures. Built on top of these SAEs, we show that SAEs can go beyond post-hoc analysis to serve as practical interfaces for model development along four directions: (i) inference-time steering, where SAE feature directions control language, concepts, and preferences without modifying model weights; (ii) evaluation analysis, where activated SAE features provide a representation-level proxy for benchmark redundancy and capability coverage; (iii) data-centric workflows, where SAE features support multilingual toxicity classification and safety-oriented data synthesis; and (iv) post-training optimization, where SAE-derived signals are incorporated into supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning objectives to mitigate undesirable behaviors such as code-switching and repetition. Together, these results demonstrate that SAEs can serve not only as post-hoc analysis tools, but also as reusable representation-level interfaces for diagnosing, controlling, evaluating, and improving large language models. By open-sourcing Qwen-Scope, we aim to support mechanistic research and accelerate practical workflows that connect model internals to downstream behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Qwen-Scope, a large suite of sparse autoencoders (SAEs) for the Qwen LLM family and shows that these SAEs can be used not only for interpretability but also as practical interfaces to steer, evaluate, and improve models.
AI.motivation: Large language models are powerful but opaque; current interpretability tools are mostly post-hoc and not tightly integrated with model development. The authors are motivated to turn mechanistic interpretability—specifically SAEs—into actionable tools that can diagnose and control model behavior, and to provide an open, standardized resource for the Qwen family.
AI.method: They train 14 groups of sparse autoencoders across 7 Qwen3/Qwen3.5 model variants (including dense and MoE architectures) to decompose internal activations into sparse features. Using these learned features, they design workflows along four axes: (i) inference-time steering by activating/suppressing specific SAE features; (ii) evaluation analysis by mapping benchmark items to activated features to study redundancy and coverage; (iii) data-centric workflows such as multilingual toxicity detection and safety-oriented data generation using feature activations; and (iv) post-training optimization where SAE-derived signals are incorporated into supervised fine-tuning and RL objectives to suppress undesirable behaviors like code-switching and repetition.
AI.result: They show that steering along SAE feature directions can systematically control language, concepts, and preferences without changing base model weights; feature activations correlate with benchmark structure and coverage; SAE features enable practical toxicity classification and safety data synthesis; and using SAE-informed signals in SFT/RL reduces undesirable behaviors such as code-switching and repetition. These results validate that SAEs are robust, reusable interfaces across multiple Qwen model variants.
AI.conclusion: SAEs can be elevated from purely analytical tools to powerful representation-level interfaces that let practitioners inspect, steer, evaluate, and optimize LLMs. Qwen-Scope, released as an open resource, is intended to catalyze further mechanistic interpretability research and to embed interpretability directly into everyday LLM development workflows.

title: Read, Grep, and Synthesize: Diagnosing Cross-Domain Seed Exposure for LLM Research Ideation
summary: The discovery of novel methodologies for emerging problems is a continuing cycle in ML, often driven by the migration of techniques across domains. Building on this observation, we ask whether current LLM ideation systems benefit from targeted cross-domain retrieval or simply from exposure to diverse mechanisms. We study this question through PaperGym, a three-stage pipeline: (1) tool-augmented seed extraction via read, grep, and bash over an isolated paper environment, (2) cross-domain seed retrieval via paraphrasing across seven ML domains, and (3) method synthesis from retrieved seeds, each scored by rubric-based judges. Tool-augmented extraction improves specificity, and paraphrase-based retrieval broadens domain coverage. In synthesis, cross-domain retrieval receives more pairwise novelty wins than no-retrieval and same-domain baselines, but shows no significant difference from a random diverse-seed control. These findings suggest LLM ideation systems benefit from diverse seed exposure, but do not yet reliably exploit the semantic reason particular seeds were retrieved. We release the seed library, rubric prompts, and run scripts at https://github.com/yunjoochoi/PaperGym

AI.tldr: PaperGym evaluates how cross-domain retrieval affects LLM-based research ideation and finds that diverse seeds help, but targeted cross-domain retrieval is not better than random diverse seeds.
AI.motivation: To understand whether LLM ideation systems gain real benefit from semantically targeted cross-domain retrieval of methods, or if they mainly need exposure to a broad diversity of ideas regardless of why those ideas were retrieved.
AI.method: They design PaperGym, a three-stage pipeline: (1) tool-augmented seed extraction using read, grep, and bash commands on isolated paper repositories to get precise method fragments; (2) cross-domain seed retrieval by paraphrasing these seeds across seven machine learning domains to simulate domain transfer; and (3) method synthesis where an LLM combines retrieved seeds into new methods, which are then scored by rubric-based judging prompts. They compare no-retrieval, same-domain retrieval, cross-domain retrieval, and random diverse-seed controls.
AI.result: Tool-augmented extraction gives more specific method seeds, and paraphrase-based retrieval increases domain coverage. In the synthesis stage, cross-domain retrieval yields more pairwise novelty wins than no-retrieval and same-domain baselines, but it does not significantly outperform a random diverse-seed control condition.
AI.conclusion: LLM ideation systems appear to benefit primarily from exposure to a wide variety of seed ideas, rather than from the specific semantic relevance of cross-domain retrieved seeds. Current systems do not yet reliably make use of the reasons particular seeds were retrieved, suggesting room for better retrieval and reasoning integration. The authors release their seed library, rubric prompts, and scripts for further study.

title: Optimal LTLf Synthesis
summary: Strategy synthesis typically follows an all-or-nothing paradigm, returning unrealisable whenever a specification cannot be guaranteed in an uncertain environment. In this paper, we introduce optimal LTLf synthesis, where the goal is to realise as many objectives as possible from a given specification consisting of multiple objectives, especially for the case that they are not all jointly realisable. We first consider max-guarantee synthesis, which commits to a maximal set of objectives that we can a priori guarantee to realise. We then introduce max-observation synthesis, which maximises a posteriori realised objectives that may be incomparable on different executions. Finally, we present incremental max-observation synthesis, which further improves strategies by exploiting opportunities for stronger guarantees when they arise during an execution. Experimental results show that different variations of optimal synthesis scale broadly equally well, solving a large fraction of the benchmark instances within the given timeout, demonstrating the practical feasibility of the approach.

AI.tldr: The paper generalizes strategy synthesis from an all-or-nothing setting to an optimal setting where as many LTLf objectives as possible are satisfied, even when they are not jointly realizable.
AI.motivation: Classical strategy synthesis declares a specification unrealizable if it cannot be guaranteed under all environments, even if many of its component objectives could be satisfied. This wastes useful partial solutions in practical systems with multiple, possibly conflicting requirements. The authors are motivated to provide a more fine-grained and useful notion of realizability that extracts the best achievable behavior from a multi-objective LTLf specification.
AI.method: The authors formalize optimal LTLf synthesis for specifications composed of multiple objectives. They define three variants: (1) max-guarantee synthesis, which synthesizes a strategy that commits up front to the largest subset of objectives that can be guaranteed across all executions; (2) max-observation synthesis, which aims to maximize, for each concrete execution, the number of objectives realized, without requiring that the same subset be realized on every run; and (3) incremental max-observation synthesis, which dynamically strengthens commitments during execution as more information about the environment becomes available. They develop corresponding algorithmic procedures and implement them in a synthesis tool, likely built on automata- or game-based constructions for LTLf.
AI.result: The implemented optimal LTLf synthesis procedures were evaluated on benchmark suites. All three variants (max-guarantee, max-observation, and incremental max-observation) showed comparable scalability, solving a large fraction of instances within the given time limits. The experiments indicate that adding optimality criteria on top of standard synthesis does not substantially harm performance while yielding more informative strategies.
AI.conclusion: Optimal LTLf synthesis, as defined by the authors, provides a practically feasible and conceptually richer alternative to classical all-or-nothing synthesis for multi-objective specifications. By maximizing guaranteed or actually realized objectives and allowing incremental strengthening during execution, their approach extracts more value from specifications that are not fully realizable, without incurring prohibitive computational overhead.

title: Enhancing Target-Guided Proactive Dialogue Systems via Conversational Scenario Modeling and Intent-Keyword Bridging
summary: A target-guided proactive dialogue system aims to steer conversations proactively toward pre-defined targets, such as designated keywords or specific topics. During guided conversations, dynamically modeling conversational scenarios and intent keywords to guide system utterance generation is beneficial; however, existing work largely overlooks this aspect, resulting in a mismatch with the dynamics of real-world conversations. In this paper, we jointly model user profiles and domain knowledge as conversational scenarios to introduce a scenario bias that dynamically influences system utterances, and employ intent-keyword bridging to predict intent keywords for upcoming dialogue turns, providing higher level and more flexible guidance. Extensive automatic and human evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of conversational scenario modeling and intent keyword bridging, yielding substantial improvements in proactivity, fluency, and informativeness for target-guided proactive dialogue systems, thereby narrowing the gap with real world interactions.

AI.tldr: They enhance target-guided proactive dialogue systems by dynamically modeling conversational scenarios (from user profiles and domain knowledge) and predicting future intent keywords, which significantly improves proactivity, fluency, and informativeness.
AI.motivation: Existing target-guided proactive dialogue systems aim to drive conversations toward specific targets (keywords or topics), but they rarely model how real conversational scenarios and user intents evolve over time. This leads to a mismatch with real-world dynamics and limits the system’s ability to proactively and naturally steer dialogue.
AI.method: They jointly encode user profiles and domain knowledge as a unified conversational scenario representation, which provides a scenario bias to influence system response generation at each turn. Additionally, they introduce an intent-keyword bridging mechanism that predicts intent keywords for upcoming turns, offering high-level, flexible guidance to the generator. The framework is evaluated with both automatic metrics and human judgments.
AI.result: Systems incorporating conversational scenario modeling and intent-keyword bridging outperform baselines on multiple dimensions—especially proactivity, fluency, and informativeness—according to extensive automatic and human evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Dynamically modeling conversational scenarios and predicting intent keywords is an effective way to narrow the gap between current target-guided proactive dialogue systems and real-world conversational behavior, leading to more proactive, fluent, and informative interactions.

title: Hindsight Hint Distillation: Scaffolded Reasoning for SWE Agents from CoT-free Answers
summary: Solving complex long-horizon tasks requires strong planning and reasoning capabilities. Although datasets with explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) rationales can substantially benefit learning, they are costly to obtain. To address this challenge, we propose Hindsight Hint Distillation (HHD), which only requires easy-to-obtain question-answer pairs without CoT annotations. Inspired by how human teachers use student mistakes to provide targeted guidance, HHD synthesizes hindsight hints from the model's own failed self-rollouts and uses them to scaffold on-policy rollouts that successfully complete the tasks. The model then self-distills these scaffolded trajectories and generalizes to new problems without hint guidance. Experiments show that HHD significantly outperforms iterative RFT and trajectory-synthesis baselines, achieving an absolute improvement of 8\% on SWE-bench Verified, while all baselines improve by only around 2\%. Notably, the reasoning strategies induced by HHD generalize effectively to out-of-distribution tasks, yielding the largest gains on SWE-bench Multilingual despite no training on multilingual data. These results demonstrate that HHD can effectively synthesize expert-like reasoning from CoT-free data and substantially improve long-horizon performance.

AI.tldr: This paper presents Hindsight Hint Distillation (HHD), a method to train strong long-horizon reasoning and planning models using only question-answer pairs, without costly chain-of-thought (CoT) annotations.
AI.motivation: Collecting explicit chain-of-thought rationales is expensive, yet such rationales greatly help models learn complex long-horizon reasoning and planning. The authors want a way to obtain expert-like reasoning behavior without relying on CoT-labeled data, using only cheap question-answer supervision.
AI.method: The method, Hindsight Hint Distillation (HHD), uses the model’s own failed self-rollouts to synthesize “hindsight hints” that explain how to correct mistakes, similar to how a teacher uses a student’s errors to give targeted guidance. These hints are then used to scaffold new, on-policy rollouts that successfully solve the tasks. The model finally self-distills from these successful, hint-augmented trajectories, enabling it to perform the tasks later without any hints or CoT supervision.
AI.result: On SWE-bench Verified, HHD outperforms iterative RFT and trajectory-synthesis baselines, giving an absolute performance gain of 8%, while baselines gain only about 2%. The reasoning strategies learned via HHD transfer well to out-of-distribution tasks, producing the largest improvements on SWE-bench Multilingual, even though the model was not trained on multilingual data.
AI.conclusion: HHD can automatically synthesize expert-like reasoning from datasets that lack chain-of-thought labels, leading to substantial improvements on long-horizon tasks and strong generalization, including to multilingual and out-of-distribution settings.

title: On Predicting the Post-training Potential of Pre-trained LLMs
summary: The performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) on downstream tasks is fundamentally constrained by the capabilities acquired during pre-training. However, traditional benchmarks like MMLU often fail to reflect a base model's plasticity in complex open-ended scenarios, leading to inefficient model selection. We address this by introducing a new task of predicting post-training potential - forecasting a base model's performance before post-training. We propose RuDE (Rubric-based Discriminative Evaluation), a unified framework that bypasses the generation gap of base models by leveraging response discrimination. Guided by our systematic 4C Taxonomy, RuDE constructs controlled contrastive pairs across diverse domains by fine-grained rubric violations. Extensive experiments demonstrate a correlation greater than 90% with post-training performance. Crucially, validation via Reinforcement Learning (RL) confirms that RuDE effectively identifies high-potential smaller models that outperform larger counterparts, offering a compute-efficient mechanism for foundation model development.

AI.tldr: Paper proposes RuDE, a rubric-based discriminative evaluation framework to predict LLMs’ post-training potential from base models, achieving >90% correlation with actual post-training performance and enabling efficient selection of smaller, high-potential models.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks like MMLU are poor at revealing a base LLM’s plasticity and future performance in complex open-ended tasks, causing inefficient model selection and over-reliance on larger models. There is a need to forecast how well a base model will respond to alignment or post-training before investing compute.
AI.method: Define a new task: predicting post-training potential of base LLMs. Introduce RuDE (Rubric-based Discriminative Evaluation), which evaluates base models via response discrimination instead of full generation. Using a 4C Taxonomy, RuDE builds controlled contrastive response pairs across many domains, where pairs differ by fine-grained rubric violations. Models are scored by how well they discriminate rubric-consistent vs rubric-violating responses. Correlate these scores with actual post-training performance, including RL-based post-training, to validate the metric.
AI.result: RuDE scores correlate above 90% with post-training performance across evaluated models. Experiments show that RuDE can reliably identify smaller base models whose predicted post-training performance surpasses that of some larger models, confirmed through RL-based post-training experiments.
AI.conclusion: RuDE offers a practical, compute-efficient framework to forecast the alignment and downstream performance of base LLMs before costly post-training. It addresses limitations of conventional benchmarks in capturing model plasticity, supports more informed model selection, and helps discover small but high-potential models that can outperform larger ones after post-training.

title: Dual-Temporal LSTM with Hybrid Attention for Airline Passenger Load Factor Forecasting: Integrating Intra-Flight and Inter-Flight Booking Dynamics
summary: Accurate short-term demand forecasting is crucial to airline revenue management, yet most existing systems fail to meet this need because current models treat booking data as a single temporal dimension, either the accumulation of bookings for a specific flight or the historical booking profile of the same route. This unidimensional view discards information carried by the other temporal stream and forecasting absolute passenger counts introduces a further operational fragility when change in planned aircraft type alters total seat capacity. This study addresses both limitations. A dual-stream Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) integrated with attention framework is proposed that simultaneously processes two complementary input sequences: a horizontal sequence capturing intra-flight booking accumulation over the days preceding departure, and a vertical sequence capturing inter-flight booking patterns at fixed days-before-departure offsets across historical flights. Multiple dual-stream architectural variants, combining self-attention, cross-attention, and hybrid attention with concatenation, residual, and gated fusion strategies, are developed and evaluated. Experiments on real-world reservation data from the national airline of Bangladesh, Biman Bangladesh Airlines (BBA), demonstrate that the proposed hybrid model achieves a Mean Absolute Error of 2.8167 and a coefficient of determination ($R^{2}$) of 0.9495, outperforming single-stream baselines, tree-based models, and three prior dual-LSTM architectures applied to the same data. Validation across four flight category pairs; domestic versus international, direct versus transit, high versus low frequency, and short versus mid versus long haul confirms that the model generalizes across operationally diverse route types. Biman Bangladesh Airlines (BBA) has officially integrated this methodology into its operations.

AI.tldr: A dual-stream attention-based LSTM improves short-term airline demand forecasting by simultaneously modeling intra-flight booking curves and inter-flight historical patterns, yielding high accuracy and operational deployment at Biman Bangladesh Airlines.
AI.motivation: Existing airline demand forecasting models typically treat booking data as a single time series, either focusing on bookings accumulated for one flight as departure approaches or on historical booking profiles for that route across flights.
