This unidimensional treatment discards information from the complementary temporal dimension and leads to less accurate forecasts. Moreover, forecasting absolute passenger counts can be fragile when aircraft type or seat capacity changes, creating operational issues. The paper aims to overcome these limitations to support more reliable revenue management and capacity decisions.
AI.method: The authors propose a dual-stream LSTM architecture integrated with attention mechanisms. One stream (horizontal) models intra-flight booking accumulation over days before departure; the other (vertical) models inter-flight booking patterns at fixed days-before-departure across historical flights. They design multiple dual-stream variants that explore self-attention, cross-attention, and hybrid attention, combined with different fusion strategies such as concatenation, residual connections, and gated fusion. These architectures are trained and evaluated on real reservation data from Biman Bangladesh Airlines.
AI.result: On real-world data from Biman Bangladesh Airlines, the preferred hybrid dual-stream attention LSTM achieves a Mean Absolute Error of 2.8167 and an R² of 0.9495, outperforming single-stream neural models, tree-based methods, and three prior dual-LSTM architectures previously used on the same dataset. The model’s performance is validated across various flight categories (domestic vs. international, direct vs. transit, high vs. low frequency, and short vs. mid vs. long haul), demonstrating robustness and generalization across diverse operational contexts.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling both intra-flight booking trajectories and inter-flight historical booking patterns via a dual-stream attention-based LSTM significantly improves short-term airline demand forecasting. The approach is robust across route types and offers practical value, as evidenced by its official adoption in Biman Bangladesh Airlines’ operations. This suggests that richer temporal representations and attention-based fusion are promising directions for demand forecasting and revenue management in airlines.

title: Towards Visually-Guided Movie Subtitle Translation for Indic Languages
summary: Movie subtitle translation is inherently multimodal, yet text-only systems often miss visual cues needed to convey emotion, action, and social nuance, especially for low-resource Indic languages (English to Hindi, Bengali, Telugu, Tamil and Kannada). We present a case study on five full-length films and compare two lightweight visual grounding strategies: structured attribute summaries from a 5-minute sliding window and free-text summaries of inter-subtitle visual gaps. Our analysis shows that temporal misalignment between subtitles and frames is a major obstacle in long-form video, often rendering indiscriminate visual grounding ineffective. However, oracle selective grounding, which replaces only the lowest-quality 20-30\% of baseline segments with visual-enhanced outputs, consistently improves COMET over the text-only baseline while requiring far less visual processing. Among the two approaches, coarse attribute-based visual context summarization is more robust, capturing scene-level emotion and contextual subtle cues that text alone often misses

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to use video information to improve movie subtitle translation into several Indic languages and finds that selectively adding coarse visual context only to weak subtitle segments gives better quality with low visual cost.
AI.motivation: Text-only subtitle translation systems miss important information that is visible on screen (emotions, actions, social cues), and this problem is worse for low-resource Indic languages and for long movies where subtitles and frames are often misaligned. The authors want to understand how to exploit visual information efficiently instead of processing all frames indiscriminately.
AI.method: They perform a case study on five full-length movies translating English subtitles into Hindi, Bengali, Telugu, Tamil, and Kannada. They compare two lightweight visual grounding strategies: (1) structured attribute summaries derived from a 5-minute sliding window over video frames, and (2) free-text summaries describing visual content in gaps between subtitles. They also analyze temporal alignment issues and test an oracle selective grounding approach where only the lowest-quality 20–30% of baseline subtitle segments are replaced with visually enhanced translations.
AI.result: They find that temporal misalignment between subtitles and video frames is a major challenge in long-form movie translation, making naive, indiscriminate visual grounding largely ineffective. In contrast, an oracle selective grounding strategy that focuses on the weakest segments and augments them with visual information reliably boosts COMET scores compared to a text-only baseline while using much less visual computation. Coarse attribute-based visual summaries perform better and more robustly than free-text summaries, especially in capturing scene-level emotions and subtle contextual cues.
AI.conclusion: Visual information can help subtitle translation for Indic languages, but only when used selectively and with robust summarization. Oracle selective grounding of low-quality segments using coarse attribute-based visual summaries improves translation quality with limited visual processing, while naive full grounding suffers from subtitle–frame misalignment. Scene-level, structured visual context is particularly useful for conveying emotions and subtle cues missing from text alone.

title: Native Explainability for Bayesian Confidence Propagation Neural Networks: A Framework for Trusted Brain-Like AI
summary: The EU Artificial Intelligence Act (Regulation 2024/1689), fully applicable to high-risk systems from August 2026, creates urgent demand for AI architectures that are simultaneously trustworthy, transparent, and feasible to deploy on resource-constrained edge devices. Brain-like neural networks built on the Bayesian Confidence Propagation Neural Network (BCPNN) formalism have re-emerged as a credible alternative to backpropagation-driven deep learning. They deliver state-of-the-art unsupervised representation learning, neuromorphic-friendly sparsity, and existing FPGA implementations that target edge deployment. Despite this momentum, no systematic framework exists for explaining BCPNN decisions -- a gap the present paper fills. We argue that BCPNN is, in the sense of Rudin's interpretable-by-design agenda, an inherently transparent model whose architectural primitives map directly onto established explainable-AI (XAI) families. We make four contributions. First, we propose the first XAI taxonomy for BCPNN. It maps weights, biases, hypercolumn posteriors, structural-plasticity usage scores, attractor dynamics, and input-reconstruction populations onto attribution, prototype, concept, counterfactual, and mechanistic explanation modalities. Second, we introduce sixteen architecture-level explanation primitives (P1--P16), several without analogue in standard ANNs. We provide closed-form algorithms for computing each from quantities the model already maintains. Third, we introduce five design-time Configuration-as-Explanation primitives (Config-P1 to Config-P5) that treat BCPNN hyperparameter choices as an auditable pre-deployment explanation artifact. Fourth, we sketch a roadmap for integration into industrial IoT deployments and discuss EU AI Act alignment, edge feasibility, and Industry 5.0 implications.

AI.tldr: Proposes a systematic explainability framework for BCPNN brain-like neural networks, aligning them with EU AI Act needs and edge/IoT deployment.
AI.motivation: The EU AI Act requires high-risk AI systems to be trustworthy, transparent, and auditable, especially when deployed on constrained edge/IoT hardware. BCPNN-based neural networks are promising for unsupervised, neuromorphic, and FPGA-friendly applications but lack a structured, principled way to explain their decisions. This gap limits their regulatory compliance and industrial adoption.
AI.method: The paper conceptually analyzes BCPNN architecture and its Bayesian components, then constructs an XAI taxonomy that maps core internal quantities (weights, biases, posteriors, structural-plasticity scores, dynamics, and reconstruction units) to well-known explanation families such as attribution, prototype, concept, counterfactual, and mechanistic explanations. It formalizes sixteen architecture-level explanation primitives with closed-form computation procedures using existing model statistics, and defines five configuration-time primitives that treat hyperparameter settings and design decisions as part of the explanation record. Finally, it outlines how these primitives can integrate into industrial IoT workflows under the EU AI Act.
AI.result: Produces (1) the first dedicated XAI taxonomy tailored to BCPNN, (2) sixteen operational, model-internal explanation primitives (P1–P16) with explicit algorithms, (3) five configuration-as-explanation primitives (Config-P1–Config-P5) for design-time documentation, and (4) an application roadmap showing how these elements support regulatory alignment, edge feasibility, and Industry 5.0 use cases.
AI.conclusion: BCPNN architectures can be viewed as inherently transparent, interpretable-by-design models whose structural components naturally realize standard XAI modalities. By formalizing explanation primitives and configuration-time artifacts, the paper claims that BCPNN systems can meet emerging transparency and auditability requirements, particularly under the EU AI Act, while remaining practical for resource-constrained industrial IoT and neuromorphic deployments.

title: Learning Agentic Policy from Action Guidance
summary: Agentic reinforcement learning (RL) for Large Language Models (LLMs) critically depends on the exploration capability of the base policy, as training signals emerge only within its in-capability region. For tasks where the base policy cannot reach reward states, additional training or external guidance is needed to recover effective learning signals. Rather than relying on costly iterative supervised fine tuning (SFT), we exploit the abundant action data generated in everyday human interactions. We propose \textsc{ActGuide-RL}, which injects action data as plan-style reference guidance, enabling the agentic policy to overcome reachability barriers to reward states. Guided and unguided rollouts are then jointly optimized via mixed-policy training, internalizing the exploration gains back into the unguided policy. Motivated by a theoretical and empirical analysis of the benefit-risk trade-off, we adopt a minimal intervention principle that invokes guidance only as an adaptive fallback, matching task difficulty while minimizing off-policy risk. On search-agent benchmarks, \textsc{ActGuide-RL} substantially improves over zero RL (+10.7 pp on GAIA and +19 pp on XBench with Qwen3-4B), and performs on par with the SFT+RL pipeline without any cold start. This suggests a new paradigm for agentic RL that reduces the reliance on heavy SFT data by using scalable action guidance instead.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ActGuide-RL, a reinforcement learning framework for LLM-based agents that uses human action traces as lightweight plan-style guidance to overcome exploration and reachability limits, achieving SFT+RL-level performance without costly supervised fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Agentic RL for LLMs often fails when the base policy cannot naturally explore trajectories that reach rewarding states; in such cases standard RL has no useful training signals, and current practice relies on expensive supervised fine-tuning with curated data to bootstrap capabilities. The authors want a more scalable, data-efficient way to help LLM agents explore difficult tasks by leveraging readily available human interaction data, while avoiding heavy dependence on SFT and mitigating off-policy risks.
AI.method: They introduce ActGuide-RL, which injects human action data as plan-style reference guidance into the agent’s rollouts. The guidance is used adaptively as a fallback when the task is difficult, following a minimal intervention principle to reduce off-policy risk. The framework trains on a mixture of guided and unguided rollouts through a mixed-policy RL objective so that the unguided policy internalizes exploration benefits gained from the guided trajectories. They also conduct theoretical and empirical analysis of the benefit–risk trade-off of injecting guidance and design their intervention strategy accordingly.
AI.result: On search-agent benchmarks such as GAIA and XBench using Qwen3-4B as the base model, ActGuide-RL significantly outperforms a zero-RL baseline, with improvements of +10.7 percentage points on GAIA and +19 percentage points on XBench. Its performance matches that of a stronger SFT+RL pipeline, but without requiring a cold-start SFT stage.
AI.conclusion: ActGuide-RL demonstrates that lightweight, scalable action guidance from human-generated plans can effectively replace heavy supervised fine-tuning as a bootstrapping mechanism for agentic RL with LLMs. By adaptively injecting guidance only when needed and jointly training on guided and unguided rollouts, the method overcomes exploration barriers while controlling off-policy risks, suggesting a more scalable paradigm for training capable LLM agents.

title: GAR: Carbon-Aware Routing for LLM Inference via Constrained Optimization
summary: The growing deployment of large language models (LLMs) makes per-request routing essential for balancing response quality and computational cost across heterogeneous model pools. Current routing methods rarely consider sustainable energy use and CO2 emissions as optimization objectives, despite grid carbon intensity varying by time and region, and models differing significantly in energy consumption. To address this gap, we introduce Green-Aware Routing (GAR), a constrained multi-objective optimization framework that minimizes per-request CO2 emissions subject to explicit accuracy floors and p95-latency service-level objectives (SLOs). GAR employs adaptive constraint optimization through per-dataset floor tuning and incorporates lightweight estimators for correctness, tail latency, and carbon emissions, enabling real-time routing decisions without additional inference passes. We present GAR-PD, a practical online primal-dual routing algorithm for rolling carbon budgets, alongside heuristic variants that achieve high feasibility coverage while limiting accuracy degradation. Comprehensive experiments across standard NLP benchmarks with heterogeneous LLM pools (7B-70B) demonstrate that GAR achieves substantial carbon reductions while maintaining competitive accuracy and p95 latency guarantees, providing a practical, theoretically grounded approach to sustainable LLM inference.

AI.tldr: Proposes Green-Aware Routing (GAR), a framework that routes LLM queries to minimize CO2 emissions while meeting accuracy and latency constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM routing focuses on quality, cost, or latency, but largely ignores sustainability, even though energy use and grid carbon intensity vary across models, time, and region. There is a need to systematically reduce carbon emissions from LLM inference without sacrificing accuracy or service-level guarantees.
AI.method: Formulate routing as a constrained multi-objective optimization that minimizes per-request CO2 emissions subject to explicit accuracy and p95-latency SLO constraints. Introduce Green-Aware Routing (GAR) with adaptive constraint optimization via per-dataset accuracy floor tuning. Use lightweight estimators for correctness, tail latency, and carbon emissions to enable real-time routing without extra inference. Develop GAR-PD, an online primal-dual algorithm to handle rolling carbon budgets, plus heuristic routing variants.
AI.result: On diverse NLP benchmarks and heterogeneous LLM pools ranging from 7B to 70B parameters, GAR-based routing strategies significantly reduce carbon emissions compared to baselines, while preserving competitive accuracy and satisfying p95-latency SLOs. Heuristic variants maintain high feasibility (few SLO violations) with only minor accuracy losses.
AI.conclusion: Green-Aware Routing provides a practical, theoretically grounded way to integrate carbon awareness into LLM request routing. By explicitly optimizing for emissions under accuracy and latency constraints and using efficient estimators, GAR enables substantial, real-time carbon reductions in LLM inference without materially degrading user-facing performance.

title: SAGE: Scalable Automated Robustness Augmentation for LLM Knowledge Evaluation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance on standard knowledge evaluation benchmarks, yet recent work shows that their knowledge capabilities remain brittle under question variants that test the same knowledge in different forms. Robustness augmentation of existing knowledge evaluation benchmarks is therefore necessary, but current LLM-assisted generate-then-verify pipelines are costly and difficult to scale due to low-yield variant generation and unreliable variant verification. We propose SAGE (Scalable Automated Generation of Robustness BEnchmarks), a framework for scalable robustness augmentation of knowledge evaluation benchmarks using fine-tuned smaller models. SAGE consists of VariantQual, a rubric-based verifier trained on human-labeled seed data, and VariantGen, a variant generator initialized with supervised fine-tuning and further optimized with reinforcement learning using VariantQual as the reward model. Experiments on HellaSwag show that SAGE constructs a large-scale robustness-augmented benchmark with quality comparable to the human-annotated HellaSwag-Pro at substantially lower cost, while the fine-tuned models further generalize to MMLU without benchmark-specific fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SAGE, a framework that uses fine-tuned smaller models to automatically generate and verify robustness-augmented variants of knowledge evaluation benchmarks, achieving human-comparable quality at lower cost and with cross-benchmark generalization.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs perform well on standard knowledge benchmarks, they are brittle when questions are rephrased or varied while testing the same underlying knowledge. Existing approaches to augment benchmarks with robust variants rely heavily on large LLMs in a generate-then-verify pipeline, which is expensive, low-yield (many bad variants), and uses unreliable verification. There is a need for a scalable, cost-effective, and reliable way to build robustness-augmented benchmarks.
AI.method: The authors propose SAGE, a two-component framework using smaller fine-tuned models. VariantQual is a rubric-based verifier trained on a human-labeled seed dataset to judge the quality of candidate variants. VariantGen is a variant generator first trained with supervised fine-tuning and then improved via reinforcement learning, where VariantQual serves as the reward model. This pipeline generates and filters robustness-testing variants for existing benchmarks, without depending on large LLMs for each generation step.
AI.result: On the HellaSwag benchmark, SAGE is able to produce a large-scale robustness-augmented benchmark whose quality is comparable to the human-created HellaSwag-Pro, but at substantially lower annotation and computation cost. The models trained within SAGE also transfer to another benchmark, MMLU, where they can support robustness augmentation without task-specific re-training, indicating generalization of the learned generation and verification behaviors.
AI.conclusion: SAGE demonstrates that fine-tuned smaller models, organized as a generator-verifier pair and optimized with human-labeled seeds plus reinforcement learning, can automatically construct high-quality robustness-augmented benchmarks. This approach reduces cost and improves scalability compared to LLM-assisted generation pipelines, while maintaining quality and showing cross-benchmark generalization, suggesting a practical path to more robust evaluation of LLM knowledge.

title: CuSearch: Curriculum Rollout Sampling via Search Depth for Agentic RAG
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a promising paradigm for training agentic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems from outcome-only supervision. Most existing methods optimize policies from uniformly sampled rollouts, implicitly treating all trajectories as equally informative. However, trajectories differ substantially in search depth and are therefore not equally informative: deeper-search trajectories contain more retrieval decision points and provide denser direct supervision for the retrieval sub-policy. Moreover, this heterogeneity grows over training as the within-batch depth distribution shifts toward higher values, yet uniform rollout sampling remains blind to this shift. To address this, we propose CuSearch, a curriculum rollout sampling framework built on Search-Depth Greedy Allocation (SDGA), a batch-level operator that reallocates a fixed update budget toward deeper-search trajectories. SDGA-Auto always targets the deepest available trajectories in the current batch, yielding an implicit training-aligned curriculum as the depth distribution shifts upward. SDGA-Phase explicitly advances the curriculum threshold as deeper trajectories become sufficiently abundant. Experiments across model types and retrieval frameworks show that CuSearch consistently improves performance, achieving up to 11.8 exact-match points over standard GRPO on ZeroSearch. These results establish per-trajectory search depth as a reliable, annotation-free proxy for retrieval supervision density in RLVR-based agentic RAG training. The code is available at https://github.com/MrToser/CuSearch.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CuSearch, a curriculum rollout sampling framework for RL with Verifiable Rewards in agentic RAG, which reallocates training updates toward deeper-search trajectories, using search depth as a proxy for supervision density and significantly improving performance over baseline methods.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR approaches for training agentic RAG systems treat all sampled trajectories as equally informative, despite large variation in search depth. Deeper trajectories offer more retrieval decisions and thus richer supervision for the retrieval sub-policy, and this heterogeneity increases during training. Uniform rollout sampling ignores this, potentially wasting update budget on less informative shallow trajectories. The authors aim to exploit this structure to create a more efficient and effective training curriculum without additional annotations.
AI.method: The authors propose CuSearch, a curriculum rollout sampling framework based on Search-Depth Greedy Allocation (SDGA). SDGA operates at the batch level and reallocates a fixed update budget toward deeper-search trajectories. Two variants are presented: (1) SDGA-Auto, which always prioritizes the deepest available trajectories within each batch, thereby inducing an implicit curriculum that naturally follows the evolving depth distribution during training; and (2) SDGA-Phase, which explicitly manages a curriculum threshold on search depth and advances it when deeper trajectories become sufficiently common. Both variants leverage per-trajectory search depth as an annotation-free signal of supervision density for the retrieval sub-policy.
AI.result: Across multiple model families and retrieval frameworks, CuSearch yields consistent performance gains compared to standard GRPO training in RLVR-based agentic RAG. On the ZeroSearch benchmark, the method attains improvements of up to 11.8 exact-match points, indicating substantially better answer quality and more effective use of retrieval during reasoning. The results validate that allocating more updates to deeper-search trajectories is beneficial and that search depth is a meaningful signal for training allocation.
AI.conclusion: CuSearch demonstrates that per-trajectory search depth can serve as a practical, annotation-free proxy for retrieval supervision density in RLVR training for agentic RAG systems. By greedily reallocating update budget toward deeper trajectories, either implicitly (SDGA-Auto) or via explicit curriculum thresholds (SDGA-Phase), the method establishes a training curriculum aligned with the evolving trajectory depth distribution. This leads to notable performance gains over standard RL baselines, suggesting that curriculum-aware rollout sampling is a valuable component for future RLVR-based RAG training pipelines.

title: Caraman at SemEval-2026 Task 8: Three-Stage Multi-Turn Retrieval with Query Rewriting, Hybrid Search, and Cross-Encoder Reranking
summary: We describe our system for SemEval-2026 Task 8 (MTRAGEval), participating in Task A (Retrieval) across four English-language domains. Our approach employs a three-stage pipeline: (1) query rewriting via a LoRA-fine-tuned Qwen 2.5 7B model that transforms context-dependent follow-up questions into standalone queries, (2) hybrid BM25 and dense retrieval combined through Reciprocal Rank Fusion, and (3) cross-encoder reranking with BGE-reranker-v2-m3. On the official test set, the system achieves nDCG@5 of 0.531, ranking 8th out of 38 participating systems and 10.7% above the organizer baseline. Development comparisons reveal that domain-specific temperature tuning for query generation, where technical domains benefit from deterministic decoding and general domains from controlled randomness, provides consistent gains, while more complex strategies such as domain-aware prompting and multi-query expansion degrade performance.

AI.tldr: Three-stage retrieval pipeline with query rewriting, hybrid retrieval, and reranking for SemEval-2026 Task 8, achieving strong nDCG@5 and insights on decoding temperature.
AI.motivation: To build a strong multi-domain retrieval system for the MTRAGEval shared task and to study how query generation strategies and decoding settings affect retrieval performance across technical and general domains.
AI.method: Use a LoRA-fine-tuned Qwen 2.5 7B model for query rewriting of conversational queries, combine BM25 and dense retrieval via Reciprocal Rank Fusion, and apply a BGE cross-encoder for reranking; systematically compare decoding temperatures, domain-aware prompting, and multi-query expansion on development data.
AI.result: The system achieves nDCG@5 of 0.531 on the official test set, placing 8th of 38 (10.7% above baseline); experiments show domain-specific temperature tuning improves retrieval, whereas domain-aware prompting and multi-query expansion hurt performance.
AI.conclusion: A relatively simple three-stage pipeline with careful temperature tuning for query rewriting yields competitive retrieval performance in MTRAGEval, and more complex query generation strategies are not necessarily beneficial.

title: Nice Fold or Hero Call: Learning Budget-Efficient Thinking for Adaptive Reasoning
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) improve problem solving through extended reasoning, but often misallocate test-time compute. Existing efficiency methods reduce cost by compressing reasoning traces or conditioning budget on perceived difficulty, yet largely overlook solvability. As a result, they may spend large budgets on queries beyond the model's capability while compressing hard-but-solvable queries that require deeper reasoning. In this work, we formulate adaptive reasoning as a computational investment under uncertainty, where budget should follow the expected return of reasoning rather than perceived difficulty alone. To instantiate this principle, we propose Budget-Efficient Thinking (BET), a two-stage framework that combines behavioral cold-start with GRPO under an investment-cost-aware reward. By aligning solve-or-fold decisions with rollout-derived solvability, BET learns three behaviors: (1) short solve, answering easy queries concisely; (2) nice fold, abstaining early when continued reasoning has near-zero expected return; and (3) hero call, preserving sufficient compute for hard-but-solvable queries. Across seven benchmarks and three base models, BET reduces reasoning tokens by ~55% on average while achieving overall performance improvements, and transfers zero-shot from mathematical reasoning to scientific QA and logical reasoning with comparable efficiency gains.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Budget-Efficient Thinking (BET), a framework that allocates test-time reasoning compute based on expected solvability, achieving ~55% fewer reasoning tokens with improved accuracy.
AI.motivation: Large reasoning models often waste test-time compute by over-reasoning on unsolvable queries and under-reasoning on hard-but-solvable ones. Existing efficiency methods focus on difficulty or trace compression, not on whether extra reasoning is likely to change the outcome. The authors aim to create a principled way to adaptively invest reasoning budget according to the expected benefit of additional computation.
AI.method: They formalize adaptive reasoning as a computational investment problem under uncertainty, where reasoning budget should track expected return (likelihood that more tokens will enable a correct solution). They propose BET, a two-stage framework: (1) a behavioral cold-start stage to initialize policies and (2) GRPO-based training with an investment-cost-aware reward that explicitly prices the cost of additional reasoning tokens. BET trains the model to make solve-or-fold decisions aligned with rollout-estimated solvability, learning when to answer quickly, when to invest more compute, and when to abstain early.
AI.result: On seven benchmarks and three different base models, BET reduces the number of reasoning tokens by around 55% on average while not only maintaining but improving overall performance compared to baselines that lack such investment-aware control.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive reasoning should be governed by expected return on computation, not just perceived difficulty. BET operationalizes this through an investment-cost-aware training framework that teaches models three behaviors—short solve, nice fold, and hero call—leading to large compute savings and accuracy gains. The approach generalizes zero-shot from math to scientific QA and logical reasoning, suggesting it captures a transferable principle for budget-efficient reasoning.

title: SkillGraph: Skill-Augmented Reinforcement Learning for Agents via Evolving Skill Graphs
summary: Skill libraries enable large language model agents to reuse experience from past interactions, but most existing libraries store skills as isolated entries and retrieve them only by semantic similarity. This leads to two key challenges for compositional tasks. Firstly, an agent must identify not only relevant skills but also how they depend on and build upon each other. Secondly, it also makes library maintenance difficult, since the system lacks structural cues for deciding when skills should be merged, split, or removed. We propose SKILLGRAPH, a framework that represents reusable skills as nodes in a directed graph, with typed edges encoding prerequisite, enhancement, and co-occurrence relations. Given a new task, SKILLGRAPH retrieves not just individual skills, but an ordered skill subgraph that can guide multi-step decision making. The graph is continuously updated from agent trajectories and reinforcement learning feedback, allowing both the skill library and the agent policy to improve together. Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and seven search-augmented QA tasks show that SKILLGRAPH achieves state-of-the-art performance against memory-augmented RL methods, with especially large gains on complex tasks that require composing multiple skills.

AI.tldr: Introduces SKILLGRAPH, a graph-structured skill library for LLM agents that captures relations between skills and improves compositional decision-making.
AI.motivation: Existing skill libraries for LLM agents store skills as isolated items and retrieve them only via semantic similarity. This is inadequate for compositional tasks because agents must understand how skills depend on and build on each other, and the lack of structure makes it hard to maintain or evolve the library (e.g., when to merge, split, or delete skills).
AI.method: Represent each reusable skill as a node in a directed graph, with typed edges denoting prerequisite, enhancement, and co-occurrence relationships. For a new task, retrieve an ordered skill subgraph instead of independent skills, and use it to guide multi-step decisions. Continuously update both the graph and the agent policy from interaction trajectories and reinforcement learning feedback, so the structure and skills co-evolve.
AI.result: On benchmarks including ALFWorld, WebShop, and seven search-augmented QA tasks, the proposed SKILLGRAPH framework outperforms memory-augmented reinforcement learning baselines, with particularly strong gains on complex tasks that require composing multiple skills in sequence.
AI.conclusion: Encoding skills in a relational graph and retrieving structured subgraphs enables better compositional reasoning and decision-making for LLM agents than flat, similarity-based skill memories. The approach not only improves performance but also provides a principled way to maintain and refine the skill library over time through continual learning.

title: Can LLM Agents Respond to Disasters? Benchmarking Heterogeneous Geospatial Reasoning in Emergency Operations
comment: DORA stress-tests LLM agents on real-world disaster operations that demand comprehensive orchestration of 108 specialized tools over heterogeneous geospatial data
summary: Operational disaster response goes beyond damage assessment, requiring responders to integrate multi-sensor signals, reason over road networks, populations and key facilities, plan evacuations, and produce actionable reports. However, prior work largely isolates remote-sensing perception or evaluates generic tool use, leaving the end-to-end workflows of emergency operations underexplored. In this paper, we introduce Disaster Operational Response Agent benchmark (DORA), the first agentic benchmark for end-to-end disaster response: 515 expert-authored tasks across 45 real-world disaster events spanning 10 types, paired with expert-verified, replayable gold trajectories totaling 3,500 tool-call steps. Tasks span five dimensions that cover the operational disaster-response pipeline: disaster perception, spatial relational analysis, rescue and evacuation planning, temporal evolution reasoning, and multi-modal report synthesis. Agents compose calls from a 108-tool MCP library over heterogeneous geospatial data: optical, SAR, and multi-spectral imagery across single-, bi-, and multi-temporal sequences (0.015-10m GSD), complemented by elevation and social vector layers. We comprehensively evaluate 13 frontier LLMs on our benchmark, revealing three persistent challenges: 1) disaster-domain grounding exposes unique failure modes (damage-semantic grounding, sensor-modality mismatch, and disaster-pipeline composition); 2) agents are doubly bottlenecked by tool selection and argument grounding, where gold tool-order hints improve accuracy by only 1.08-4.40%, and alternative scaffolds yield at most a 3.24% gain; 3) compositional fragility scales with trajectory length, the agent-to-gold gap widening from 7% to 56% on long pipelines. DORA establishes a rigorous testbed for operationally reliable disaster-response agents.

AI.tldr: DORA is a benchmark to evaluate end-to-end disaster-response AI agents using realistic, expert-authored tasks and tool-based trajectories over multi-sensor geospatial data.
AI.motivation: Existing disaster-response research mainly focuses on isolated components like remote-sensing perception or generic tool use, and lacks a rigorous way to evaluate full, operational workflows that responders actually need (e.g., integrating multi-sensor data, reasoning over infrastructure and populations, planning evacuations, and generating actionable reports). A specialized benchmark is needed to test whether agentic LLM systems can perform these complex, multi-step, tool-augmented tasks reliably in real disaster scenarios.
AI.method: The authors construct DORA, an agentic benchmark with 515 expert-authored tasks from 45 real disaster events (10 disaster types). Each task comes with expert-verified, replayable gold trajectories comprising about 3,500 total tool calls. Tasks cover five dimensions of the disaster-response pipeline: (1) disaster perception, (2) spatial relational analysis, (3) rescue and evacuation planning, (4) temporal evolution reasoning, and (5) multi-modal report synthesis. Agents operate over heterogeneous geospatial data (optical, SAR, multispectral imagery at various resolutions and temporal settings, plus elevation and social vector layers) via a large library of 108 tools. Thirteen frontier LLMs are evaluated by how well they reproduce gold trajectories and solve tasks, considering tool selection, argument grounding, and performance across pipeline length.
AI.result: The evaluation shows three major challenges: (1) disaster-domain grounding causes unique model failures, including misinterpreting damage semantics, mismatching sensor modalities to tasks, and failing to correctly compose steps in the disaster-response pipeline; (2) agent performance is constrained both by choosing the right tools and by providing correct arguments, with only modest gains (1.08–4.40%) when models are given gold tool-order hints and similarly small improvements (≤3.24%) from alternative scaffolding strategies; (3) performance degrades sharply as task/trajectory length grows, with the gap between agent behavior and gold trajectories increasing from 7% on short pipelines to 56% on long ones, indicating strong compositional fragility.
AI.conclusion: DORA provides the first comprehensive, agent-centric benchmark for testing end-to-end disaster-response capabilities of LLM-based agents, grounded in realistic, expert-designed tasks and multi-sensor geospatial data. The empirical findings highlight current limitations in domain grounding, tool usage, and long-horizon compositional reasoning, underscoring that substantial research is needed before such agents can be operationally reliable in real disaster-response settings.

title: Is Child-Directed Language Optimized for Word Learning? A Computational Study of Verb Meaning Acquisition
summary: Is child-directed language (CDL) optimized to support language learning, and which aspects of linguistic development does it facilitate? We investigate this question using neural language models trained on CDL versus adult-directed language (ADL). We selectively remove syntactic or lexical co-occurrence information from the model training data, and evaluate the impact of these manipulations on verb meaning acquisition. While disrupting syntax impairs learning across all datasets, models trained on CDL and spoken ADL show significantly higher resilience than those trained on written input. Tracking semantic and syntactic performance over training, we observe a semantic-first trajectory, with verb meanings emerging prior to robust syntactic proficiency, an asynchrony most pronounced in the spoken domain, especially CDL. These results suggest that the advantage for verb learning previously attributed to CDL may instead reflect broader properties of the spoken register, rather than a uniquely CDL-specific optimization.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether child-directed language offers a special learning advantage by comparing neural language models trained on child-directed vs adult-directed speech and text, manipulating syntax and lexical co-occurrence, and examining verb learning.
AI.motivation: To clarify if child-directed language is specially optimized for children’s language acquisition or if its apparent benefits arise from general properties of spoken language, and to understand which specific aspects of linguistic input (syntax vs lexical co-occurrence) most support verb meaning learning.
AI.method: Train neural language models separately on corpora of child-directed language, spoken adult-directed language, and written adult-directed language. Systematically remove (or disrupt) syntactic structure and lexical co-occurrence information in the training data. Evaluate the models’ ability to acquire verb meanings and track their semantic and syntactic performance over the course of training to infer learning trajectories.
AI.result: Disrupting syntactic structure harms verb learning across all types of input. However, models trained on child-directed and spoken adult-directed language are more robust to this disruption than models trained on written language. Across conditions, models show a semantic-first learning trajectory: verb meanings start to emerge before strong syntactic knowledge, with this asynchrony being especially strong in spoken language, and most of all in child-directed speech.
AI.conclusion: Verb learning benefits seen in child-directed input are not uniquely due to a special optimization of child-directed language but are largely shared with properties of spoken registers in general. Spoken language—especially child-directed speech—supports early semantic learning (verb meanings) even before full syntactic proficiency develops, highlighting broader advantages of spoken input over written input for early verb meaning acquisition.

title: Seirênes: Adversarial Self-Play with Evolving Distractions for LLM Reasoning
summary: We present Seirênes, a self-play RL framework that transforms contextual interference from a failure mode of LLM reasoning into an internal training signal for co-evolving more resilient reasoners. While RL with verifiable rewards has significantly advanced reasoning capabilities, models can still exhibit fragility when encountering non-idealized contexts: scenarios characterized by superfluous information, tangential instructions, or incidental correlations that differ from the clean distributions typical of standard benchmarks. Seirênes harnesses this vulnerability through a parameter-shared and adversarial self-play loop. Within this framework, a single model is trained to both construct plausible yet distracting contexts that expose its own reasoning blind spots, and solve problems by discerning the essential task from these perturbations to recover the core underlying logic. By pitting these competing objectives against each other, Seirênes compels the model to move beyond superficial pattern matching and anchors its capabilities in robust underlying reasoning. This continuous interaction sustains an informative co-evolutionary curriculum as the model improves. Across seven mathematical reasoning benchmarks and model scales from 4B to 30B, Seirênes achieves average gains of +10.2, +9.1, and +7.2 points. Besides, distracting contexts produced by the 4B Seirênes model reduce the accuracy of top-tier closed-source models (GPT and Gemini) by roughly 4--5 points, revealing Seirênes' general ability to uncover reasoning models' blind spots.

AI.tldr: Seirênes is a self-play RL framework where an LLM both creates distracting contexts and solves tasks within them, turning contextual interference into a training signal to build more robust reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing RL methods with verifiable rewards improve reasoning but leave models fragile in messy, non-ideal contexts that include irrelevant details, tangential instructions, or spurious correlations. Benchmarks usually assume clean inputs, so models may fail when faced with real-world noisy prompts. The authors want a way to systematically expose and fix these contextual blind spots, rather than just optimizing for clean benchmark performance.
AI.method: Seirênes uses a parameter-shared, adversarial self-play loop. A single model plays two roles: (1) context adversary, generating plausible yet distracting contexts that wrap or perturb a base problem to induce reasoning failures; (2) solver, which must identify the core task within the distracting context and solve it. Both roles share parameters so that the same underlying model learns to generate strong perturbations and to be robust against them. The objectives are set up so that the adversarial context construction and robust solving compete, forming a co-evolutionary curriculum. Training uses RL with verifiable rewards on mathematical reasoning tasks under these perturbed contexts.
AI.result: On seven mathematical reasoning benchmarks and across models from 4B to 30B parameters, Seirênes yields average accuracy gains of +10.2, +9.1, and +7.2 points (likely corresponding to different scales or settings). Additionally, distracting contexts generated by a 4B Seirênes-trained model reduce the accuracy of strong closed-source models (GPT and Gemini) by about 4–5 points, showing that the adversarial contexts transfer to other models and expose their vulnerabilities as well.
AI.conclusion: Transforming contextual interference into a training signal via adversarial self-play makes LLM reasoners more robust to noisy, non-ideal prompts. Seirênes not only boosts performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks but also uncovers generalizable blind spots in other advanced models, suggesting this approach is a promising path toward more reliable reasoning in realistic settings.

title: Do Language Models Encode Knowledge of Linguistic Constraint Violations?
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong linguistic performance, yet their internal mechanisms for producing these predictions remain unclear. We investigate the hypothesis that LLMs encode representations of linguistic constraint violations within their parameters, which are selectively activated when processing ungrammatical sentences. To test this, we use sparse autoencoders to decompose polysemantic activations into sparse, monosemantic features and recover candidates for violation-related features.
  We introduce a sensitivity score for identifying features that are preferentially activated on constraint-violated versus well-formed inputs, enabling unsupervised detection of potential violation-specific features. We further propose a conjunctive falsification framework with three criteria evaluated jointly.
  Overall, the results are negative in two respects: (1) the falsification criteria are not jointly satisfied across linguistic phenomena, and (2) no features are consistently shared across all categories. While some phenomena show partial evidence of selective causal structure, the overall pattern provides limited support for a unified set of grammatical violation detectors in current LMs.

AI.tldr: The paper probes whether large language models contain dedicated internal features that detect grammatical violations, using sparse autoencoders to isolate such features, and finds mostly negative evidence for unified violation detectors.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs perform well on linguistic tasks, it is unclear how they internally represent grammatical constraints or detect ungrammaticality. The authors aim to test whether LLMs have explicit parameter-level representations corresponding to violations of linguistic constraints, which would shed light on the models’ internal structure and interpretability.
AI.method: They apply sparse autoencoders to LLM activations to decompose polysemantic neuron activations into sparse, more interpretable features. They then define a sensitivity score to identify features that preferentially activate on sentences that violate linguistic constraints compared with well-formed sentences. Finally, they use a conjunctive falsification framework with three jointly evaluated criteria to test whether discovered features are genuine, selective grammatical-violation detectors across multiple linguistic phenomena.
AI.result: Using these tools, they find candidate features that appear related to grammatical violations for some specific phenomena, but overall the identified features fail to meet all falsification criteria consistently. Furthermore, there is no common set of violation-related features that generalizes across all tested linguistic categories.
AI.conclusion: The evidence suggests that current LLMs do not rely on a unified set of dedicated grammatical-violation detectors encoded as stable, monosemantic features. While there may be some localized, partially selective causal structure for particular types of violations, grammaticality judgments in LLMs likely arise from more distributed or heterogeneous internal mechanisms.

title: A CAP-like Trilemma for Large Language Models: Correctness, Non-bias, and Utility under Semantic Underdetermination
summary: The CAP theorem states that a distributed system cannot simultaneously guarantee consistency, availability, and partition tolerance under network partition. Inspired by this result, this paper formulates a CAP-like conjecture for Large Language Models (LLMs). The proposed trilemma states that, under semantic underdetermination, an LLM cannot always simultaneously guarantee strong correctness, strict non-bias, and high utility. A prompt is semantically underdetermined when the given premises do not determine a unique answer. In such cases, a useful and decisive response requires the model to introduce a selection criterion, preference, prior, or value ordering. If this criterion is not supplied by the user or justified by the available premises, the response becomes biased in a broad selection-theoretic sense. Conversely, if the model avoids unsupported preferences, it may preserve correctness and non-bias but may reduce utility through refusal, hedging, or clarification. The paper formalizes this correctness--non-bias--utility trilemma, develops examples, and argues that certain LLM failures arise not merely from model limitations but from the structure of underdetermined decision requests.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a CAP-like trilemma for LLMs: in semantically underdetermined prompts, an LLM cannot always achieve strong correctness, strict non-bias, and high utility at the same time.
AI.motivation: Motivated by the CAP theorem in distributed systems, which shows an inherent trade-off between desirable properties, the paper seeks an analogous fundamental limitation for LLMs. It aims to explain systematic LLM “failures” not just as engineering weaknesses but as consequences of structural constraints when prompts do not determine a unique correct answer.
AI.method: The paper introduces formal definitions for three properties—strong correctness, strict non-bias, and high utility—under the condition of semantic underdetermination. It defines underdetermined prompts, analyzes how any decisive answer implicitly introduces unsupported selection criteria, and uses selection-theoretic reasoning and constructed examples to show that not all three properties can be satisfied simultaneously in such cases.
AI.result: The analysis shows that for semantically underdetermined prompts, if the model gives a decisive and useful answer, it must introduce preferences or priors not justified by the input, which counts as bias; if it avoids such preferences to remain strictly unbiased and correct, it must reduce utility by refusing, hedging, or asking for clarification. Thus, at least one of the three desiderata—strong correctness, strict non-bias, high utility—must be relaxed.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that some observed shortcomings of LLM behavior are inherent trade-offs caused by semantic underdetermination rather than only by model or training flaws. It emphasizes that designers and users must choose which dimension to relax—correctness, non-bias, or utility—in specific contexts, and that clearer prompts or explicit preference elicitation are needed to navigate this trilemma.

title: Sign Language Recognition and Translation for Low-Resource Languages: Challenges and Pathways Forward
summary: Sign languages are natural, visual-gestural languages used by Deaf communities worldwide. Over 300 distinct sign languages remain severely low-resource due to limited documentation, sparse datasets, and insufficient computational tools. This systematic review synthesizes literature on sign language recognition and translation for under-resourced languages, using Azerbaijan Sign Language (AzSL) as a case study. Analysis of global initiatives extracts eight actionable lessons, including community co-design, dialectal diversity capture, and privacy-preserving pose-based representations. Turkic sign languages (Kazakh, Turkish, Azerbaijani) receive special attention, as linguistic proximity enables effective transfer learning. We propose three paradigm shifts: from architecture-centric to data-centric AI, from signer-independent to signer-adaptive systems, and from reference-based to task-specific evaluation metrics. A technical roadmap for AzSL leverages lightweight MediaPipe-based architectures, community-validated annotations, and offline-first deployment. Progress requires sustained interdisciplinary collaboration centered on Deaf communities to ensure cultural authenticity, ethical governance, and practical communication benefit.

AI.tldr: Systematic review of sign language recognition and translation for low-resource sign languages, using Azerbaijan Sign Language as a detailed case and proposing paradigm shifts and a roadmap.
AI.motivation: Over 300 sign languages are low-resource, lacking data, tools, and documentation, which limits computational support and practical communication technologies for Deaf communities. There is a need to understand existing work and derive guidance for developing systems for such under-resourced sign languages, particularly AzSL.
AI.method: Conduct a systematic review of literature on sign language recognition and translation focused on under-resourced sign languages, with an in-depth case analysis of Azerbaijan Sign Language. Examine global initiatives, especially within Turkic sign languages, to derive lessons and best practices. Analyze architectures, datasets, evaluation methods, and community engagement models to propose future directions.
AI.result: The review identifies eight actionable lessons such as the importance of community co-design, capturing dialectal diversity, and using privacy-preserving pose-based representations. It finds that Turkic sign languages offer promising grounds for transfer learning due to linguistic proximity. The study proposes three key paradigm shifts—toward data-centric AI, signer-adaptive systems, and task-specific evaluation metrics—and outlines a concrete technical roadmap for AzSL using lightweight architectures, validated annotations, and offline-capable systems.
AI.conclusion: Effective progress in under-resourced sign language technology, particularly for AzSL, depends on shifting focus to better data practices, personalization, and more meaningful evaluation, alongside a practical, lightweight deployment strategy. Central to success is long-term, interdisciplinary collaboration that is led by and accountable to Deaf communities, ensuring cultural authenticity, ethical governance, and real communicative value.

title: OOM-Free Alpamayo via CPU-GPU Memory Swapping for Vision-Language-Action Models
comment: Submitted to IEEE RTCSA on March 26, 2026 (KST); Accepted on May 4, 2026 (KST)
summary: End-to-end Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for autonomous driving unify perception, reasoning, and control in a single neural network, achieving strong driving performance but requiring 20-60GB of GPU memory-far exceeding the 12-16GB available on commodity GPUs. We present a framework, which enables memory-efficient VLA inference on VRAM-constrained GPUs through system-level optimization alone, without model modification. Our work proceeds in three stages: (1) Sequential Demand Layering reduces VRAM usage from model-level to layer-level granularity; (2) Pipelined Demand Layering hides parameter transfer time within layer execution time via transfer--compute overlap; and (3) a GPU-Resident Layer Decision Policy, informed by per-module residency benefit analysis, eliminates the residual transfer overhead that pipelining cannot hide. We further propose a performance prediction model that determines the optimal configuration-both the number and placement of resident layers-from a single profiling run with less than 1.3% prediction error across all configurations. Applied to NVIDIA's Alpamayo-R1-10B (21.52GB) on an RTX 5070Ti (16GB), our work achieves up to 3.55x speedup over Accelerate offloading while maintaining full BF16 precision.

AI.tldr: Memory-efficient system-level framework to run large vision-language-action driving models on commodity GPUs without changing the model.
AI.motivation: End-to-end VLA driving models need 20–60GB of GPU memory, but many users only have 12–16GB VRAM, making deployment impractical with current offloading approaches that are too slow or require reduced precision.
AI.method: Introduce a three-stage system optimization pipeline: (1) Sequential Demand Layering to manage parameters at layer-level granularity; (2) Pipelined Demand Layering to overlap parameter transfer with computation, hiding most transfer latency; and (3) a GPU-Resident Layer Decision Policy that, using a per-module residency benefit analysis and a performance prediction model, chooses which layers remain on GPU to eliminate remaining transfer overhead. A prediction model selects the optimal resident-layer configuration from one profiling run with low error.
AI.result: On NVIDIA Alpamayo-R1-10B (21.52GB) running on an RTX 5070Ti (16GB), the framework achieves up to 3.55× speedup over Accelerate offloading, while retaining full BF16 precision. The performance predictor attains less than 1.3% prediction error across all configurations.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed system-level memory management and scheduling can enable large VLA models to run efficiently on VRAM-limited GPUs, avoiding model changes or precision loss while significantly outperforming standard offloading solutions.

title: Metaphor Is Not All Attention Needs
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed in safety-critical applications, where their ability to resist harmful instructions is essential. Although post-training aims to make models robust against many jailbreak strategies, recent evidence shows that stylistic reformulations, such as poetic transformation, can still bypass safety mechanisms with alarming effectiveness. This raises a central question: why do literary jailbreaks succeed? In this work, we investigate whether their effectiveness depends on specific poetic devices, on a failure to recognize literary formatting, or on deeper changes in how models process stylistically irregular prompts. We address this problem through an interpretability analysis of attention patterns. We perform input-level ablation studies to assess the contribution of individual and combinations of poetic devices; construct an interpretable vector representation of attention maps; cluster these representations and train linear probes to predict safety outcomes and literary format. Our results show that models distinguish poetic from prose formats with high accuracy, yet struggle to predict jailbreak success within each format. Clustering further reveals clear separation by literary format, but not by safety label. These findings indicate that jailbreak success is not caused by a failure to recognize poetic formatting; rather, poetic prompts induce distinct processing patterns that remain largely independent of harmful-content detection. Overall, literary jailbreaks appear to misalign large language models not through any single poetic device, but through accumulated stylistic irregularities that alter prompt processing and avoid lexical triggers considered during post-training. This suggests that robustness requires safety mechanisms that account for style-induced shifts in model behavior. We use Qwen3-14B as a representative open-weight case study.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why poetic or literary jailbreak prompts can bypass safety mechanisms in large language models using attention-based interpretability on Qwen3-14B.
AI.motivation: Large language models are being deployed in safety-critical settings, so they need to resist harmful instructions. However, "literary" or poetic reformulations of harmful requests can still jailbreak models effectively. It is unclear whether this is due to specific poetic devices, failure to detect the format as literary, or deeper processing changes, motivating a systematic analysis of how such prompts are handled internally.
AI.method: The authors use interpretability of attention patterns in Qwen3-14B. They (1) perform input-level ablation on different poetic devices individually and in combination to measure their impact on jailbreak success, (2) build interpretable vector representations of the model’s attention maps, (3) cluster these attention representations, and (4) train linear probes on them to predict both safety outcomes (jailbreak vs refusal) and literary format (poetry vs prose).
AI.result: The model can reliably distinguish poetic from prose prompts based on attention patterns, and clustering of attention representations clearly separates by literary format. However, neither clustering nor linear probes can effectively separate successful from unsuccessful jailbreaks within each format. No single poetic device explains jailbreak success; instead, accumulated stylistic irregularities shift the model’s processing while leaving harmful-content detection largely unaffected by style.
AI.conclusion: Literary jailbreaks succeed not because the model fails to recognize poetic format but because stylistic irregularities induce distinct processing trajectories that are weakly tied to harmful-content detection and can bypass lexical safety triggers set during post-training. Robust safety thus requires mechanisms that explicitly account for style-induced shifts in model behavior, going beyond simple lexical or straightforward pattern-based filters.

title: Explaining and Breaking the Safety-Helpfulness Ceiling via Preference Dimensional Expansion
summary: In the realm of multi-objective alignment for large language models, balancing disparate human preferences often manifests as a zero-sum conflict. Specifically, the intrinsic tension between competing goals dictates that aggressively optimizing for one metric (e.g., helpfulness) frequently incurs a substantial penalty on another (e.g., harmlessness). While prior work mainly focuses on data selection, parameter merging, or algorithmic balancing during training, these approaches merely force compromises between divergent preferences along a fixed Pareto frontier, failing to fundamentally resolve the inherent trade-off. In this work, we approach this problem from a novel perspective of multi-dimensional rewards. By scaling up the model's rollouts and analyzing the outputs across different reward dimensions, we arrive at a critical conclusion: the conflict among multiple objectives stems from the fact that the prompt itself inherently restricts the achievable multi-dimensional rewards. Based on this core observation, we propose MORA: Multi-Objective Reward Assimilation. Specifically, MORA isolates single-reward prompts through pre-sampling and expands their reward diversity by rewriting the original questions to incorporate multi-dimensional intents. Extensive experiments demonstrate that: (1) in sequential alignment, MORA achieves single-preference improvements ranging from 5% to 12.4%, with exceptional gains in harmlessness, after multiple-preference alignment across helpful, harmless, and truthful dimensions. (2) In simultaneous alignment, MORA achieves an average overall reward improvement of 4.6%. Our codes are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/MORA-MPA.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MORA, a method to improve multi-objective alignment (helpfulness, harmlessness, truthfulness) in large language models by re-writing prompts to embed multiple reward dimensions, reducing zero-sum trade-offs and improving overall rewards in both sequential and simultaneous alignment settings.
AI.motivation: Multi-objective alignment of large language models must handle conflicting human preferences such as helpfulness vs. harmlessness. Existing methods (data selection, parameter merging, training-time balancing) operate along a fixed Pareto frontier and mainly enforce compromises, often worsening one objective when optimizing another. The authors aim to understand and mitigate the inherent trade-offs, hypothesizing that conflicts may be driven by prompt limitations rather than model capacity alone.
AI.method: The authors introduce a multi-dimensional reward perspective, scaling up model rollouts and evaluating outputs along different reward dimensions. From this analysis, they infer that prompts restrict achievable rewards across objectives. They then propose MORA (Multi-Objective Reward Assimilation), which (1) pre-samples prompts that are primarily associated with a single reward dimension (“single-reward prompts”), and (2) rewrites these prompts to explicitly incorporate multiple intents corresponding to several reward dimensions, thereby expanding reward diversity in training data. They evaluate MORA both in sequential alignment (optimizing objectives one after another) and simultaneous alignment (optimizing multiple objectives jointly).
AI.result: In sequential alignment across helpfulness, harmlessness, and truthfulness, MORA provides single-preference improvements between 5% and 12.4%, with especially strong gains in harmlessness after multi-preference training. In simultaneous alignment, MORA achieves an average overall reward improvement of 4.6% across the evaluated objectives. These results empirically support the claim that prompt design and rewriting can alleviate apparent zero-sum conflicts in multi-objective alignment.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that a key source of conflict in multi-objective alignment is the prompt’s limited ability to elicit high rewards across several dimensions simultaneously. By systematically identifying single-reward prompts and rewriting them to encode multi-dimensional intents, MORA can shift the effective trade-off frontier, leading to better performance across multiple objectives without purely zero-sum compromises. This suggests that better prompt-level reward assimilation is a promising direction for improving multi-objective alignment in large language models.

title: Latent Causal Void: Explicit Missing-Context Reconstruction for Misinformation Detection
summary: Automatic misinformation detection performs well when deception is visible in what an article explicitly states. However, some misinformation articles remain locally coherent and only become misleading once compared with contemporaneous reports that supply background facts the article omits. We study this omission-relevant setting and observe that current omission-aware approaches typically either attach retrieved context as auxiliary evidence or infer a categorical omission signal, leaving the specific missing fact implicit. We propose \emph{Latent Causal Void} (LCV), a retrieval-guided detector that explicitly reconstructs the missing fact for each target sentence and uses it as a textual cross-source relation in graph reasoning. Concretely, LCV retrieves temporally aligned context articles, asks a frozen instruction-tuned large language model to generate a short missing-context description for each sentence--article pair, and feeds the resulting relation text into a heterograph over target sentences and context articles. On the bilingual benchmark of Sheng et al., LCV improves over the strongest omission-aware baseline by $2.56$ and $2.84$ macro-F1 points on the English and Chinese splits, respectively. The results indicate that modeling the missing cross-source fact itself, rather than only attaching retrieved evidence or predicting an omission signal, is a useful representation for omission-aware misinformation detection.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Latent Causal Void (LCV), a method for omission-aware misinformation detection that explicitly reconstructs missing facts by leveraging retrieved contemporaneous articles and using the inferred missing context as relational text in a heterograph model, outperforming prior omission-aware baselines on a bilingual benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing misinformation detectors work well when falsehoods are explicit but struggle with omission-based misinformation, where articles appear locally coherent yet become misleading only when contrasted with omitted background facts from other sources. Current omission-aware methods either simply attach retrieved context or predict a coarse omission signal, without explicitly representing what concrete fact is missing. The authors aim to address this gap by directly modeling the missing cross-source facts to improve omission-aware misinformation detection, especially in realistic multi-article news settings.
AI.method: The authors propose Latent Causal Void (LCV), a retrieval-guided detection framework. First, for each target article sentence, LCV retrieves temporally aligned context articles. Then, a frozen instruction-tuned large language model is prompted to generate a short description of the missing context for each target-sentence–context-article pair, effectively hypothesizing the omitted fact. These generated missing-fact descriptions are treated as textual relation edges in a heterograph whose nodes represent target sentences and context articles. A graph reasoning module then processes this heterograph, leveraging the explicit cross-source relations (the reconstructed missing facts) to detect misinformation at the article level. The approach is bilingual and operates on both English and Chinese data.
AI.result: On the bilingual omission-aware misinformation benchmark of Sheng et al., the proposed LCV method outperforms the strongest omission-aware baseline. Specifically, it achieves improvements of 2.56 macro-F1 points on the English split and 2.84 macro-F1 points on the Chinese split. These gains demonstrate that explicitly modeling missing cross-source facts yields better detection performance than methods that either only attach retrieved evidence or infer a categorical omission flag.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that explicitly reconstructing and modeling the missing cross-source facts—rather than just appending retrieved context or predicting a generic omission signal—is an effective representation strategy for omission-aware misinformation detection. By using LLM-generated missing-context descriptions as relational text in a heterograph over target sentences and context articles, LCV captures the subtle omission-based misleading patterns and leads to consistent performance gains across both English and Chinese. This suggests that future omission-aware misinformation detectors should focus on making the missing information itself an explicit, learnable object in the model.

title: Persistent and Conversational Multi-Method Explainability for Trustworthy Financial AI
summary: Financial institutions increasingly require AI explanations that are persistent, cross-validated across methods, and conversationally accessible to human decision-makers. We present an architecture for human-centered explainable AI in financial sentiment analysis that combines three contributions. First, we treat XAI artifacts -- LIME feature attributions, occlusion-based word importance scores, and saliency heatmaps -- as persistent, searchable objects in distributed S3-compatible storage with structured metadata and natural-language summaries, enabling semantic retrieval over explanation history and automatic index reconstruction after system failures. Second, we enable multi-method explanation triangulation, where a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) assistant compares and synthesizes results from multiple XAI methods applied to the same prediction, allowing users to assess explanation robustness through natural-language dialogue. Third, we evaluate the faithfulness of generated explanations using automated checks over grounding completeness, hallucinated claims, and method-attribution behavior. We demonstrate the architecture on an EXTRA-BRAIN financial sentiment analysis pipeline using FinBERT predictions and present evaluation results showing that constrained prompting reduces hallucination rate by 36\% and increases method-attribution citations by 73\% compared to naive prompting. We discuss implications for trustworthy, human-centered AI services in regulated financial environments.

AI.tldr: Architecture for human-centered explainable AI in financial sentiment analysis, storing and retrieving XAI artifacts, triangulating multiple explanation methods via RAG, and automatically evaluating explanation faithfulness, demonstrated on FinBERT with reduced hallucinations and better method attribution.
AI.motivation: Financial institutions need explanations for AI decisions that are robust, auditable over time, and understandable in natural language. Existing XAI tools often produce one-off visualizations or scores that are not persistent, hard to compare across methods, and not directly usable in conversational interfaces or regulatory settings. There is a gap in creating a system that makes explanations first-class, searchable objects and that can synthesize multiple XAI methods into coherent, trustworthy narratives for human decision-makers.
AI.method: The paper proposes an architecture with three main components: (1) treating XAI artifacts—LIME attributions, occlusion-based word importance scores, and saliency heatmaps—as persistent objects stored in S3-compatible storage with rich metadata and natural-language summaries, supporting semantic search and recovery; (2) a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) conversational assistant that retrieves these artifacts and performs multi-method triangulation, comparing and synthesizing explanations from different XAI methods on the same prediction; (3) an automated evaluation framework to assess faithfulness of generated explanations via checks on grounding completeness, hallucinations, and correct citation of XAI methods. The architecture is instantiated on a financial sentiment pipeline (EXTRA-BRAIN) using FinBERT as the base sentiment model.
AI.result: In experiments on the FinBERT-based sentiment analysis pipeline, the system shows that using constrained prompting strategies in the RAG assistant significantly improves explanation quality: hallucination rate is reduced by 36% and method-attribution citations (i.e., explicit references to which XAI method supports which claim) increase by 73% compared with naive prompting. The system also successfully maintains persistent explanation artifacts and supports semantic retrieval and multi-method triangulation in interactive use cases.
AI.conclusion: The architecture demonstrates that making XAI artifacts persistent, searchable, and integrable into a RAG-based conversational assistant enables more robust, transparent, and auditable explanations for financial sentiment models. Multi-method explanation triangulation helps users assess robustness, and automated faithfulness checks can quantify and improve explanation reliability. These contributions support the development of trustworthy, human-centered AI services suitable for regulated financial environments and suggest a path toward more principled explainable AI infrastructures beyond one-off visualizations.

title: Correcting Selection Bias in Sparse User Feedback for Large Language Model Quality Estimation: A Multi-Agent Hierarchical Bayesian Approach
summary: [Abridged] Production LLM deployments receive feedback from a non-random fraction of users: thumbs sit mostly in the tails of the satisfaction distribution, and a naive average over them can land 40-50 percentage points away from true system quality. We treat this as a topic- and sentiment- stratified selection-bias problem and propose a three-agent hierarchical Bayesian pipeline that does not require ground-truth labels on individual interactions. A Topic Clustering Agent partitions the stream via UMAP + HDBSCAN over text embeddings; a Bias Modeling Agent fits a two-stage hierarchical Beta-Binomial under NUTS, inferring per-topic selection rates $s_c$ and quality $q_c$ with partial pooling; a Synthesis Agent reweights $q_c$ by true topic prevalence $\hatπ_c = n_c/N$ to report a bias-corrected aggregate posterior $\bar Q = \sum_c \hatπ_c q_c$ with credible interval, plus drift signals for online recalibration. Validation uses UltraFeedback (N=10,232 retained interactions, $C=18$ clusters, $Q^\star=0.6249$) with simulated topic- and sentiment-dependent selection biases. We compare five Bayesian variants against Naive and IPW baselines. A mild prior on the feedback channel (typical positive-feedback rate and negative-to-positive ratio, both readable from any production dashboard without labels) keeps Hierarchical-Informed within 4-13 pp of $Q^\star$ as the bias ratio sweeps from 1:1 to 30:1, with 95% credible intervals covering $Q^\star$ in 50/50 random-seed replicates at $κ_{\max}=10$. Without channel-side priors, every weak-prior variant misses $Q^\star$ by 22-33 pp: the per-cluster sufficient statistics admit a one-parameter family of equally good fits, and the prior on the bias channel (not on latent quality) is what breaks the degeneracy.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses selection bias in user feedback for LLM systems and proposes a hierarchical Bayesian pipeline to recover unbiased quality estimates without labeled data.
AI.motivation: In production, LLM feedback is highly non-random: users mainly give thumbs up/down at satisfaction extremes, so naive averaging of this feedback can be off by 40-50 percentage points from the true quality. There is a need for a principled way to correct for this selection bias across topics and sentiments.
AI.method: They model the problem as topic- and sentiment-stratified selection bias, using a three-agent pipeline: (1) Topic Clustering Agent clusters interactions using UMAP and HDBSCAN over embeddings; (2) Bias Modeling Agent fits a two-stage hierarchical Beta-Binomial model with NUTS to infer per-topic selection rates and quality with partial pooling, incorporating priors on feedback channel behavior; (3) Synthesis Agent reweights per-topic quality by empirical topic prevalence to produce a bias-corrected overall quality posterior and drift signals.
AI.result: Using the UltraFeedback dataset with simulated selection biases over 18 clusters and true quality 0.6249, they compare five Bayesian variants to Naive and IPW baselines. A hierarchical model with informative priors on the feedback channel stays within 4-13 percentage points of true quality across a wide bias-ratio range and its 95% credible intervals cover the true quality in half of random-seed runs, while variants without such priors miss by 22-33 percentage points due to non-identifiability.
AI.conclusion: Correcting selection bias in LLM feedback requires modeling the feedback channel, not just outcome quality.
