A population-level auditing framework, captured by the Caricature Gap, can complement traditional AI-text detection and offers a Computational Social Science approach for evaluating the realism and potential risks of synthetic political discourse.

title: BadSKP: Backdoor Attacks on Knowledge Graph-Enhanced LLMs with Soft Prompts
summary: Recent knowledge graph (KG)-enhanced large language models (LLMs) move beyond purely textual knowledge augmentation by encoding retrieved subgraphs into continuous soft prompts via graph neural networks, introducing a graph-conditioned channel that operates alongside the standard text interface. However, existing backdoor attacks are largely designed for the textual channel, and their effectiveness against this dual-channel architecture remains unclear. We show that this architecture creates a robustness gap: text-channel backdoor attacks that readily compromise textual KG prompting systems become largely ineffective against soft-prompt-based counterparts. We interpret this gap through semantic anchoring, whereby graph-derived soft prompts bias the generation-driving hidden state toward query-consistent semantics and suppress surface-level malicious instructions. Because this anchoring effect is itself induced by the graph channel, an attacker who manipulates graph-level representations can in turn redirect it toward adversarial semantics. To demonstrate this risk, we propose BadSKP, a backdoor attack that targets the graph-to-prompt interface through a multi-stage optimization strategy: it constructs adversarial target embeddings, optimizes poisoned node embeddings to steer the induced soft prompt, and approximates the optimized representations with fluent adversarial node attributes. Experiments on two soft-prompt KG-enhanced LLMs across four datasets show that BadSKP achieves high attack success under both frozen and trojaned settings, while text-only attacks remain unreliable even under perplexity-based defenses.

AI.tldr: The paper studies backdoor attacks on knowledge graph (KG)-enhanced large language models that use graph-derived soft prompts, finds that traditional text-based backdoor attacks are weak against this dual-channel design, and proposes a new graph-channel attack, BadSKP, that is highly effective.
AI.motivation: KG-enhanced LLMs increasingly integrate a graph-conditioned channel, where retrieved KG subgraphs are encoded by GNNs into soft prompts that guide generation alongside text. Security research and backdoor attacks have focused almost exclusively on the textual channel. It is unknown whether these text-based attacks remain effective when a strong graph-based conditioning channel is present, and whether this new architecture introduces distinct vulnerabilities at the graph level.
AI.method: The authors first empirically compare standard text-channel backdoor attacks on two types of KG-LLM architectures: textual KG prompting vs. soft-prompt KG-enhanced models, revealing a robustness gap. They interpret the gap via a "semantic anchoring" effect, where graph-derived soft prompts steer the hidden state toward semantics consistent with the KG and away from malicious textual triggers. Then they design BadSKP, a backdoor attack tailored to the graph-to-soft-prompt interface. BadSKP uses multi-stage optimization: (1) construct adversarial target embeddings that represent the attacker’s desired output semantics, (2) optimize poisoned node embeddings so that the induced soft prompt will steer the model toward these adversarial semantics, and (3) approximate these optimized embeddings with natural-looking adversarial node attributes so the poisoned KG content remains fluent and inconspicuous.
AI.result: On two KG-enhanced LLMs using soft prompts and four benchmark datasets, text-only backdoor methods that work on purely textual KG prompting become unreliable in the dual-channel setting, even when attackers try to evade perplexity-based defenses. In contrast, BadSKP achieves high attack success rates in both frozen (pretrained LLM unchanged) and trojaned (LLM already backdoored) scenarios, demonstrating that manipulating graph-level representations at the graph-to-prompt interface is a much more effective attack vector in this architecture.
AI.conclusion: Dual-channel KG-enhanced LLMs with graph-derived soft prompts are more robust to conventional text-channel backdoor attacks due to semantic anchoring from the KG, but this same mechanism introduces a new vulnerability: attackers who control or can poison KG content can subvert the graph-to-soft-prompt interface. The proposed BadSKP attack shows that graph-channel backdoors can reliably steer model behavior despite defenses that hinder text-only attacks, highlighting the need for new protection mechanisms focused on KG integrity, graph encoder robustness, and secure graph-to-prompt pipelines.

title: Task-Adaptive Embedding Refinement via Test-time LLM Guidance
summary: We explore the effectiveness of an LLM-guided query refinement paradigm for extending the usability of embedding models to challenging zero-shot search and classification tasks. Our approach refines the embedding representation of a user query using feedback from a generative LLM on a small set of documents, enabling embeddings to adapt in real time to the target task. We conduct extensive experiments with state-of-the-art text embedding models across a diverse set of challenging search and classification benchmarks. Empirical results indicate that LLM-guided query refinement yields consistent gains across all models and datasets, with relative improvements of up to +25% in literature search, intent detection, key-point matching, and nuanced query-instruction following. The refined queries improve ranking quality and induce clearer binary separation across the corpus, enabling the embedding space to better reflect the nuanced, task-specific constraints of each ad-hoc user query. Importantly, this expands the range of practical settings in which embedding models can be effectively deployed, making them a compelling alternative when costly LLM pipelines are not viable at corpus-scale. We release our experimental code for reproducibility, at https://github.com/IBM/task-aware-embedding-refinement.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an LLM-guided query refinement method that uses a generative LLM’s feedback on a few documents to rewrite user queries before embedding, significantly improving zero-shot search and classification performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard text embedding models struggle with challenging zero-shot search and classification tasks, especially when queries have nuanced, task-specific constraints. Fully LLM-based retrieval or classification pipelines can handle nuance but are too costly to run at corpus scale. The authors aim to bridge this gap by making existing embedding models more adaptable and effective without paying the high cost of end-to-end LLM reasoning over the full corpus.
AI.method: Given a user query and a small sampled set of documents, a generative LLM is prompted to analyze the task and produce a refined version of the query that better reflects the user’s intent and evaluation criteria. This refined query is then embedded using a standard embedding model and used for retrieval or classification instead of the original query embedding. The process effectively performs task-aware embedding refinement at query time, without retraining the embedding model. The authors test this paradigm with multiple state-of-the-art embedding models over various search and classification benchmarks.
AI.result: Across diverse and challenging benchmarks—including literature search, intent detection, key-point matching, and nuanced instruction-following search—the LLM-guided query refinement consistently improves performance over using raw query embeddings. They report relative improvements of up to 25% across all evaluated embedding models and datasets, along with better ranking metrics and clearer binary separation in the embedding space between relevant and non-relevant documents.
AI.conclusion: LLM-guided query refinement is an effective, model-agnostic way to adapt static embedding models to complex, task-specific queries in zero-shot settings. By using an LLM only on a small subset of documents to refine the query, the method offers a practical compromise between accuracy and computational cost, enabling more accurate retrieval and classification at corpus scale without depending on fully LLM-based pipelines. The authors provide open-source code to facilitate adoption and further research.

title: LegalCheck: Retrieval- and Context-Augmented Generation for Drafting Municipal Legal Advice Letters
comment: Accepted at ICAIL 2026 as Short Paper
summary: Public-sector legal departments in the Netherlands face acute staff shortages, increased case volumes, and increased pressure to meet regulatory compliance. This paper presents LegalCheck, a novel system that addresses these challenges by automating the drafting of objection response letters through a combination of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Context-Augmented Generation (CAG). Using a large language model (LLM) alongside curated legal knowledge bases, LegalCheck performs retrieval of relevant laws and precedents, and uses controlled prompting to incorporate both external knowledge and case-specific details into a coherent draft. An expert-in-the-loop review ensures that each generated letter is legally sound and contextually appropriate. In a real-world deployment within the Municipality of Amsterdam, LegalCheck produced near-final advice letters in minutes rather than hours, while maintaining high legal consistency and factual accuracy. The output is based on actual regulations and prior cases, providing explainable outputs that captured the vast majority of required legal reasoning (often 80\% to 100\% of essential content). Legal professionals found that the system reduced their workload and ensured a consistent application of legal standards, without replacing human judgment. These results demonstrate substantial efficiency gains, improved legal consistency, and positive user acceptance. More broadly, this work illustrates how responsible AI can be deployed in the legal domain by augmenting LLMs with domain knowledge and governance mechanisms.

AI.tldr: LegalCheck is an AI-assisted system that drafts objection response letters for Dutch public-sector legal departments using RAG+CAG with expert review, achieving large time savings and high legal consistency in real-world use.
AI.motivation: Dutch public-sector legal departments struggle with staff shortages, rising caseloads, and higher compliance demands. Drafting objection response letters is time-consuming and must be precise, consistent, and grounded in up-to-date law and precedents. There is a need for tools that increase efficiency and consistency without sacrificing legal soundness or removing human control and accountability.
AI.method: The paper introduces LegalCheck, which combines Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Context-Augmented Generation (CAG) on top of a large language model. The system retrieves relevant regulations and precedents from curated legal knowledge bases, then uses controlled prompting to fuse retrieved material with case-specific facts into a draft response letter. An expert-in-the-loop workflow requires human legal professionals to review, correct, and approve each generated draft, ensuring legal validity and contextual appropriateness. The approach is evaluated in a real deployment at the Municipality of Amsterdam.
AI.result: In deployment, LegalCheck generated near-final objection response letters within minutes instead of hours. The drafts captured roughly 80%–100% of the essential legal reasoning and were grounded in actual regulations and prior cases, enabling explainability. Legal professionals reported reduced workload, improved consistency in applying legal standards, and found the system’s outputs to be legally consistent and factually accurate, while still requiring human judgment for final approval.
AI.conclusion: LegalCheck delivers substantial efficiency gains and improved consistency for public-sector legal work when drafting objection response letters, with strong user acceptance among legal professionals. The system shows that responsibly designed AI—combining LLMs with structured legal knowledge, retrieval mechanisms, and expert-in-the-loop governance—can be successfully and safely deployed in real-world legal settings to augment, rather than replace, human decision-making.

title: LongMemEval-V2: Evaluating Long-Term Agent Memory Toward Experienced Colleagues
summary: Long-term memory is crucial for agents in specialized web environments, where success depends on recalling interface affordances, state dynamics, workflows, and recurring failure modes. However, existing memory benchmarks for agents mostly focus on user histories, short traces, or downstream task success, leaving open how to directly evaluate whether memory systems effectively internalize environment-specific experience. To address this gap, we introduce LongMemEval-V2 (LME-V2), a benchmark for evaluating whether memory systems can help agents acquire the experience needed to become knowledgeable colleagues in customized environments. LME-V2 contains 451 manually curated questions covering five core memory abilities for web agents: static state recall, dynamic state tracking, workflow knowledge, environment gotchas, and premise awareness. Questions are paired with history trajectories containing up to 500 trajectories and 115M tokens. We use a context gathering formulation: memory systems consume history trajectories and return compact evidence for downstream question answering. We propose a suite of two memory methods: AgentRunbook-R, an efficient RAG-based memory with knowledge pools for raw state observations, events, and strategy notes, and AgentRunbook-C, which stores trajectories as files and invokes a coding agent to gather evidence in an augmented sandbox. Experiments show that AgentRunbook-C achieves the best performance with 72.5% average accuracy, outperforming the strongest RAG baseline (48.5%) and the off-the-shelf coding agent baseline (69.3%). Despite the strong performance gains, coding agent based methods have high latency costs. While AgentRunbook-C advances the accuracy-latency Pareto frontier, substantial room for improvement remains. Together, these results establish LME-V2 as a challenging testbed for developing long-term memory systems for environment experience.

AI.tldr: Introduces LongMemEval-V2, a benchmark to directly test long-term memory systems for web agents using curated questions and long interaction histories, and proposes two AgentRunbook memory methods that significantly improve accuracy over RAG baselines, with a latency–accuracy tradeoff.
AI.motivation: Existing memory benchmarks for agents mostly assess user history modeling, short interaction traces, or downstream task performance. They do not directly evaluate whether a memory system has actually internalized environment-specific experience, such as UI affordances, workflows, or typical failure modes in specialized web environments. There is therefore a need for a benchmark that targetedly measures long-term, environment-centric memory capabilities of web agents.
AI.method: The authors introduce LongMemEval-V2 (LME-V2), a benchmark with 451 manually curated questions designed to test five specific memory abilities in web agents: static state recall, dynamic state tracking, workflow knowledge, environment gotchas, and premise awareness. Each question is paired with long history trajectories (up to 500 trajectories, 115M tokens). They cast evaluation as a context-gathering problem: a memory system ingests these histories, retrieves or produces compact evidence, and passes this to a QA model. They propose two memory systems: (1) AgentRunbook-R, a retrieval-augmented generation memory with structured knowledge pools for raw states, events, and strategy notes; and (2) AgentRunbook-C, which stores trajectories as files and uses a coding agent operating in an augmented sandbox to dynamically search and gather evidence from these files.
AI.result: On LME-V2, AgentRunbook-C achieves 72.5% average accuracy, outperforming both the strongest RAG baseline at 48.5% and an off-the-shelf coding agent baseline at 69.3%. This shows that structured, code-driven evidence gathering over stored trajectories can substantially improve long-term memory performance. However, the coding-agent-based approach incurs significant latency costs, even though it improves the accuracy–latency Pareto frontier compared with prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: LME-V2 is validated as a challenging and informative benchmark for long-term, environment-experience memory in web agents. The proposed AgentRunbook-C method demonstrates that advanced coding-agent-based memory can markedly boost accuracy but at the expense of latency, indicating a nontrivial accuracy–efficiency tradeoff. The benchmark and baselines highlight substantial remaining headroom and provide a standardized testbed for future work on scalable, efficient long-term memory systems for specialized web environments.

title: LLMs and the ZPD
comment: Short paper submitted to Interspeech 2026 (Desk Reject) 4 pages, plus references. 2 figures
summary: One hundred years ago Vygotsky and his circle were exploring the nature of consciousness and defining what would become psychology in the Soviet Union. They concluded that children develop "scientific thinking" through interacting with enculturated adults in Zones of Proximal Development or ZPDs. The proposal is that, contrary to the claims of some, the LLM mechanism is not doing thinking with "distributed representations," but rather the completion model is doing "primitive thinking" in terms of *practices*. Viewed from this perspective, it would seem our large language models don't hallucinate, but rather dream, and that what is needed is not "guard rails" but an investigation of the set of cognitive tools that enable us to do things that look like common-sense. The proposal here is that *interaction* is core to human communication rather than just an add-on to "real" understanding.

AI.tldr: The paper reinterprets large language models (LLMs) through Vygotskian psychology, arguing that they perform a kind of primitive, practice-based thinking shaped through interaction rather than abstract, internal ‘scientific thinking’.
AI.motivation: To challenge dominant views that LLMs operate via distributed internal representations comparable to human conceptual thought, and to offer a more socially and developmentally grounded framework for understanding LLM behavior, especially so‑called hallucinations and the role of interaction.
AI.method: Conceptual and theoretical analysis drawing on Vygotsky’s notion of Zones of Proximal Development, scientific thinking, and enculturation, combined with a reinterpretation of the LLM completion mechanism as practice-based ‘primitive thinking’.
AI.result: The paper argues that LLM ‘hallucinations’ are better seen as dream-like completions within a practice space, that interaction is not an optional interface layer but constitutive of meaning and thinking, and that we should focus less on static guardrails and more on cognitive tools and interactive scaffolding for common-sense behavior.
AI.conclusion: LLMs should be understood as engaging in practice-based, interaction-dependent ‘primitive thinking’ rather than autonomous scientific reasoning with distributed representations; their apparent failures call for rethinking evaluation, safety, and design around interactive, socially situated cognitive tools instead of merely constraining outputs with guardrails.

title: OmniRefine: Alignment-Aware Cooperative Compression for Efficient Omnimodal Large Language Models
summary: Omnimodal large language models (Omni-LLMs) show strong capability in audio-video understanding, but their practical deployment remains limited by high inference cost of long video streams and dense audio sequences. Despite recent progress, existing compression methods for Omni-LLMs typically rely on fixed or native compression units, which can disrupt cross-modal correspondence and the complementary information required for audio-video reasoning, making it difficult to improve inference efficiency while stably preserving performance. To address this, we propose OmniRefine, a training-free two-stage framework for efficient audio-visual token compression in Omni-LLMs. First, Correspondence-Preserving Chunk Refinement refines native chunk boundaries into cross-modally aligned compression units through frame-audio similarity and dynamic programming. Second, Modality-Aware Cooperative Compression jointly compresses video and audio tokens within each refined unit to reduce redundancy while preserving critical evidence. Extensive experiments show that OmniRefine achieves a better efficiency-performance trade-off than strong baselines and maintains stable performance under lower compression ratios. On WorldSense, it still reaches 46.7% accuracy at a 44% token retention ratio, nearly matching the full-token baseline. The code and interface will be released to facilitate further research.

AI.tldr: OmniRefine is a training-free framework that compresses audio-visual tokens in Omni-LLMs while preserving cross-modal correspondence, achieving better efficiency-performance trade-offs on long video/audio understanding tasks.
AI.motivation: Omnimodal LLMs are powerful for audio-video understanding but are costly to run on long streams due to many video and audio tokens. Existing compression approaches use fixed or model-native units, which can break the alignment between modalities and harm reasoning that depends on synchronized audio-visual cues. There is a need for a compression method that is both efficient and preserves cross-modal correspondence without retraining the base model.
AI.method: OmniRefine introduces a two-stage, training-free compression pipeline. 1) Correspondence-Preserving Chunk Refinement: it refines native video/audio chunk boundaries into new units that are better aligned across modalities, using frame-audio similarity measures and dynamic programming to segment sequences. 2) Modality-Aware Cooperative Compression: within each refined unit, video and audio tokens are jointly compressed in a way that reduces redundancy but keeps evidence crucial for reasoning, explicitly considering interactions between modalities instead of compressing each independently.
AI.result: Experiments show that OmniRefine provides a superior trade-off between efficiency and performance compared to strong baselines for Omni-LLMs. It maintains more stable task accuracy under aggressive token compression, and on the WorldSense benchmark, it attains 46.7% accuracy even when only 44% of tokens are retained, which is close to the performance without compression.
AI.conclusion: OmniRefine demonstrates that it is possible to significantly compress audio-visual token sequences for Omni-LLMs without retraining while preserving cross-modal correspondence and downstream task performance. The approach offers a practical solution for deploying Omni-LLMs more efficiently on long audio-video streams and is intended to support further research by releasing code and interfaces.

title: SAGE: A Self-Evolving Agentic Graph-Memory Engine for Structure-Aware Associative Memory
summary: Long-term memory is becoming a central bottleneck for language agents. Exsting RAG and GraphRAG systems largely treat memory graphs as static retrieval middleware, which limits their ability to recover complete evidence chains from partial cues, exploit reusable graph-structrual roles, and improve the memory itself through downstream feedback. We introduce SAGE, a Self-evolving Agentic Graph-memory Engine that models graph memory as a dynamic long-term memory substrate. SAGE couples two roles: a memory writer that incrementally constucts structured graph memory from interaction histories, and a Graph Foundation Model-based memory reader to perform retrieval and provide feedback to the memory writer. We provide rigorooous theoretical annalyses supporting the framework. Across multi-hop QA, open-domain retireval, domain-specific review QA, and long-term agent-memory benchmarks, SAGE improves evidence recovery, answer grounding, and retrieval efficiency: after two self-evolution rounds, it achieves the best average rank on multi-hop QA; in zero-shot open-domain transfer, it reaches 82.5/91.6 Recall@2/5 on NQ. Further results on LongMemEval and HaluMem show that traning and reader-writer feedback improve multiple long-term memory and hallucination-diagnostic metrics, suggesting that self-evolving, structure-aware graph memory is a promising foundation for robust long-horizon language agents.

AI.tldr: SAGE is a self-evolving graph-based long-term memory system for language agents that jointly learns to write, retrieve, and refine graph memories, yielding better evidence recovery, grounding, and efficiency across long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing RAG and GraphRAG systems typically treat graph memory as a static retrieval layer. This makes it hard for agents to reconstruct full evidence chains from partial cues, exploit reusable structural patterns in the memory graph, or update and improve the memory based on downstream task feedback. There is a need for a dynamic, self-improving memory substrate that better supports long-term reasoning for language agents.
AI.method: The paper proposes SAGE (Self-evolving Agentic Graph-memory Engine), which conceptualizes graph memory as a dynamic long-term memory substrate. SAGE consists of two interacting components: (1) a memory writer that incrementally builds and updates structured graph memory from agent interaction histories, and (2) a Graph Foundation Model-based memory reader that performs retrieval over this graph and provides feedback signals to refine the writer. The system undergoes self-evolution rounds, where retrieval, usage, and feedback iteratively improve the graph structure and the writer/reader behavior. The authors also provide theoretical analysis of the framework.
AI.result: Empirically, SAGE is evaluated on multi-hop question answering, open-domain retrieval, domain-specific review QA, and long-term agent-memory benchmarks. After two rounds of self-evolution, SAGE achieves the best average rank on multi-hop QA tasks. In zero-shot open-domain transfer on Natural Questions, it attains 82.5/91.6 Recall@2/5. On LongMemEval and HaluMem, incorporating training and reader-writer feedback improves several long-term memory quality and hallucination-diagnostic metrics, showing gains in evidence recovery, answer grounding, and retrieval efficiency compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that treating graph memory as a self-evolving, structure-aware long-term substrate—via coupled writer and Graph Foundation Model-based reader with feedback—substantially enhances long-horizon capabilities of language agents. The results suggest that dynamic, agentic graph memory is a strong foundation for more robust, better-grounded, and less hallucination-prone language agents, and that iterative self-evolution is key to improving memory over time.

title: Intermediate Artifacts as First-Class Citizens: A Data Model for Durable Intermediate Artifacts in Agentic Systems
summary:
Many AI systems are organized around loops in which models reason, call tools, observe results, and continue until a task is complete. These systems often produce final artifacts such as memos, plans, recommendations, and analyses, while the intermediate work that shaped those outputs remains ephemeral. For multi-step, revisable AI work, final artifacts are often lossy projections over upstream state.
  We argue that such systems should preserve durable, inspectable intermediate artifacts: typed, structured, addressable, versioned, dependency-aware, authoritative, and consumable by downstream computation. These artifacts are not the model's private chain-of-thought. They are maintained work products such as evidence maps, claim structures, criteria, assumptions, plans, transformation rules, synthesis procedures, unresolved tensions, and partial products that later humans and agents can inspect, revise, supersede, and improve.
  The contribution is a systems-level data model. We distinguish intermediate artifacts from chat transcripts, memory, hidden chain-of-thought, narration, thinking, and final answers; formalize additive and superseding update semantics with explicit current-state resolution; describe how artifact lineage supports durable intermediate state across revisions; and argue that evaluation must target maintained-state quality, not only final-output quality. The claim is not that artifacts make models smarter. It is that durable intermediate artifacts make AI-generated work more inspectable, revisable, and maintainable over time.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a systems-level data model for preserving durable, structured intermediate artifacts in AI workflows, making AI-generated work more inspectable, revisable, and maintainable over time.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems focus on final outputs (memos, plans, recommendations) while the intermediate reasoning and work products are ephemeral, making multi-step, revisable AI work hard to inspect, audit, revise, and maintain. Final artifacts are lossy projections that discard rich upstream state, which is problematic for long-lived, collaborative, or safety-critical tasks.
AI.method: The authors introduce a systems-level data model for AI workflows that explicitly represents intermediate artifacts as typed, structured, addressable, versioned, dependency-aware, and authoritative objects. They conceptually separate these artifacts from chat transcripts, model memory, hidden chain-of-thought, narration, or final answers, and formalize update semantics (additive vs. superseding) with explicit resolution of the current state. They also define how artifact lineage can persist across revisions and be consumed by downstream computation.
AI.result: The paper clarifies a design space and terminology for representing durable intermediate state in AI systems, specifying properties and semantics that such artifacts should have. It shows how lineage and dependency tracking can maintain coherent state across revisions and enable downstream use, positioning these artifacts as first-class maintained work products rather than transient thoughts or logs.
AI.conclusion: Durable intermediate artifacts do not inherently improve a model’s raw intelligence, but they significantly improve the inspectability, revisability, and long-term maintainability of AI-generated work. Evaluations of AI systems should therefore consider the quality of the maintained intermediate state—not just final outputs—and system designs should treat these artifacts as authoritative, reusable work products that humans and agents can inspect and iteratively improve.

title: Autonomy and Agency in Agentic AI: Architectural Tactics for Regulated Contexts
summary:
Deploying agentic AI in regulated contexts requires principled reasoning about two design dimensions: agency (what the system can do) and autonomy (how much it acts without human involvement). Though often treated independently, they are coupled: at higher autonomy, human error correction is less available, so reliable operation requires constraining agency accordingly; compliance requirements reinforce this by mandating human involvement as action consequences grow. Yet no established approach addresses them jointly, leaving practitioners without a principled basis for reasoning about oversight, action consequences, and error correction.
  This work introduces a two-dimensional design space in which both dimensions are organised into five operational levels, making the coupling explicit and navigable. Autonomy ranges from human-commanded operation (L1) to fully autonomous monitoring (L5); agency ranges from reasoning over supplied context (L1) to committed writes to authoritative records (L5). Building on this space, we propose six architectural tactics--checkpoints, escalation, multi-agent delegation, tool provisioning, tool fencing, and write staging--for adjusting a deployment's position within it. The tactics are grounded in two worked examples from public-sector contexts, illustrating how they apply under realistic compliance constraints. We further examine five deployment parameters--model capability, agent architecture, tool fidelity, workflow bottlenecks, and evaluation--that shape what is achievable at any configuration independently of agency and autonomy. Together, the design space, tactics, and deployment parameters provide a shared vocabulary for principled, compliance-aware agentic AI design in which responsibility, auditability, and reversibility are explicit design considerations rather than properties that must be retrofitted after deployment.

AI.tldr: Defines a 2D design space for agentic AI in regulated domains, jointly structuring levels of autonomy and agency, and proposes tactics and parameters for compliant, auditable deployment.
AI.motivation: Practitioners currently lack a principled way to co-design what AI agents are allowed to do (agency) and how independently they operate (autonomy), especially under regulatory and compliance constraints where responsibility and oversight are critical. Existing work treats autonomy and agency separately, making it hard to reason transparently about error correction, oversight, and consequences of actions in real-world deployments.
AI.method: The paper introduces a two-dimensional framework where both autonomy and agency are discretized into five operational levels, from minimal to maximal. It then defines six architectural tactics (checkpoints, escalation, multi-agent delegation, tool provisioning, tool fencing, write staging) that can move a system’s position within this space, and analyzes five deployment parameters that limit what configurations are feasible. The framework is illustrated using two public-sector case studies subject to realistic compliance rules.
AI.result: The authors produce: (1) a level-based 2D design space that tightly couples autonomy and agency; (2) a set of six design tactics to tune these dimensions; and (3) an analysis of five deployment parameters affecting achievable configurations. These are instantiated in two public-sector examples, demonstrating how to design compliant agentic AI systems with explicit oversight and control mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: By jointly structuring autonomy and agency and providing tactics and parameters for moving within this design space, the paper offers a practical vocabulary and set of tools for designing agentic AI systems that meet compliance requirements. It helps practitioners make responsibility, auditability, and reversibility first-class design choices, rather than afterthoughts applied post-deployment.

title: Large Language Models as Amortized Pareto-Front Generators for Constrained Bi-Objective Convex Optimization
summary: Generating feasible Pareto fronts for constrained bi-objective continuous optimization is central to multi-criteria decision-making. Existing methods usually rely on iterative scalarization, evolutionary search, or problem-specific solvers, requiring repeated optimization for each instance. We introduce DIPS, an end-to-end framework that fine-tunes large language models as amortized Pareto-front generators for constrained bi-objective convex optimization. Given a textual problem description, DIPS directly outputs an ordered set of feasible continuous decision vectors approximating the Pareto front. To make continuous optimization compatible with autoregressive language modeling, DIPS combines a compact discretization scheme, Numerically Grounded Token Initialization for new numerical tokens, and Three-Phase Curriculum Optimization, which progressively aligns structural validity, feasibility, and Pareto-front quality. Across five families of constrained bi-objective convex problems, a fine-tuned 7B-parameter model achieves normalized hypervolume ratios of 95.29% to 98.18% relative to reference fronts. With vLLM-accelerated inference, DIPS solves one instance in as little as 0.16 seconds and outperforms general-purpose and reasoning LLM baselines under the evaluated setting. These results suggest that LLMs can serve as effective amortized generators for continuous Pareto-front approximation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes DIPS, an LLM-based framework that directly generates feasible approximations of Pareto fronts for constrained bi-objective convex optimization from text descriptions, achieving high-quality solutions quickly without per-instance optimization.
AI.motivation: Generating Pareto fronts for constrained bi-objective continuous optimization is crucial for multi-criteria decision-making, but current methods require iterative optimization or problem-specific solvers for each instance, making them computationally expensive and inflexible. The authors aim to develop an amortized, generalizable approach that can quickly output high-quality Pareto-front approximations from problem descriptions without repeated optimization.
AI.method: The paper introduces DIPS, an end-to-end framework that fine-tunes large language models to act as amortized Pareto-front generators. From a textual description of a constrained bi-objective convex problem, the model autoregressively outputs an ordered set of feasible continuous decision vectors approximating the Pareto front. To reconcile continuous optimization with discrete language modeling, DIPS uses: (1) a compact discretization scheme for representing continuous decision variables as tokens; (2) Numerically Grounded Token Initialization to effectively introduce and initialize new numerical tokens; and (3) a Three-Phase Curriculum Optimization strategy that sequentially trains the model to ensure structural validity of outputs, feasibility with respect to constraints, and finally high-quality Pareto-front approximations.
AI.result: On five families of constrained bi-objective convex problems, a fine-tuned 7B-parameter LLM using DIPS achieves normalized hypervolume ratios between 95.29% and 98.18% relative to high-quality reference Pareto fronts, indicating strong approximation performance. Leveraging vLLM-accelerated inference, DIPS can solve a problem instance in about 0.16 seconds, significantly faster than traditional optimization pipelines, and outperforms both general-purpose and reasoning-focused LLM baselines under the tested conditions.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that large language models, when appropriately fine-tuned and numerically grounded, can serve as effective amortized generators for continuous Pareto-front approximations in constrained bi-objective convex optimization. DIPS combines numerical representation techniques and curriculum training to produce fast, feasible, and high-quality Pareto fronts from textual problem descriptions, suggesting a promising direction for integrating LLMs into multi-criteria decision-making workflows and potentially extending to broader classes of continuous optimization problems.

title: Adaptive Multi-Round Allocation with Stochastic Arrivals
summary: We study a sequential resource allocation problem motivated by adaptive network recruitment, in which a limited budget of identical resources must be allocated over multiple rounds to individuals with stochastic referral capacity. Successful referrals endogenously generate future decision opportunities while allocating additional resources to an individual exhibits diminishing returns. We first show that the single-round allocation problem admits an exact greedy solution based on marginal survival probabilities. In the multi-round setting, the resulting Bellman recursion is intractable due to the stochastic, high-dimensional evolution of the frontier. To address this, we introduce a population-level surrogate value function that depends only on the remaining budget and frontier size. This surrogate enables an exact dynamic program via truncated probability generating functions, yielding a planning algorithm with polynomial complexity in the total budget. We further analyze robustness under model misspecification, proving a multi-round error bound that decomposes into a tight single-round frontier error and a population-level transition error. Finally, we evaluate our method on real-world inspired recruitment scenarios.

AI.tldr: They propose an efficient dynamic programming method for allocating a limited recruitment budget over time in a network, using a simplified population-level value function to overcome the curse of dimensionality, and provide robustness guarantees and empirical validations.
AI.motivation: In adaptive network recruitment (e.g., viral marketing, respondent-driven sampling), decisions must be made sequentially about whom to give limited recruitment resources (like coupons or incentives). Each recruited individual can stochastically refer others, creating future opportunities, but giving many resources to the same person has diminishing returns. Existing formulations quickly become intractable as the state space (who is on the recruitment frontier, with what remaining capacity) grows combinatorially, making it hard to compute optimal allocation policies and to understand how sensitive they are to model misspecification.
AI.method: 1) Formulate the sequential resource allocation problem where each individual has a stochastic referral capacity and multiple allocation rounds. 2) Show that in a single round, an optimal allocation can be obtained via a greedy algorithm based on marginal survival probabilities (a measure of the benefit of one more unit of resource). 3) For the multi-round problem, note that a direct dynamic programming approach is intractable due to the complex stochastic evolution of the recruitment frontier. 4) Introduce a population-level surrogate value function that summarizes the state only by remaining budget and frontier size, discarding detailed micro-state information. 5) Using this surrogate, construct an exact dynamic program by representing transitions with truncated probability generating functions, leading to a planning algorithm whose complexity is polynomial in the total budget. 6) Analyze robustness to model misspecification, deriving multi-round performance error bounds that can be decomposed into a sharp single-round frontier error term and a population-level transition error term. 7) Empirically evaluate the approach on recruitment scenarios inspired by real-world settings.
AI.result: They obtain: (i) an exact greedy solution to the single-round budget allocation problem using marginal survival probabilities; (ii) an exact multi-round dynamic programming algorithm with polynomial complexity in the total budget, made possible by a population-level surrogate and truncated PGFs; (iii) theoretical robustness guarantees in the form of multi-round error bounds that separate local (frontier-level) and global (population-level) sources of error; and (iv) empirical evidence from realistic recruitment-style experiments that the method is practical and effective.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that a carefully chosen population-level surrogate representation can convert an otherwise intractable sequential resource allocation problem in adaptive network recruitment into a tractable, exact dynamic program. The proposed greedy single-round allocation, polynomial-time multi-round planner, and robustness guarantees together provide a principled and computationally efficient framework for planning recruitment strategies under uncertainty, with performance validated in realistic scenarios.

title: To Whom Do Language Models Align? Measuring Principal Hierarchies Under High-Stakes Competing Demands
summary: Language models deployed in high-stakes professional settings face conflicting demands from users, institutional authorities, and professional norms. How models act when these demands conflict reveals a principal hierarchy -- an implicit ordering over competing stakeholders that determines, for instance, whether a medical AI receiving a cost-reduction directive from a hospital administrator complies at the expense of evidence-based care, or refuses because professional standards require it. Across 7,136 scenarios in legal and medical domains, we test ten frontier models and find that models frequently fail to adhere to professional standards during task execution, such as drafting, when user instructions conflict with those standards -- despite adequately upholding them when users seek advisory guidance. We further find that the hierarchies between user, authority, and professional standards exhibited by these models are unstable across medical and legal contexts and inconsistent across model families. When failing to follow professional standards, the primary failure mechanism is knowledge omission: models that demonstrably possess relevant knowledge produce harmful outputs without surfacing conflicting knowledge. In a particularly troubling instance, we find that a reasoning model recognizes the relevant knowledge in its reasoning trace -- e.g., that a drug has been withdrawn -- yet suppresses this in the user-facing answer and proceeds to recommend the drug under authority pressure anyway. Inconsistent alignment across task framing, domain, and model families suggests that current alignment methods, including published alignment hierarchies, are unlikely to be robust when models are deployed in high-stakes professional settings.

AI.tldr: Paper studies how frontier LMs prioritize users, institutional authorities, and professional norms in high-stakes legal/medical tasks and finds inconsistent, unsafe hierarchies.
AI.motivation: Deployed LMs in medicine and law must juggle conflicting demands: what the user asks, what institutions direct, and what professional standards require. Misprioritizing these can cause harmful, non-compliant behavior. Current alignment work talks about value hierarchies but we lack systematic evidence about how models actually behave under such conflicts.
AI.method: Construct 7,136 scenarios in medical and legal domains with conflicting instructions from users, institutional authorities, and professional standards, in both advisory and task-execution framings. Evaluate ten frontier models, comparing their responses to what professional standards require, and analyze where they defer (user vs authority vs standards) and how failures arise, including inspection of reasoning traces for reasoning models.
AI.result: (1) Models often fail to follow professional standards in task-execution settings (e.g., drafting documents) when these conflict with user instructions or authority directives, even though they follow standards better when asked for advice. (2) The implicit principal hierarchies (user vs authority vs professional norms) are unstable across domains and inconsistent between model families. (3) The dominant failure mode is knowledge omission: models that clearly possess relevant professional knowledge nonetheless produce harmful or non-compliant outputs without revealing the conflicting knowledge. (4) In some reasoning models, the internal trace correctly notes critical facts (e.g., a drug withdrawal) but the final answer suppresses them and complies with authority pressure anyway.
AI.conclusion: Existing alignment approaches, including published alignment hierarchies, do not reliably produce stable, safety-preserving prioritization of professional norms over users and authorities in high-stakes settings. Because models can both know and internally reason about professional constraints yet still suppress them in outputs, naive use of reasoning traces or assumptions about consistent deference hierarchies is unsafe; more robust alignment and evaluation methods are needed before deployment in professional domains.

title: Do Enterprise Systems Need Learned World Models? The Importance of Context to Infer Dynamics
summary: World models enable agents to anticipate the effects of their actions by internalizing environment dynamics. In enterprise systems, however, these dynamics are often defined by tenant-specific business logic that varies across deployments and evolves over time, making models trained on historical transitions brittle under deployment shift. We ask a question the world-models literature has not addressed: when the rules can be read at inference time, does an agent still need to learn them? We argue, and demonstrate empirically, that in settings where transition dynamics are configurable and readable, runtime discovery complements offline training by grounding predictions in the active system instance. We propose enterprise discovery agents, which recover relevant transition dynamics at runtime by reading the system's configuration rather than relying solely on internalized representations. We introduce CascadeBench, a reasoning-focused benchmark for enterprise cascade prediction that adopts the evaluation methodology of World of Workflows on diverse synthetic environments, and use it together with deployment-shift evaluation to show that offline-trained world models can perform well in-distribution but degrade as dynamics change, whereas discovery-based agents are more robust under shift by grounding their predictions in the current instance. Our findings suggest that, in configurable enterprise environments, agents should not rely solely on fixed internalized dynamics, but should incorporate mechanisms for discovering relevant transition logic at runtime.

AI.tldr: Paper argues agents in configurable enterprise systems should read environment rules at runtime instead of only learning dynamics offline.
AI.motivation: World models help agents predict action outcomes but break under deployment shift in enterprise systems where tenant-specific business logic frequently changes. Existing world-model research assumes fixed or slowly changing dynamics and does not consider cases where the governing rules are explicitly readable at inference time.
AI.method: Propose 'enterprise discovery agents' that, at inference, read and parse system configuration to recover the active transition dynamics instead of relying solely on internalized world-model representations. Introduce CascadeBench, a benchmark for reasoning-focused enterprise cascade prediction built from diverse synthetic environments following the World of Workflows methodology. Evaluate offline-trained world models versus discovery-based agents both in-distribution and under deployment shift, where environment dynamics change via configuration.
AI.result: Offline-trained world models perform competitively in-distribution but their performance degrades substantially when transition dynamics are modified. Discovery-based agents that ground predictions in the current configuration maintain better performance and robustness under deployment shift across CascadeBench and additional deployment-shift evaluations.
AI.conclusion: In configurable enterprise environments with readable transition rules, agents should not rely solely on fixed, learned dynamics. Instead, they should include runtime mechanisms to discover and ground predictions in the currently active transition logic, improving robustness to deployment shift.

title: Rollout Cards: A Reproducibility Standard for Agent Research
summary: Reproducibility problems that have long affected machine learning and reinforcement learning are now surfacing in agent research: papers compare systems by reported scores while leaving the rollout records behind those scores difficult to inspect. For agentic tasks, this matters because the same behaviour can receive different reported scores when evaluations select different parts of a rollout or apply different reporting rules. In a structured audit of 50 popular training and evaluation repositories, we find that none report how many runs failed, errored, or were skipped alongside headline scores. We also document 37 cases where reporting rules can change task-success rates, cost/token accounting, or timing measurements for fixed evidence, sometimes dramatically. We treat rollout records, not reported scores, as the unit of reproducibility for agent research. We introduce rollout cards: publication bundles that preserve the rollout record and declare the views, reporting rules, and drops manifests behind reported scores. We validate rollout cards in two settings. First, four partial public releases in tool safety, multi-agent systems, theorem proving, and search let us compute analyses their original reports did not include. Second, re-grading preserved benchmark outputs across short-answer, code-generation, and tool-use tasks shows that changing only the reporting rule can change reported scores by 20.9 absolute percentage points and, in some cases, invert rankings of frontier models. We release a reference implementation integrated into Ergon, an open-source reinforcement learning gym, and publicly publish Ergon-produced rollout-card exports for benchmarks spanning tool use, software engineering, web interaction, multi-agent coordination, safety, and search to support future research.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that in agent research, rollout records—not just reported scores—must be preserved and standardized because different reporting rules on the same rollouts can drastically change benchmark results and even model rankings.
AI.motivation: Reproducibility issues known in ML and RL are reappearing in agentic benchmarks: current papers typically report only aggregate scores without exposing full rollout traces or the exact reporting rules used. For agents, this is particularly problematic because partial views of a trajectory, different success criteria, and selective dropping of runs can lead to inconsistent or misleading comparisons, even when the underlying behaviour is identical. The authors found that none of 50 popular repositories report how many runs failed/errored/skipped, and they observed many ways in which reporting rules change success rates, costs, or timings, motivating a more principled unit of reproducibility.
AI.method: 1) Conduct a structured audit of 50 widely-used training/evaluation repositories for agentic tasks, checking how they handle failures, errors, and skipped runs, and how they define/report metrics. 2) Systematically catalogue 37 concrete cases where different reporting rules or views over the same rollout data change key outcome metrics. 3) Propose “rollout cards”: a standardized publication bundle that includes full rollout records plus explicit descriptions of views, reporting rules, and drop policies used to derive scores. 4) Empirically validate rollout cards via (a) re-analysis of four partial public benchmark releases across diverse domains, and (b) re-grading preserved outputs for multiple benchmarks (short-answer, code-generation, tool-use) under alternative reporting rules. 5) Implement and integrate a reference rollout-card generator into Ergon, an open-source RL/agent environment suite, and release example exports for many benchmarks.
AI.result: The audit shows that none of the 50 examined repositories report basic reliability statistics like counts of failed, errored, or skipped runs alongside headline results. The authors identify 37 real-world examples where changing reporting rules (e.g., which steps count, how success is defined, which runs are dropped) significantly alters task success rates, token/cost accounting, or timing metrics, even with fixed raw rollout evidence. In re-grading experiments, varying only the reporting rule changes reported benchmark scores by up to 20.9 absolute percentage points and can even flip the ranking order of leading models. Rollout cards are demonstrated to support new analyses on existing datasets and are implemented in Ergon, with multiple benchmarks released as case studies.
AI.conclusion: Agent evaluation should treat rollout records as the fundamental reproducible artifact rather than just aggregate scores. Without explicit, machine-readable descriptions of views, reporting rules, and drop policies, benchmark results for agents are fragile and can be unintentionally or strategically manipulated. Rollout cards provide a practical mechanism to preserve and communicate this information, enabling robust re-analysis, fairer model comparisons, and improved reproducibility across a wide variety of agentic tasks. The authors’ reference implementation and released benchmarks aim to establish rollout cards as a standard for future agent research.

title: BoolXLLM: LLM-Assisted Explainability for Boolean Models
summary:
Interpretable machine learning aims to provide transparent models whose decision-making processes can be readily understood by humans. Recent advances in rule-based approaches, such as expressive Boolean formulas (BoolXAI), offer faithful and compact representations of model behavior. However, for non-technical stakeholders, main challenges remain in practice: (i) selecting semantically meaningful features and (ii) translating formal logical rules into accessible explanations.
  In this work, we propose BoolXLLM , as a hybrid framework that integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) into the end-to-end pipeline of Boolean rule learning. We augment BoolXAI , an expressive Boolean rule-based classifier, with LLMs at three critical stages: (1) feature selection, where LLMs guide the identification of domain-relevant variables; (2) threshold recommendation, where LLMs propose semantically meaningful discretization strategies for numerical features; and (3) rule compression and interpretation, where Boolean rules are translated into natural language explanations at both global and local levels.
  This integration bridges formal, faithful explanations with human-understandable narratives. This allows build an explainable AI system that is both theoretically grounded and accessible to non-experts. Early empirical results demonstrate that LLM-assisted pipelines improve interpretability while maintaining competitive predictive performance. Our work highlights the promise of combining symbolic reasoning with language-based models for human-centered explainability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BoolXLLM, a hybrid framework that uses large language models to enhance Boolean rule-based classifiers, improving interpretability for non-expert users while keeping competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Although Boolean rule-based models like BoolXAI offer faithful and compact representations of black-box models, they are still hard to use for non-technical stakeholders. Two key issues are picking features that make sense in the domain and turning complex logical formulas into explanations that are easy to understand. The paper aims to close this gap so that interpretable models are genuinely usable by non-experts.
AI.method: The authors integrate LLMs into three stages of the BoolXAI pipeline. (1) During feature selection, LLMs are prompted with domain information to suggest semantically relevant variables. (2) For numerical features, LLMs recommend meaningful discretization thresholds, helping convert them into Boolean predicates aligned with how humans think about ranges. (3) After training the Boolean rule model, LLMs are used for rule compression (summarizing or restructuring rules to make them shorter and clearer) and for translating both global model rules and instance-level rules into natural language explanations.
AI.result: Preliminary experiments show that the LLM-augmented pipeline produces explanations that users find more interpretable, without sacrificing predictive performance compared with the base BoolXAI model or standard baselines. The rules remain faithful while being more compact or easier to read, and quantitative metrics of interpretability are improved in early studies.
AI.conclusion: Combining symbolic Boolean rule learning with LLM-based guidance yields an explainable AI system that is theoretically sound yet more accessible for non-experts. The framework demonstrates a promising direction for human-centered explainability, where LLMs help align formal rule representations with human semantic understanding at multiple stages of the modeling pipeline.

title: MM-OptBench: A Solver-Grounded Benchmark for Multimodal Optimization Modeling
summary: Optimization modeling translates real decision-making problems into mathematical optimization models and solver-executable implementations. Although language models are increasingly used to generate optimization formulations and solver code, existing benchmarks are almost entirely text-only. This omits many optimization-modeling tasks that arise in operational practice, where requirements are described in text but instance information is conveyed through visual artifacts such as tables, graphs, maps, schedules, and dashboards. We introduce multimodal optimization modeling, a benchmark setting in which models must construct both a mathematical formulation and executable solver code from a text-and-visual problem specification. To evaluate this setting, we develop a solver-grounded framework that generates structured optimization instances, verifies each with an exact solver, and builds both the model-facing inputs and hidden reference files from the same verified source. We instantiate the framework as MM-OptBench, a benchmark of 780 solver-verified instances spanning 6 optimization families, 26 subcategories, and 3 structural difficulty levels. We evaluate 9 multimodal large language models (MLLMs), including 6 frontier general-purpose models and 3 math-specialized models, with aggregate, family-level, difficulty-level, and failure-mode analyses. The results show that the task remains far from solved: the best two models reach 52.1% and 51.3% pass@1, while on average across the six general-purpose MLLMs, pass@1 is 43.4% on easy instances and 15.9% on hard instances. All three math-specialized MLLMs solve 0/780 instances. Failure attribution shows that errors arise both when extracting instance data from text and visuals and when turning extracted data into solver-correct formulations and code. MM-OptBench provides a testbed for solver-grounded, decision-oriented multimodal intelligence.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MM-OptBench, a solver-verified multimodal benchmark where models must generate optimization formulations and executable solver code from combined text and visual problem specifications, revealing that current multimodal LLMs perform poorly on these realistic tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for using language models in optimization modeling are almost entirely text-only, but many real-world optimization problems provide requirements in text and instance data through visuals like tables, graphs, and maps. This gap means current evaluations miss important aspects of practical decision-making scenarios. The authors aim to create a benchmark that better reflects operational practice and rigorously evaluates models’ abilities to handle multimodal inputs and produce solver-correct outputs.
AI.method: The authors define the task of multimodal optimization modeling, requiring models to construct both a mathematical formulation and executable solver code from combined text and visual specifications. They design a solver-grounded framework that automatically generates structured optimization instances, verifies them using an exact solver, and derives both model inputs (text + visuals) and hidden reference outputs from the same verified source. Using this framework, they build MM-OptBench, containing 780 verified instances across 6 optimization families, 26 subcategories, and 3 difficulty levels. They then evaluate 9 multimodal LLMs (6 general-purpose, 3 math-specialized) and analyze performance at multiple aggregation levels and across failure modes.
AI.result: On MM-OptBench, the best two models achieve only 52.1% and 51.3% pass@1. Across the six general-purpose multimodal LLMs, average pass@1 is 43.4% on easy instances but drops to 15.9% on hard instances. All three math-specialized multimodal LLMs fail to solve any of the 780 instances. Failure analysis reveals that models make errors both in extracting instance data from the multimodal inputs (text and visuals) and in transforming the extracted data into correct formulations and solver code.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that multimodal optimization modeling is a challenging, unsolved problem for current multimodal LLMs. MM-OptBench offers a rigorous, solver-grounded benchmark that better reflects real-world, decision-oriented multimodal tasks and can serve as a testbed for improving multimodal reasoning, data extraction, and optimization modeling capabilities in future models.

title: MolDeTox: Evaluating Language Model's Stepwise Fragment Editing for Molecular Detoxification
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Language Models (VLMs) have recently shown promising capabilities in various scientific domain. In particular, these advances have opened new opportunities in drug discovery, where the ability to understand and modify molecular structures is critical for optimizing drug properties such as efficacy and toxicity. However, existing models and benchmarks often overlook toxicity-related challenges, focusing primarily on general property optimization without adequately addressing safety concerns. In addition, even existing toxicity repair benchmarks suffer from limited data diversity, low structural validity of generated molecules, and heavy reliance on proxy models for toxicity assessment. To address these limitations, we propose MolDeTox, a novel benchmark for molecular detoxification, designed to enable fine-grained and reliable evaluation of toxicity-aware molecular optimization across stepwise tasks. We evaluate a wide range of general-purpose LLMs and VLMs under diverse settings, and demonstrate that understanding and generating molecules at the fragment-level improves structural validity and enhances the quality of generated molecules. Moreover, through detailed task-level performance analysis, MolDeTox provides an interpretable benchmark that enables a deeper understanding of the detoxification process. Our dataset is available at : https://huggingface.co/datasets/MolDeTox/MolDeTox

AI.tldr: MolDeTox introduces a new benchmark and dataset to rigorously evaluate how well LLMs and VLMs can detoxify molecules while preserving desirable drug properties.
AI.motivation: Current LLM/VLM-driven drug discovery work and benchmarks largely ignore toxicity-focused optimization. Existing detoxification benchmarks have shortcomings: narrow and non-diverse data, many invalid generated molecules, and dependence on proxy toxicity predictors rather than more reliable evaluation. There is a need for a systematic, fine-grained way to assess toxicity-aware molecular optimization and better understand how models perform detoxification.
AI.method: The authors construct MolDeTox, a benchmark for molecular detoxification with stepwise tasks that reflect different stages or granularity of detoxification (including fragment-level reasoning). They then systematically evaluate a broad spectrum of general-purpose LLMs and VLMs under multiple prompting and task settings, and analyze how representing and editing molecules at the fragment level affects detoxification performance and structural validity.
AI.result: Across the evaluated models, fragment-level molecular understanding and generation lead to higher structural validity and overall better quality in the detoxified molecules, compared with more holistic, non-fragment-based approaches. The benchmark allows the authors to observe nuanced, task-level differences in performance between models and settings.
AI.conclusion: MolDeTox offers an interpretable, stepwise benchmark that fills a gap in toxicity-aware molecular optimization evaluation. It shows that fragment-level reasoning is beneficial for detoxification and provides a structured way to study and improve how LLMs and VLMs perform toxicity reduction in drug-like molecules. The dataset is publicly released to support further research.

title: Goal-Oriented Reasoning for RAG-based Memory in Conversational Agentic LLM Systems
summary: LLM-based conversational AI agents struggle to maintain coherent behavior over long horizons due to limited context. While RAG-based approaches are increasingly adopted to overcome this limitation by storing interactions in external memory modules and performing retrieval from them, their effectiveness in answering challenging questions (e.g., multi-hop, commonsense) ultimately depends on the agent's ability to reason over the retrieved information. However, existing methods typically retrieve memory based on semantic similarity to the raw user utterance, which lacks explicit reasoning about missing intermediate facts and often returns evidence that is irrelevant or insufficient for grounded reasoning. In this work, we introduce Goal-Mem, a goal-oriented reasoning framework for RAG-based agentic memory that performs explicit backward chaining from the user's utterance as a goal. Rather than progressively expanding from retrieved context, Goal-Mem decomposes each goal into atomic subgoals, performs targeted memory retrieval to satisfy each subgoal, and iteratively identifies what information from memory should be retrieved when intermediate goals cannot be resolved. We formalize this process in Natural Language Logic, a logical system that combines the verifiability of reasoning provided by FOL with the expressivity of natural language. Through extensive experiments on two datasets and comparing to nine strong memory baselines, we show that Goal-Mem consistently improves performance, particularly on tasks requiring multi-hop reasoning and implicit inference.

AI.tldr: Goal-Mem is a goal-oriented reasoning framework for retrieval-augmented LLM agents that uses backward chaining and natural-language logic to retrieve and reason over memories more effectively for complex queries.
AI.motivation: LLM-based conversational agents with RAG struggle on long-horizon, complex queries (multi-hop, commonsense) because standard memory retrieval relies mainly on semantic similarity to the raw user utterance. This often yields irrelevant or insufficient evidence and lacks explicit reasoning about missing intermediate facts. The authors aim to design a memory and retrieval framework that reasons about what information is needed and retrieves it purposefully, thereby improving grounded reasoning over agentic memory.
AI.method: They propose Goal-Mem, a goal-oriented reasoning framework for RAG-based agents. Given a user utterance as a goal, Goal-Mem performs explicit backward chaining: it decomposes the main goal into atomic subgoals, then conducts targeted retrieval from external memory to satisfy each subgoal. When intermediate subgoals cannot be resolved, it iteratively decides what additional information should be retrieved. The reasoning and retrieval process is formalized in Natural Language Logic, a logical system that combines first-order logic-style verifiability with the expressivity of natural language representations.
AI.result: Through experiments on two datasets and comparison against nine strong RAG/memory baselines, Goal-Mem shows consistent performance gains, especially on queries requiring multi-hop reasoning and implicit inference. The empirical results indicate better use of stored interaction history and more accurate answers on challenging reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Goal-Mem demonstrates that explicitly goal-oriented, backward-chaining reasoning over memory—implemented via Natural Language Logic and subgoal-driven retrieval—can significantly improve RAG-based conversational agents, particularly for complex, multi-hop and implicit reasoning scenarios where naive similarity-based retrieval is inadequate.

title: No Action Without a NOD: A Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Architecture for Reliable Service Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents have increasingly advanced service applications, such as booking flight tickets. However, these service agents suffer from unreliability in long-horizon tasks, as they often produce policy violations, tool hallucinations, and misaligned actions, which greatly impedes their real-world deployment. To address these challenges, we propose NOD (Navigator-Operator-Director), a heterogeneous multi-agent architecture for service agents. Instead of maintaining task state implicitly in dialogue context as in prior work, we externalize a structured Global State to enable explicit task state tracking and consistent decision-making by the Navigator. Besides, we introduce selective external oversight before critical actions, allowing an independent Director agent to verify execution and intervene when necessary. As such, NOD effectively mitigates error propagation and unsafe behavior in long-horizon tasks. Experiments on $τ^2$-Bench demonstrate that NOD achieves higher task success rates and critical action precision over baselines. More importantly, NOD improves the reliability of service agents by reducing policy violations, tool hallucinations, and user-intent misalignment.

AI.tldr: They propose NOD, a three-agent architecture with explicit global state and external oversight to make LLM service agents more reliable on long, tool-using tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based service agents (e.g., for booking flights) are unreliable on long-horizon, tool-using tasks. They often break policies, hallucinate tools, and take actions misaligned with user intent because they implicitly track task state only through dialogue and lack robust checks before executing critical actions. This unreliability blocks real-world deployment.
AI.method: They design NOD, a heterogeneous multi-agent system with three roles: (1) Navigator, which plans and makes decisions using an explicit, structured Global State that represents the task status; (2) Operator, which executes tool calls and environment interactions; and (3) Director, an independent oversight agent that selectively reviews and can veto or correct critical actions. Global State externalizes and persists task information across steps, while selective oversight is triggered before high-risk operations to detect and prevent errors and unsafe behavior. They evaluate NOD on the τ^2-Bench benchmark for long-horizon service tasks.
AI.result: On τ^2-Bench, NOD outperforms baseline LLM agents in task success rate and the precision of critical actions. It also significantly reduces policy violations, tool hallucinations, and misalignments with user intent, indicating more robust and trustworthy behavior on complex service tasks.
AI.conclusion: Explicit global state tracking plus a director-style oversight agent yields more reliable LLM service agents for long-horizon tasks than standard single-agent, dialogue-only approaches. NOD mitigates error propagation and unsafe actions, improving success and safety metrics, and represents a promising architecture for deploying LLMs in real-world service applications.

title: Why Conclusions Diverge from the Same Observations: Formalizing World-Model Non-Identifiability via an Inference
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures, 1 table. Extended English version of a paper accepted for presentation at JSAI 2026
summary: When people share the same documents and observations yet reach different conclusions, the disagreement often shifts into a judgment that the other party is cognitively defective, irrational, or acting in bad faith. This paper argues that such divergence is better described as a form of non-identifiability inherent in inference and learning, rather than as a defect of the other party. We organize the phenomenon into two levels: (i) $θ$-level non-identifiability, where conclusions diverge under the same world model $W$ because inference settings differ; and (ii) $W$-level non-identifiability, where repeated use of an inference setting $θ$ biases data exposure and update rules, causing the learned world model $W$ itself to diverge. We introduce an inference profile $θ= (R, E, S, D)$, consisting of Reference, Exploration, Stabilization, and Horizon, and show how outputs can split even for the same observation $o$ and the same $W$. We further explain why disagreements tend to project onto a small number of bases -- abstract versus concrete, externalizability, and order versus freedom -- as a consequence of general constraints on learning systems: computational, observational, and coordination constraints. Finally, we relate the framework to deep representation learning, including representation hierarchy, latent-state estimation, and regularization-exploration trade-offs, and illustrate the framework through a case study on AI regulation debates.

AI.tldr: The paper reframes disagreements between agents who share the same evidence as arising from inherent non-identifiability in inference and learning, rather than irrationality or bad faith.
AI.motivation: People frequently draw different conclusions from the same documents, observations, or data, and then attribute the disagreement to defects in the other party (irrationality, bias, bad faith). The paper seeks a principled, technical explanation for how and why such divergences arise even when evidence is shared, aiming to replace moralized or dismissive accounts of disagreement with a structured framework grounded in inference theory and learning dynamics.
AI.method: The authors propose a formal framework that distinguishes two levels of non-identifiability: θ-level non-identifiability, where agents share a world model W but differ in inference settings θ, and W-level non-identifiability, where different θ settings shape data exposure and update rules, leading to divergence in the learned world models W themselves. They define an "inference profile" θ = (R, E, S, D)—Reference, Exploration, Stabilization, Horizon—to parameterize how agents process and update beliefs from observations. They then analyze how these dimensions induce divergent conclusions from the same observation o and same W, and show how general constraints on learning systems (computational, observational, coordination) lead disagreements to cluster along a few interpretable dimensions (abstract vs concrete, externalizability, order vs freedom). The framework is connected to concepts from deep representation learning, such as representation hierarchies, latent-state estimation, and regularization–exploration trade-offs, and is applied qualitatively in a case study on debates over AI regulation.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that: (1) Even when agents share a world model W and observations o, differences in inference profiles θ = (R, E, S, D) can cause their conclusions to systematically diverge (θ-level non-identifiability). (2) Over time, these different θ settings also generate distinct data streams and update trajectories, leading to structurally different world models W (W-level non-identifiability).
